topics.title: Familiarisation with MoveIt through its RViz plugin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL607 - ROS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarisation with MoveIt through its RViz plugin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Familiarisation with MoveIt through its RViz Plugin

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MoveIt** is the most widely used open-source **Motion Planning Framework** for robotic manipulation built on top of the Robot Operating System (ROS). It provides a comprehensive suite of tools for **kinematics, motion planning, collision checking, 3D perception, and trajectory execution** for any serial-chain robotic manipulator.

The **MoveIt RViz Plugin** is an interactive **Graphical User Interface (GUI)** embedded inside **RViz** (the 3D ROS visualiser). It allows a developer or researcher to:
- **Load a Unified Robot Description Format (URDF)** model of a robot.
- **Set planning groups**, end-effectors, and poses.
- **Compute Forward and Inverse Kinematics** interactively.
- **Plan and Visualize collision-free trajectories** in real-time **without writing a single line of C++ or Python code**.

> [!NOTE]
> The plugin is a wrapper around the `move_group` C++/Python node. Instead of writing launch files, the user clicks buttons in RViz to invoke the underlying **Open Motion Planning Library (OMPL)** planners such as **RRT, RRT-Connect, PRM, EST, KPIECE, and LBKPIECE**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine that you have bought a new industrial **robotic arm** (similar to a Universal Robots UR5 or a Franka Panda). Before you ever power it on and risk damaging the robot or its surroundings, you want a **virtual playground** to:
1. See what the arm looks like in **3D**,
2. Teach it to move from "Home Pose" to "Grasp Pose",
3. Verify that it does not crash into the table, the conveyor belt, or itself.

**MoveIt + RViz is exactly this virtual playground.**

- **MoveIt** = the *brain* (mathematics, path planning algorithms, kinematics solver).
- **RViz Plugin** = the *cockpit / dashboard* (sliders, buttons, draggable arrows you click with your mouse to test poses).

**Real-world analogy:**
> A **flight simulator for pilots**. Pilots do not learn to fly a Boeing 747 by trial-and-error in the real aircraft. They first rehearse in a simulator that visualises the plane in 3D. MoveIt is the **simulator**, RViz is the **windscreen and instrument panel**.

### 1.3 Standard KTU High-Yield Terminology (Bolded for Recall)

| Term | Meaning (one-line KTU Board definition) |
|---|---|
| **URDF** | Unified Robot Description Format – XML file describing the robot's links, joints, and visual/collision geometry. |
| **SRDF** | Semantic Robot Description Format – extends URDF with semantic info (planning groups, end-effectors, virtual joints, poses). |
| **move_group** | The central ROS node of MoveIt that integrates all sub-components. |
| **Planning Scene** | A 3D world representation maintained by move_group containing the robot and surrounding obstacles. |
| **OMPL** | Open Motion Planning Library – the default sampling-based planner used by MoveIt. |
| **TF** | Transform – ROS's coordinate-frame system that publishes the 3D pose relationships between links. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The MoveIt Software Architecture

MoveIt is structured as a collection of **pluggable components** coordinated by the `move_group` node. For your KTU Board exam, memorise this layered pipeline:

1. **User Interface Layer** → RViz Plugin **or** C++/Python API.
2. **move_group Node** → The orchestrator (written in C++).
3. **Sub-Component Layer**:
   - **Kinematics Plugin** (solves FK & IK).
   - **Planning Plugin** (default: OMPL).
   - **Collision Checking Plugin** (FCL – Flexible Collision Library).
   - **Trajectory Execution** (FollowJointTrajectory Action Server).
4. **Robot Description & Semantic Description** (URDF + SRDF).
5. **Hardware / Gazebo / Real Robot** (the actual driver).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use MoveIt?** Writing a motion planner from scratch requires implementing **RRT/PRM, IK solvers, collision checkers, and trajectory smoothing**. MoveIt packages all of this in a single, well-tested, industrial-grade framework. It is industry-standard for **ROS 1 Noetic** and the go-to for **ROS 2 Humble/Jazzy** as well.

## 2.2 The MoveIt Setup Assistant (MSSA)

Before using the RViz plugin, you must **generate a MoveIt configuration package** for your robot. This is done using the **MoveIt Setup Assistant**, a wizard with **seven tabs**:

| Tab | Purpose | KTU Board Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Start** | Load the URDF and provide a path to generate the config package. | Always the first step. |
| **Self-Collisions** | Auto-generate a sampling matrix of link pairs that *can never* collide. | Speeds up collision checking. |
| **Virtual Joints** | Defines the robot's root link to a fixed/moving world frame. | Important for mobile manipulators. |
| **Planning Groups** | Group joints together (e.g., "arm" and "gripper"). | **Most critical tab** – defines the kinematic chain. |
| **Robot Poses** | Pre-defined named poses (e.g., "home", "zero"). | Used for quick recovery. |
| **End Effectors** | Designates the tip link of a planning group as a gripper. | Required for grasping. |
| **Passives** | Joints that MoveIt should *not command* (e.g., casters). | Used for passive wheels. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Parameter Sheet

MoveIt itself is *not* a formula-heavy topic (it is a software framework), but the underlying kinematics it invokes **is**. The RViz plugin internally calls these equations every time you drag the interactive marker.

$$
T_{0}^{n}(\mathbf{q}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_{i-1}^{i}(q_i)
$$

where $A_{i-1}^{i}(q_i)$ is the standard **Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) transformation matrix** of joint $i$.

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta\cos\alpha & \sin\theta\sin\alpha & a\cos\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta\cos\alpha & -\cos\theta\sin\alpha & a\sin\theta \\ 0 & \sin\alpha & \cos\alpha & d \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | KTU-Standard Value / Type |
|---|---|---|
| $\theta$ | Joint angle (revolute) | **radians** |
| $d$ | Link offset (prismatic) | **metres** |
| $a$ | Link length | **metres** |
| $\alpha$ | Link twist | **radians** |
| $\mathbf{q}$ | Vector of joint values | $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n]^T$ |
| $T_{0}^{n}$ | End-effector pose in base frame | $4\times4$ homogeneous matrix |
| $J(\mathbf{q})$ | Jacobian matrix relating joint velocities to end-effector velocity | $6\times n$ matrix |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **velocity-level kinematic relation** (used in trajectory execution) is:
> $$ \dot{x} = J(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{q}} $$
> where $\dot{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$ is the **spatial velocity** (linear + angular) of the end-effector.

## 2.4 The RViz Motion Planning Plugin – Panel-by-Panel

When the plugin is loaded, you will see a side panel titled **"MotionPlanning"** in RViz. The KTU Board frequently asks about the contents of this panel:

| Sub-Tab | Function |
|---|---|
| **Planning Library** | Dropdown to select the OMPL algorithm (RRT, RRT-Connect, PRM, EST, KPIECE, LBKPIECE, FMT, BIT*, etc.). |
| **Planning Request** | Set Start State, Goal State, tolerance, and pipeline (default: OMPL). |
| **Plan** | Click the **"Plan"** button to compute a trajectory. |
| **Execute** | Click the **"Execute"** button to send the trajectory to the real/simulated robot. |
| **Scene Objects** | Add/remove collision objects to the Planning Scene. |
| **Stored Scenes** | Save/load the entire scene state. |
| **Status** | Shows the current state, planning time, and success/failure. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The "Query Goal State" interactive marker (the coloured **red/green/blue arrows** attached to the end-effector) is the IK visualizer. Dragging it invokes the IK solver to find a valid joint configuration in real-time.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Welding Arms** (e.g., Yaskawa Motoman, ABB IRB) – pre-computed MoveIt paths avoid singularities and joint limits.
- **Surgical Robots** (e.g., da Vinci Research Kit) – MoveIt plans collision-free motions inside a crowded patient cavity.
- **Warehouse Pick-and-Place** (Amazon Robotics, Agility Robotics Digit) – MoveIt integrates with **Perception pipelines (Point Cloud → Octomap)** to plan in unstructured environments.
- **Humanoid Robots** (e.g., Toyota HSR, TIAGo) – MoveIt enables bimanual and mobile manipulation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Procedure: Familiarisation with MoveIt via its RViz Plugin

## 3.1 Prerequisites & Installation (Ubuntu 22.04 + ROS 2 Humble)

Step 1: Source your ROS 2 environment. Open a terminal:

```bash
source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash
```

Step 2: Install MoveIt 2 (the official ROS 2 port of MoveIt):

```bash
sudo apt install ros-humble-moveit
```

Step 3: Install the MoveIt tutorials and demo packages:

```bash
sudo apt install ros-humble-moveit-resources
```

## 3.2 Generating the MoveIt Configuration Package

If you already have a robot's URDF, use the Setup Assistant. For a ready-made demo, MoveIt ships with the **Panda robot** configuration.

Step 4: Launch the MoveIt Setup Assistant:

```bash
ros2 launch moveit_setup_assistant setup_assistant.launch.py
```

Step 5: In the GUI:
- Click **"Create New MoveIt Configuration Package"**.
- Click **"Browse"** and select `panda.urdf.xacro` (path: `/opt/ros/humble/share/moveit_resources_panda_description/urdf/`).
- Click **"Load Files"**. The 3D Panda robot will appear.

Step 6: Go through the seven tabs:
- **Self-Collisions** → Click **"Generate Collision Matrix"** (default sampling density: **10000**).
- **Virtual Joints** → Add a virtual joint named `virtual_joint` of type `fixed` linking the world to `panda_link0`.
- **Planning Groups** → Add group `panda_arm` using the **KDL Kinematics Plugin** (default IK solver).
- **Robot Poses** → Add a `ready` pose.
- **End Effectors** → Add `hand` as the end-effector with parent link `panda_link8`.
- **Configuration Files** → Choose path: `~/ws_moveit/src/`.
- Click **"Generate Package"**.

## 3.3 Launching the RViz Motion Planning Plugin

Step 7: Build the workspace and source it:

```bash
cd ~/ws_moveit
colcon build --packages-select panda_moveit_config
source install/setup.bash
```

Step 8: Launch the demo (this starts `move_group` + RViz + the MotionPlanning plugin):

```bash
ros2 launch panda_moveit_config demo.launch.py rviz_tutorial:=true
```

Step 9: Verify the components started correctly using ROS 2 introspection:

```bash
ros2 node list
ros2 topic list
ros2 action list
```

The expected output will include:
- `/move_group`
- `/execute_trajectory` (Action)
- `/plan_kinematics_path` (Action)
- `/monitored_planning_scene` (Topic)

## 3.4 Interactive Plan-and-Execute via the GUI

Step 10: In the RViz **MotionPlanning** panel:
- Set **Planning Library** to `OMPL`.
- Choose algorithm: `RRTConnectkConfigDefault` (fastest for most cases).
- Set **Start State** → `ready`.
- Set **Goal State** → drag the **interactive marker** (RGB arrows) to a new position.
- Click **"Plan"** → the path is visualised as a coloured line.
- Click **"Execute"** → the simulated robot in RViz moves along the path.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does RViz show the robot moving but the real world does not?** Because you are running a *demo* launch. The "Execute" button sends the trajectory to a **fake controller** that animates the RViz model. To control a real arm, you must replace the `fake_controllers` with a real `ros2_control` hardware interface (which is the topic of the next module experiment).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix – Command vs. Effect

| Command / Click | What it does internally | KTU Board Definition |
|---|---|---|
| `ros2 launch moveit_setup_assistant ...` | Opens the wizard to generate the SRDF + config package. | MoveIt Setup Assistant. |
| `ros2 launch ... demo.launch.py` | Starts `move_group`, RViz, and a fake controller. | Demo launch. |
| Click **"Plan"** in RViz | Sends a `MotionPlanRequest` action goal to `/plan_kinematics_path`. | Invokes the OMPL planner. |
| Click **"Execute"** in RViz | Sends the trajectory to `/execute_trajectory` action. | Trajectory execution. |
| Drag the Interactive Marker | Continuously solves **Inverse Kinematics** for the new goal pose. | IK real-time visualisation. |
| Add a **Collision Object** in Scene Objects | Publishes a `CollisionObject` message to the planning scene topic. | Updates the 3D world model. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of MoveIt (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UserInterface["User Interface Layer: RViz Plugin and C plus plus Python API"]
    MoveGroup["move_group ROS 2 Node: Central Orchestrator"]
    KinePlugin["Kinematics Plugin: KDL, IKFast, TRAC IK"]
    PlanPlugin["Planning Plugin: OMPL RRT, PRM, EST, BITstar"]
    CollPlugin["Collision Plugin: FCL Flexible Collision Library"]
    TrajExec["Trajectory Execution: FollowJointTrajectory Action"]
    PlanScene["Planning Scene Monitor: Octomap, Point Cloud"]
    RobotDesc["URDF plus SRDF: Robot and Semantic Description"]
    Hardware["Hardware Layer: Real Robot or Gazebo Simulator"]

    UserInterface --> MoveGroup
    MoveGroup --> KinePlugin
    MoveGroup --> PlanPlugin
    MoveGroup --> CollPlugin
    MoveGroup --> TrajExec
    MoveGroup --> PlanScene
    PlanScene --> CollPlugin
    KinePlugin --> RobotDesc
    PlanPlugin --> RobotDesc
    CollPlugin --> RobotDesc
    TrajExec --> Hardware
```

## 4.2 MoveIt Setup Assistant Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    StartTab["Step 1: Start: Load URDF"]
    SelfCol["Step 2: Self Collisions: Generate Matrix"]
    VirtJ["Step 3: Virtual Joints: Anchor to World"]
    PlanGrp["Step 4: Planning Groups: Define Kine Chain"]
    Poses["Step 5: Robot Poses: Home and Ready"]
    EE["Step 6: End Effectors: Gripper Designation"]
    Passives["Step 7: Passives: Exclude Joints"]
    GenPkg["Generate Config Package: SRDF plus YAML Files"]
    DemoLaunch["Launch demo.launch.py: Open RViz"]

    StartTab --> SelfCol --> VirtJ --> PlanGrp --> Poses --> EE --> Passives --> GenPkg --> DemoLaunch
```

## 4.3 RViz Motion Planning Plugin Internal State Machine (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Planning : Click Plan Button
    Planning --> PlanSuccess : OMPL Returns Path
    Planning --> PlanFailed : No Valid Path
    PlanSuccess --> Visualising : Path Drawn in RViz
    Visualising --> Executing : Click Execute
    Visualising --> Idle : User Modifies Goal
    PlanFailed --> Idle : User Adjusts Scene
    Executing --> Idle : Trajectory Finished
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualisation Tip:** When asked to draw the MoveIt architecture in the exam, this layered block diagram (User Interface → move_group → Plugins → Robot) is the **highest-scoring** representation. The KTU Board examiner awards full marks when all four sub-components (Kinematics, Planning, Collision, Execution) are explicitly labelled.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Jun 2023 (Model)]**
*Define MoveIt and list its three primary sub-components.*

**Model Answer:**
MoveIt is an open-source **motion planning framework** for robotic manipulators built on top of ROS. Its three primary sub-components coordinated by the `move_group` node are:
1. **Kinematics Plugin** – solves Forward and Inverse Kinematics.
2. **Planning Plugin** – typically uses the **Open Motion Planning Library (OMPL)** to compute collision-free paths.
3. **Collision Checking Plugin** – uses the **Flexible Collision Library (FCL)** to detect self-collision and world-collision.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for naming the node + 1 for listing the three plugins.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 (Model)]**
*What is the difference between URDF and SRDF? Why is SRDF required by MoveIt?*

**Model Answer:**
- **URDF (Unified Robot Description Format):** an XML file that describes the *physical* properties of the robot: links, joints, inertias, visual meshes, and collision meshes. It is **purely geometric/dynamic**.
- **SRDF (Semantic Robot Description Format):** a complementary XML file that describes *semantic* information MoveIt needs: **planning groups, end-effectors, virtual joints, named poses, and passive joints**.
- **Why SRDF is required:** URDF alone cannot tell MoveIt which joints belong to the "arm" and which belong to the "gripper". SRDF provides this **planning-group abstraction**, which is the foundation of all motion planning requests.

*(3 marks: 1 each for definition + 1 for the why-part.)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]

**(a)** Explain the architecture of MoveIt with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the `move_group` node. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a step-by-step procedure, explain how you would **familiarise yourself with the MoveIt RViz plugin** using the Panda robot demo. Mention the purpose of the **Planning Library dropdown** and the **interactive marker**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

The MoveIt architecture is organised in **four layers**:

**Layer 1 – User Interface:**
The user interacts with MoveIt through either the **RViz Motion Planning Plugin** (GUI) or the **C++/Python API**. The RViz plugin is used for quick prototyping and visualisation; the API is used for autonomous pipelines.

**Layer 2 – move_group Node (C++):**
The `move_group` node is the **central orchestrator**. It:
- Subscribes to the **Joint States** topic to know the current configuration $\mathbf{q}_{current}$.
- Publishes to the **TF** tree to broadcast the transforms of every link.
- Provides ROS **Actions** `/plan_kinematics_path` and `/execute_trajectory`.
- Loads the **URDF** and **SRDF** from the parameter server.
- Maintains the **Planning Scene**.

**Layer 3 – Plugin Layer:**
- **Kinematics Plugin** – KDL, IKFast, or TRAC-IK solves IK.
- **Planning Plugin** – OMPL computes collision-free paths.
- **Collision Plugin** – FCL does real-time collision queries.

**Layer 4 – Robot Layer:**
The **ros2_control** hardware interface (or Gazebo) executes the trajectory.

> Block diagram (refer to **Section 4.1** Mermaid diagram for the full schematic). Full marks require the diagram + the role of `move_group` + 3 plugin names.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Step 1:** Open a terminal and source ROS 2:
`source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash`

**Step 2:** Launch the pre-built Panda demo:
`ros2 launch panda_moveit_config demo.launch.py rviz_tutorial:=true`

This command does three things simultaneously:
- Starts the `move_group` node.
- Launches RViz with the MotionPlanning plugin pre-loaded.
- Starts a fake trajectory controller that animates the robot in RViz.

**Step 3:** In the RViz MotionPlanning panel:
- **Planning Library dropdown** lets you choose the OMPL algorithm. Common choices:
  - `RRTConnectkConfigDefault` (fastest, most common).
  - `PRMkConfigDefault` (good for narrow passages).
  - `BITstar` (asymptotically optimal).

**Step 4:** The **interactive marker** is a set of RGB arrows attached to the end-effector.
- **Drag it** to set a new goal pose.
- The IK solver continuously computes $\mathbf{q}_{goal}$ such that $T_{0}^{n}(\mathbf{q}_{goal}) = X_{goal}$.
- If a valid IK solution exists, the arm "snaps" to the new pose in RViz.

**Step 5:** Click **"Plan"** → OMPL returns a trajectory. Click **"Execute"** → the robot animates the motion in RViz.

---

### Question B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]

**(a)** What is the MoveIt Setup Assistant? List and briefly explain **any five tabs** of the Setup Assistant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is OMPL? Name and briefly explain **three sampling-based planners** used by MoveIt. Justify why RRT-Connect is the default choice for most industrial manipulators. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

The **MoveIt Setup Assistant (MSSA)** is a GUI wizard used to generate the **MoveIt configuration package** (SRDF + YAML files) for a given robot. The seven tabs are:

1. **Start** – Loads the URDF and sets the output directory.
2. **Self-Collisions** – Pre-computes which link pairs can never collide (e.g., adjacent links sharing a joint) to speed up planning.
3. **Virtual Joints** – Anchors the robot's root link to the world (fixed) or to a mobile base (planar).
4. **Planning Groups** – The most important tab; groups joints into a kinematic chain (e.g., `arm`, `gripper`) and selects the kinematics solver (KDL, IKFast).
5. **Robot Poses** – Stores named configurations such as `home`, `ready`, `up` for quick recall.
6. **End Effectors** – Designates the final link of a group as a gripper, allowing the marker to be attached to it.
7. **Passives** – Excludes joints that should not be commanded (e.g., passive caster wheels).

*(5 tabs × ~1.2 marks = 6 marks, plus 1 mark for the introductory definition.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library)** is an open-source C++ library of **sampling-based motion planning algorithms**. MoveIt uses OMPL as its default planning backend.

**Three sampling-based planners:**

1. **RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree):** Grows a tree from the start state by extending towards randomly sampled points. Provides fast initial solutions but is not optimal.
2. **RRT-Connect:** A bidirectional variant of RRT that grows **two trees** simultaneously (one from start, one from goal) and tries to connect them. **Typically 10–100× faster** than vanilla RRT.
3. **PRM (Probabilistic Roadmap):** Pre-computes a graph of collision-free configurations, then uses graph search (A*) to find a path. Excellent for **narrow passages** and repeated queries in the same environment.
4. **EST (Expansive Space Trees):** Grows trees in low-density regions; good for problems where the state space is not well-understood.
5. **KPIECE / LBKPIECE:** Kinematic planners designed specifically for **high-dimensional robotic systems**.

**Why RRT-Connect is the default:**
- It is **probabilistically complete** (guaranteed to find a solution if one exists, given enough time).
- It is **fastest on average** for typical 6-DOF and 7-DOF manipulators in moderately cluttered environments.
- It has **low memory footprint** and a simple implementation, making it industrial-grade and reliable.

*(3 planners × 1.5 marks = 4.5 marks + 2.5 marks for RRT-Connect justification.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in the KTU Board exam (and how to avoid losing marks):**
> 1. **Writing only the MoveIt definition** without naming `move_group`, OMPL, and FCL → -2 marks. **Always list at least three sub-components.**
> 2. **Confusing URDF with SRDF** → URDF is geometric, SRDF is semantic. Mixing them up costs a full mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention "OMPL" by name** when asked about the planner → the default answer "RRT" without OMPL is incomplete. Always say "OMPL-based RRT".
> 4. **Drawing the architecture diagram without arrows** showing data flow (User → move_group → Plugin → Robot) → -1 mark. **Use arrows**, not just labelled boxes.
> 5. **Saying "MoveIt plans the path"** without explaining *how* (sampling-based, collision-checked) → the Board expects the keyword "sampling-based planner" or "OMPL".

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MoveIt = Motion Planning Framework for ROS** (the de-facto standard).
- The **RViz Motion Planning Plugin** is a GUI that hides the C++/Python complexity behind clickable buttons and an interactive marker.
- **Three core plugins** (memorise verbatim): Kinematics, Planning (OMPL), Collision (FCL).
- **URDF = geometry/kinematics; SRDF = semantics/planning groups.** Both are mandatory.
- The **Setup Assistant** has **7 tabs**; the most exam-relevant are *Planning Groups*, *End Effectors*, and *Virtual Joints*.
- **OMPL planners** to remember: **RRT, RRT-Connect (default), PRM, EST, KPIECE, BIT\***.
- **Interactive marker** = real-time **Inverse Kinematics** visualiser attached to the end-effector.
- **"Plan"** computes a trajectory; **"Execute"** sends it to the controller.
- The demo launch animates the robot in **RViz only**; real hardware needs **ros2_control**.
- **Block diagram arrows matter** in the exam – always show the data flow from User Interface → `move_group` → Plugin Layer → Robot.
- **TF (Transform)** is what makes the entire 3D visualisation possible – the robot's pose at every instant is broadcast on the `/tf` topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Planning with the Panda or other robot models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL607 - ROS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Motion Planning with the Panda or other robot models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Motion Planning** in the context of the Franka Emika **Panda** (or any serial manipulator integrated with ROS) refers to the computational process of generating a collision-free, kinematically feasible, and dynamically smooth trajectory for a robot from an initial configuration to a desired goal configuration, expressed in either joint space ($\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$) or task space ($\mathbf{x} \in SE(3)$).

Within the **Robot Operating System (ROS)** ecosystem, motion planning is implemented through the **MoveIt! 2** framework, which acts as a meta-package that orchestrates:
- The **OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library)** for sampling-based planning algorithms (RRT, RRT*, PRM, KPIECE, etc.).
- The **FCL (Flexible Collision Library)** / **Bullet** physics engine for collision detection.
- The **KDL (Kinematics and Dynamics Library)** / **Trac-IK** for inverse kinematics.
- The **TF2** library for coordinate transformations.

For the **Panda robot**, which is a **7 Degrees of Freedom (7-DOF)** redundant manipulator, motion planning must address the redundancy resolution problem, where infinite joint configurations exist for a given end-effector pose.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Panda robot URDF (Unified Robot Description Format) is the standard benchmark manipulator in modern robotics research. KTU laboratory examinations and assignments frequently use it as the reference model because it is lightweight, fully open-source, and natively supported by `moveit_resources`.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded inside a room cluttered with furniture, and you must walk from the door to a window.

- **You** = the robot's end-effector.
- **The cluttered room** = the configuration space ($C$-space), where every point represents a possible joint state.
- **The furniture** = obstacles in the *planning scene*, expanded by safety margins (the "padding" around obstacles).
- **Your memory of the room** = the *occupancy voxel grid* maintained by MoveIt.
- **A path you find by sampling random safe points and connecting them** = a sampling-based planner (like RRT).

**Motion planning with the Panda** is essentially this scenario — but the "room" is a **7-dimensional hyperspace** (because the Panda has 7 revolute joints), and the "furniture" is represented as 3D collision objects in **RViz**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** The reason sampling-based planners work in high-dimensional spaces is that explicit grid-based search becomes computationally intractable beyond 4-5 dimensions (the "curse of dimensionality"). RRT and PRM circumvent this by randomly probing the free space.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are universally applied to evaluate a Panda motion plan:

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** **7** (seven revolute joints, $q_1$ through $q_7$).
- **Reach (Workspace Radius):** **0.855 m** (from base to end-effector tip).
- **Payload:** **3 kg** nominal, **5 kg** peak (payload matters because it affects dynamic limits but not geometric planning).
- **Joint Velocity Limit:** **2.175 rad/s** (typical maximum, $q_1, q_2$); other joints vary.
- **Planning Time Default in MoveIt:** **5.0 seconds**.
- **Goal Position Tolerance:** **0.01 m** translational, **0.01 rad** rotational (default `position_only_ik` thresholds).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A planner is considered to have **SUCCEEDED** only if it returns a trajectory where every waypoint is collision-free and respects joint limits. A trajectory that reaches the goal but violates a joint limit is a **FAILED** plan in KTU's evaluation rubric.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The motion planning pipeline for the Panda robot (or any ROS-compatible manipulator) follows a **seven-step logical chain**:

### Step 1 — Scene Representation
The world is described as a **Planning Scene** consisting of two layers:
1. **Robot Layer:** The Panda's self-collision geometry (collision-enabled meshes from URDF).
2. **World Layer:** External obstacles, added as `CollisionObject` messages via `/collision_object` topic or Python API.

### Step 2 — Forward Kinematics Mapping
Given a joint vector $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_7]^T$, the end-effector pose is computed as:

$$
\mathbf{T}_{base}^{ee}(\mathbf{q}) = \prod_{i=1}^{7} \mathbf{A}_i(q_i) \in SE(3)
$$

where $\mathbf{A}_i$ is the $4 \times 4$ Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) transformation matrix for joint $i$.

### Step 3 — Configuration Space Sampling
A sampling-based planner like **RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree)** iteratively samples random configurations $\mathbf{q}_{rand} \in C_{free}$ and extends the tree from the nearest node $\mathbf{q}_{near}$:

$$
\mathbf{q}_{new} = \mathbf{q}_{near} + \Delta q \cdot \frac{\mathbf{q}_{rand} - \mathbf{q}_{near}}{\| \mathbf{q}_{rand} - \mathbf{q}_{near} \|}
$$

where $\Delta q$ is the step size (default: **0.5 rad** in MoveIt's `RRTConnectkConfigDefault`).

### Step 4 — Collision Checking
Each candidate $\mathbf{q}_{new}$ is checked against the FCL/Bullet engine using an **AABB (Axis-Aligned Bounding Box)** pre-filter, followed by exact GJK (Gilbert-Johnson-Keerthi) distance computation.

### Step 5 — Inverse Kinematics (IK) Solution
For a target Cartesian pose $\mathbf{x}_{goal} = (p_x, p_y, p_z, \phi_x, \phi_y, \phi_z)$, the IK solver finds:

$$
\mathbf{q}^* = \arg\min_{\mathbf{q}} \| f(\mathbf{q}) - \mathbf{x}_{goal} \|
$$

subject to $\mathbf{q}_{min} \leq \mathbf{q} \leq \mathbf{q}_{max}$. For the Panda, **KDL** is the default; **Trac-IK** is preferred for its higher success rate on redundant arms.

### Step 6 — Trajectory Post-Processing
The discrete waypoint path is time-parameterized using algorithms like **ITERative SiLK (ITSL)** or **Time-Optimal Trajectory Parameterization (TOPP-RA)** to ensure velocity and acceleration continuity:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}}(t) \text{ and } \ddot{\mathbf{q}}(t) \text{ must satisfy the actuator torque limits.}
$$

### Step 7 — Execution
The trajectory is published to a controller (e.g., `panda_arm_controller`) via the `FollowJointTrajectory` action server.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Purpose | KTU-Revealed Insight |
|------|---------|----------------------|
| Scene Representation | Convert 3D geometry into a queryable structure | MoveIt uses Octomap for voxelized 3D perception |
| Forward Kinematics | Map joint state → Cartesian pose | Required for any IK-based goal specification |
| C-space Sampling | Avoid explicit enumeration (NP-hard) | RRT is *probabilistically complete*, not optimal |
| Collision Checking | Reject infeasible configurations | The bottleneck; ~70% of planning time |
| IK Solution | Bridge Cartesian goals to joint space | For 7-DOF Panda, redundancy = null-space motions |
| Trajectory Smoothing | Ensure smooth, executable motion | Raw RRT paths are jerky; smoothing is mandatory |
| Execution | Close the perception-action loop | Real hardware requires `controller_manager` |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** Vectors are bold, scalars are italic. $\vert \cdot \vert$ is used for absolute value in tables to avoid breaking Markdown.

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | DH Transformation | $A_i = R_z(\theta_i) \cdot T_z(d_i) \cdot T_x(a_i) \cdot R_x(\alpha_i)$ | $\theta$ [rad], $d$ [m], $a$ [m], $\alpha$ [rad] |
| 2 | End-Effector Pose | $T_{base}^{ee}(\mathbf{q}) \in SE(3)$ | $4 \times 4$ homogeneous matrix |
| 3 | RRT Extend | $\mathbf{q}_{new} = \mathbf{q}_{near} + \Delta q \cdot \frac{\mathbf{q}_{rand} - \mathbf{q}_{near}}{\vert \mathbf{q}_{rand} - \mathbf{q}_{near} \vert}$ | $\Delta q \in [0, \pi]$ [rad] |
| 4 | Joint Limits | $q_{i,min} \leq q_i \leq q_{i,max}$ | From URDF `<limit>` tags |
| 5 | Workspace Volume (Panda) | $V = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3$ | $r = 0.855$ [m] |
| 6 | Manipulability Index | $w(\mathbf{q}) = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}))}$ | $\mathbf{J}$ is the $6 \times 7$ Jacobian |
| 7 | IK Null-Space Projection | $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0 = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}^\dagger \mathbf{J}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}_0$ | $\mathbf{J}^\dagger$ = pseudoinverse |
| 8 | Planning Time Default | $T_{plan} = 5.0$ [s] | MoveIt default |
| 9 | Collision Padding | $d_{pad} = 0.02$ [m] | Standard safety margin |
| 10 | Position Tolerance | $\vert \mathbf{p}_{goal} - \mathbf{p}_{actual} \vert \leq 0.01$ [m] | `goal_position_tolerance` |
| 11 | Orientation Tolerance | $\vert \theta_{err} \vert \leq 0.01$ [rad] | `goal_orientation_tolerance` |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Motion planning with the Panda is foundational in:

- **Industrial Pick-and-Place:** Used in research labs and pilot factories for bin-picking and assembly.
- **Medical Robotics:** Surgical arms (like the da Vinci Research Kit) reuse MoveIt + Panda URDFs for safe human-robot collaboration.
- **Autonomous Manipulation:** Mobile manipulators in warehouses (Amazon Robotics style) use similar pipelines.
- **AI/RL Training:** The Panda is the de facto benchmark in **NVIDIA Isaac Sim** and **MuJoCo** for reinforcement learning research.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics for a Generic 7-DOF Arm

The **Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) parameters** define the relative pose between consecutive links. For each joint $i$, the homogeneous transformation is:

$$
A_i(q_i) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(q_i) & -\sin(q_i)\cos(\alpha_i) & \sin(q_i)\sin(\alpha_i) & a_i\cos(q_i) \\ \sin(q_i) & \cos(q_i)\cos(\alpha_i) & -\cos(q_i)\sin(\alpha_i) & a_i\sin(q_i) \\ 0 & \sin(\alpha_i) & \cos(\alpha_i) & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step-by-Step Construction

**Step 1:** Define the DH table for the Panda (using the **modified DH convention** as in `franka_description`):

| Joint $i$ | $a_i$ [m] | $d_i$ [m] | $\alpha_i$ [rad] | $q_i$ offset [rad] |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | 0.0 | 0.333 | 0.0 | 0.0 |
| 2 | 0.0 | 0.0 | $-\pi/2$ | 0.0 |
| 3 | 0.0 | 0.316 | $\pi/2$ | 0.0 |
| 4 | 0.0825 | 0.0 | $\pi/2$ | 0.0 |
| 5 | -0.0825 | 0.384 | $-\pi/2$ | 0.0 |
| 6 | 0.0 | 0.0 | $\pi/2$ | 0.0 |
| 7 | 0.088 | 0.0 | $\pi/2$ | 0.0 |

**Step 2:** Compose the full transformation:

$$
T_{base}^{ee}(q_1, \ldots, q_7) = A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 \cdot A_4 \cdot A_5 \cdot A_6 \cdot A_7
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} R_{3 \times 3} & p_{3 \times 1} \\ 0_{1 \times 3} & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Extract position $p = [p_x, p_y, p_z]^T$ and orientation $R$:

$$
p_x = f_x(q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5, q_6, q_7) \quad \text{[m]}
$$

$$
p_y = f_y(q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5, q_6, q_7) \quad \text{[m]}
$$

$$
p_z = f_z(q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5, q_6, q_7) \quad \text{[m]}
$$

The full symbolic expansion is omitted here for brevity but is computed symbolically in MoveIt's `robot_state` and used by `kdl_parser`.

**Step 4:** For the analytical Jacobian:

$$
\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial p}{\partial q_1} & \cdots & \frac{\partial p}{\partial q_7} \\ \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial q_1} & \cdots & \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial q_7} \end{bmatrix}_{6 \times 7}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the Panda's 7-DOF arm, the Jacobian is **$6 \times 7$** (rectangular, not square). This means $\mathbf{J}$ is not directly invertible. KTU's valuation expects you to recognize this as the source of the **redundancy resolution** problem, which is solved via the **pseudoinverse** $\mathbf{J}^\dagger = \mathbf{J}^T (\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)^{-1}$ or the **null-space projection** $(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}^\dagger \mathbf{J})$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Motion Planning Algorithms Comparison

| Algorithm | Type | Optimality | Completeness | Best Use Case | MoveIt Default? |
|-----------|------|------------|--------------|---------------|------------------|
| RRT | Sampling | No | Probabilistic | Single-query, cluttered | No |
| RRT* | Sampling | Asymptotically optimal | Probabilistic | Research benchmarks | No |
| RRT-Connect | Sampling, bidirectional | No | Probabilistic | **Default for Panda** | **YES** |
| PRM | Sampling | No (KPIECE variant is) | Probabilistic | Multi-query, static | No |
| KPIECE | Sampling, projection-based | No | Probabilistic | High-DOF (>10) | Yes (alternative) |
| CHOMP | Optimization | Local optimum | Local | Smooth trajectories | Optional plugin |
| STOMP | Optimization, stochastic | Local optimum | Local | Obstacle avoidance | Optional plugin |
| OMPL's LBKPIECE | Hybrid | No | Probabilistic | Constrained spaces | Yes |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Generating a Plan in MoveIt (Python API)

This is the canonical KTU lab procedure for moving the Panda to a target pose:

**Step 1 — Initialize the MoveIt commander:**

```python
import rospy
import moveit_commander
import geometry_msgs.msg

moveit_commander.roscpp_initialize(sys.argv)
rospy.init_node('panda_motion_planner', anonymous=True)

robot = moveit_commander.RobotCommander()
scene = moveit_commander.PlanningSceneInterface()
group = moveit_commander.MoveGroupCommander("panda_arm")
```

**Step 2 — Set the planning parameters:**

```python
group.set_planning_time(5.0)        # 5-second timeout
group.set_num_planning_attempts(10) # 10 retries before failure
group.set_goal_position_tolerance(0.01)  # 1 cm accuracy
group.set_goal_orientation_tolerance(0.01)  # ~0.57 deg
group.set_max_velocity_scaling_factor(0.5)  # 50% of max speed
group.set_max_acceleration_scaling_factor(0.5)
```

**Step 3 — Define the target pose:**

```python
pose_goal = geometry_msgs.msg.Pose()
pose_goal.position.x = 0.4
pose_goal.position.y = 0.0
pose_goal.position.z = 0.4
pose_goal.orientation.w = 1.0  # Neutral orientation
```

**Step 4 — Invoke the planner:**

```python
group.set_pose_target(pose_goal)

plan = group.go(wait=True)  # Returns True on success

group.stop()
group.clear_pose_targets()
```

**Step 5 — Validate the result:**

```python
if plan:
    rospy.loginfo("Planning SUCCEEDED — trajectory executed.")
else:
    rospy.logerr("Planning FAILED — no collision-free path found within timeout.")
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Line-by-line reasoning for KTU lab records:**
> - `set_planning_time(5.0)` corresponds to OMPL's `solve()` deadline.
> - `set_num_planning_attempts(10)` triggers a different random seed each retry, increasing success probability.
> - `wait=True` blocks the script until execution finishes (essential for sequence-controlled demos).
> - The `panda_arm` group is defined in the SRDF (Semantic Robot Description Format) file `panda.srdf`.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: IK Seed Validation

**Problem:** Given a goal pose $(p_x, p_y, p_z) = (0.5, 0.0, 0.3)$ [m] and neutral orientation, verify whether the joint seed $\mathbf{q}_{seed} = [0, -0.785, 0, -2.356, 0, 1.571, 0.785]^T$ [rad] is within reachable workspace.

**Step 1:** Compute the distance from base:

$$
d = \sqrt{p_x^2 + p_y^2 + p_z^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0 + 0.09} = \sqrt{0.34} \approx 0.583 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** Compare with Panda's reach:

$$
d = 0.583 \text{ m} \quad < \quad r_{reach} = 0.855 \text{ m} \quad \Rightarrow \text{REACHABLE}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with minimum reach (Panda minimum $\approx 0.0$ m with fingers closed):

$$
d = 0.583 \text{ m} \quad > \quad d_{min} = 0.0 \text{ m} \quad \Rightarrow \text{IN BOUNDS}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the FK function $f(\mathbf{q}_{seed})$ using the DH chain:

$$
f(\mathbf{q}_{seed}) = (0.5, 0.0, 0.3) \quad \text{(matches the goal within } 0.01 \text{ m tolerance)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This numerical check is exactly what KTU evaluators expect in **Part B 14-mark questions**: an explicit comparison of the candidate distance against the workspace bounds, with a clear YES/NO conclusion and a numerical justification.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Motion Planning Pipeline (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Goal Pose or Joint Goal] --> B[MoveIt MoveGroup Commander]
    B --> C[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    C --> D{Goal Type}
    D -->|Joint Space| E[Direct Joint Target]
    D -->|Cartesian Space| F[IK to Joint Target]
    E --> G[OMPL Planner RRTConnect]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Collision Checking via FCL]
    H --> I{Collision Free}
    I -->|No| J[Backtrack or Resample]
    J --> G
    I -->|Yes| K[Add Waypoint to Tree]
    K --> L{Goal Reached}
    L -->|No| G
    L -->|Yes| M[Trajectory Post Processing]
    M --> N[Time Parameterization ITSL or TOPP]
    N --> O[FollowJointTrajectory Action]
    O --> P[Panda Joint Controllers]
    P --> Q[Execution on Real or Simulated Robot]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Start at the top-left `A` (the user's goal) and follow the arrows downward. Every decision diamond (`{}`) represents a binary branch. The loop `G → H → I → J → G` is the *rejection sampling* mechanism that gives RRT its probabilistic completeness.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Q:** Define *configuration space* and explain why a 7-DOF Panda robot has a 7-dimensional configuration space.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **configuration space** ($C$-space) of a robot is the set of all possible configurations the robot can attain. For a rigid robot, a *configuration* is a complete specification of the position of every point on the robot. For the **Panda** with 7 revolute joints, each joint is described by a single angle $q_i \in \mathbb{R}$, so a configuration is the joint vector $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5, q_6, q_7]^T \in \mathbb{R}^7$. Hence the configuration space $C = \mathbb{R}^7$ is 7-dimensional. The subset of collision-free configurations is denoted $C_{free} \subset C$, and planning aims to find a continuous path in $C_{free}$ from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$.
*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for joint vector, 1 mark for $C_{free}$ and planning objective.)*

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**Q:** List any three sampling-based motion planning algorithms supported by OMPL that can be used with the Panda robot.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree)** — single-query, grows a tree from the start.
2. **RRT-Connect** — bidirectional variant, grows two trees from start and goal simultaneously; **default** for the Panda in MoveIt.
3. **PRM (Probabilistic Roadmap)** — multi-query, builds a graph of randomly sampled milestones.
*(Optional 4th: KPIECE, LBKPIECE, RRT*, etc. for 1 bonus mark.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B.A [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model] (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the **MoveIt 2** framework for motion planning of a Panda robot in ROS. Discuss the role of the URDF, SRDF, OMPL, and FCL components. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **MoveIt 2** framework is a modular motion planning stack built on ROS 2. Its architecture consists of three principal layers:

1. **Input Layer:**
   - **URDF (Unified Robot Description Format):** XML file describing the Panda's kinematic and dynamic properties — links, joints, inertial tensors, collision meshes, and visual meshes. The file is `panda.urdf.xacro` from the `franka_description` package.
   - **SRDF (Semantic Robot Description Format):** Extends the URDF with planning-specific data — *planning groups* (e.g., `panda_arm`, `panda_hand`), *virtual joints*, and *collision link pairs to disable* (e.g., adjacent links whose self-collision is geometrically impossible).
   - **TF2 / Octomap:** Real-time 3D transforms and voxelized perception data.

2. **Core Engine Layer:**
   - **OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library):** Provides sampling-based planners like RRT, RRT-Connect, PRM. MoveIt wraps OMPL via the `ompl_interface` plugin.
   - **FCL (Flexible Collision Library):** Performs AABB and GJK-based collision detection between the robot's swept volume and the planning scene.
   - **KDL / Trac-IK:** Solve the inverse kinematics for Cartesian goals.

3. **Output Layer:**
   - **Trajectory Messages** of type `trajectory_msgs/JointTrajectory` published to controllers.
   - **RViz Motion Planning Plugin** for interactive scene editing and plan visualization.
   - **MoveIt Commander (C++ / Python)** for scripted planning.

*Block diagram: refer to Section 4.2 above.*

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for the three-layer structure, 2 marks for URDF/SRDF roles, 2 marks for OMPL/FCL roles, 1 mark for output layer.)*

**(b)** Describe the **RRT algorithm** in detail. Show the pseudo-code and explain how the algorithm ensures probabilistic completeness for the Panda robot's motion planning. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree)** is a single-query sampling-based planner introduced by LaValle (1998). It incrementally grows a tree $T = (V, E)$ rooted at $q_{start}$ by random sampling in $C_{free}$.

**Pseudo-code:**

```
RRT(q_start, q_goal, K, Δq):
    V ← {q_start}
    E ← {}
    for k = 1 to K do
        q_rand ← RANDOM_CONFIGURATION(C_free)
        q_near ← NEAREST(V, q_rand)
        q_new ← STEER(q_near, q_rand, Δq)
        if COLLISION_FREE(q_near, q_new) then
            V ← V ∪ {q_new}
            E ← E ∪ {(q_near, q_new)}
            if ||q_new − q_goal|| < ε then
                return PATH(q_start, q_new)
    return FAILURE
```

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Random Sampling:** $q_{rand}$ is drawn uniformly from $C$ (or biased toward $q_{goal}$ with probability $p_{goal}$, typically 0.05–0.1).
2. **Nearest Neighbor:** $q_{near} = \arg\min_{q \in V} \vert q - q_{rand} \vert$ using Euclidean distance in joint space.
3. **Steering:** $q_{new} = q_{near} + \Delta q \cdot \frac{q_{rand} - q_{near}}{\vert q_{rand} - q_{near} \vert}$, capped at step size $\Delta q$.
4. **Collision Check:** Verify the segment $q_{near} \rightarrow q_{new}$ is in $C_{free}$ using FCL.
5. **Insertion:** Add $q_{new}$ to $V$ and the edge to $E$.
6. **Goal Test:** If $q_{new}$ is within $\varepsilon$ of $q_{goal}$, return the path; otherwise iterate.

**Probabilistic Completeness:** As the number of iterations $K \to \infty$, the probability that RRT fails to find a solution (when one exists) approaches zero. Formally:

$$
\lim_{K \to \infty} P[\text{RRT fails to find a solution}] = 0
$$

This holds for any robot, including the 7-DOF Panda, because the Voronoi bias of random sampling causes the tree to rapidly explore $C_{free}$.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for pseudo-code, 2 marks for step-by-step logic, 2 marks for probabilistic completeness, 1 mark for Panda-specific comment.)*

---

### Question B.B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model] (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **inverse kinematics problem** for a redundant manipulator like the Panda. Discuss the role of the **Jacobian pseudoinverse** and the **null-space projection** in redundancy resolution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A manipulator is **kinematically redundant** when it has more DOF than the task space requires. The Panda has 7 DOF, while a typical end-effector task in $SE(3)$ requires 6 DOF (3 position + 3 orientation). Hence there is **1 degree of redundant mobility**.

**IK Problem:** Given a desired end-effector pose $\mathbf{x}_d \in SE(3)$, find $\mathbf{q}^*$ such that:

$$
f(\mathbf{q}^*) = \mathbf{x}_d
$$

For 7-DOF arms, the inverse problem has **infinitely many solutions** if any exist.

**Jacobian Pseudoinverse Method:** The differential IK relation is:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

A least-norm solution is obtained via the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^\dagger(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \left[ \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \right]^{-1} \dot{\mathbf{x}}
$$

This minimizes $\vert \dot{\mathbf{q}} \vert^2$ subject to the task constraint.

**Null-Space Projection:** The homogeneous (null-space) solution adds internal motions that do not affect the end-effector:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0 = (\mathbf{I}_7 - \mathbf{J}^\dagger \mathbf{J}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}_0
$$

The full solution is:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^\dagger \dot{\mathbf{x}} + (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}^\dagger \mathbf{J}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}_0
$$

The null-space term $(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}^\dagger \mathbf{J})$ projects any arbitrary joint velocity $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0$ onto the null space of $\mathbf{J}$, so it has zero effect on $\dot{\mathbf{x}}$. This allows secondary objectives — such as **obstacle avoidance**, **joint-limit avoidance**, or **manipulability maximization** — to be performed simultaneously with the primary task.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for IK problem definition, 2 marks for pseudoinverse derivation, 2 marks for null-space projection, 1 mark for secondary objectives.)*

**(b)** Demonstrate with a Python code snippet (using `moveit_commander`) how you would plan and execute a motion of the Panda arm from its current configuration to a Cartesian target pose $(0.4, 0.0, 0.4)$ with neutral orientation. Explain each line. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

```python
import sys
import rospy
import moveit_commander
import geometry_msgs.msg

# 1. Initialize the moveit_commander and ROS node
moveit_commander.roscpp_initialize(sys.argv)
rospy.init_node('panda_plan_and_execute', anonymous=True)

# 2. Instantiate the robot, scene, and move group interface
robot = moveit_commander.RobotCommander()
scene = moveit_commander.PlanningSceneInterface()
group = moveit_commander.MoveGroupCommander("panda_arm")

# 3. Configure planning parameters
group.set_planning_time(5.0)          # 5-second planner timeout
group.set_num_planning_attempts(10)   # 10 retries with new seeds
group.set_goal_position_tolerance(0.01)     # 1 cm positional accuracy
group.set_goal_orientation_tolerance(0.01)  # ~0.57 deg orient accuracy
group.set_max_velocity_scaling_factor(0.5)  # 50% of max joint speed
group.set_max_acceleration_scaling_factor(0.5)

# 4. Define the target pose
pose_target = geometry_msgs.msg.Pose()
pose_target.position.x = 0.4
pose_target.position.y = 0.0
pose_target.position.z = 0.4
pose_target.orientation.x = 0.0
pose_target.orientation.y = 0.0
pose_target.orientation.z = 0.0
pose_target.orientation.w = 1.0   # Unit quaternion for neutral orientation

# 5. Set the target and invoke the planner
group.set_pose_target(pose_target)
plan_success = group.go(wait=True)  # Blocks until execution finishes

# 6. Clean up
group.stop()
group.clear_pose_targets()

# 7. Verify the result
if plan_success:
    rospy.loginfo("Motion plan executed successfully.")
else:
    rospy.logwarn("Motion planning failed within the allotted time.")
moveit_commander.roscpp_shutdown()
```

**Line-by-line explanation:**

- **Lines 1–5:** Import dependencies and initialize the MoveIt commander; the `anonymous=True` flag allows multiple instances of the same node.
- **Line 6:** `MoveGroupCommander("panda_arm")` binds to the planning group defined in `panda.srdf`.
- **Lines 9–13:** Set tolerance, planning time, and velocity/acceleration limits — these are crucial for **safe execution** on real hardware.
- **Lines 16–22:** Define the target as a `geometry_msgs/Pose` message. The neutral orientation $(0,0,0,1)$ corresponds to the gripper pointing downward in the Panda's convention.
- **Line 25:** `group.go(wait=True)` performs **plan + execute** atomically. Setting `wait=False` would only plan.
- **Line 30:** `group.clear_pose_targets()` resets internal state to avoid contamination of subsequent plans.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for the imports/init, 2 marks for planning parameters, 2 marks for the `go()` execution flow, 1 mark for cleanup and validation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing `group.go()` (plan + execute) with `group.plan()` (plan only). In KTU exams, the difference is worth 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to call `moveit_commander.roscpp_shutdown()` at the end — this causes node leaks and will be penalized in lab evaluations.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing `set_pose_target` with **radians** in the orientation field. MoveIt expects a **unit quaternion**, not Euler angles.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Claiming the Panda has 6 DOF. It has **7** DOF — this single error has historically cost students 2 marks in viva voce.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Failing to mention the **`wait=True`** parameter and its role in synchronous execution — this is a frequent trap in 14-mark coding questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Panda Motion Planning Module:**

- [x] The **Panda** is a **7-DOF** redundant serial manipulator from **Franka Emika**.
- [x] Motion planning in ROS is implemented via **MoveIt 2**, which orchestrates **OMPL**, **FCL**, **KDL/Trac-IK**, and **TF2**.
- [x] The Panda's reach is **0.855 m**, payload is **3 kg** nominal.
- [x] The default planner in MoveIt for the Panda is **RRT-Connect** (bidirectional RRT).
- [x] **Configuration space** $C = \mathbb{R}^7$ for the Panda; planning aims to find a path in $C_{free} \subset C$.
- [x] **Sampling-based planners** (RRT, PRM, KPIECE) are **probabilistically complete** but not optimal in finite time.
- [x] **RRT*** is **asymptotically optimal** — the path cost converges to the global optimum as iterations $\to \infty$.
- [x] The Panda's Jacobian $\mathbf{J}$ is a **$6 \times 7$** rectangular matrix; the redundancy is resolved via the **pseudoinverse** $\mathbf{J}^\dagger$.
- [x] **Null-space projection** $(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}^\dagger \mathbf{J})$ allows secondary objectives (obstacle avoidance, manipulability) without disturbing the primary task.
- [x] The **URDF** describes geometry/kinematics; the **SRDF** adds planning groups and disabled collision pairs.
- [x] The Python API for MoveIt is `moveit_commander`; the C++ API is `moveit::planning_interface::MoveGroupInterface`.
- [x] **Planning time** default is **5 s**; default position tolerance is **0.01 m**; default orientation tolerance is **0.01 rad**.
- [x] Trajectories must be **time-parameterized** (ITSL or TOPP-RA) before execution to enforce velocity and acceleration continuity.
- [x] The `set_max_velocity_scaling_factor` and `set_max_acceleration_scaling_factor` parameters are **essential safety knobs** in lab evaluations.
- [x] The `panda_arm` planning group excludes the gripper by default; the `panda_arm_hand` group includes the gripper fingers.
- [x] In KTU lab records, always report the **planner used**, **planning time**, **trajectory duration**, and **success/failure status**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Create MoveIt package for robotic arm simulation and add controllers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL607 - ROS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Create MoveIt package for robotic arm simulation and add controllers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module Experiment 2 — MoveIt Package Creation & Controller Integration

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MoveIt** is the most widely adopted open-source **Motion Planning Framework** for robotic manipulators, built on top of ROS (Robot Operating System). It integrates sampling-based planners (OMPL), inverse kinematics solvers (KDL, IKFast, TRAC-IK), 3D perception (Octomap), and execution management into a unified `move_group` node.

A **MoveIt Configuration Package** is a generated ROS package (using the **MoveIt Setup Assistant**) that contains all the launch, configuration, and SRDF files required to make a robot arm motion-plannable inside **RViz** and **Gazebo**. **Controllers** are the `ros_control`-compliant software components (running inside `controller_manager`) that translate MoveIt's planned trajectories into real-time joint commands (`position`, `velocity`, or `effort`).

In this experiment we will:
1. Generate a MoveIt package for a manipulator (e.g., a 6-DOF arm).
2. Attach a **ros_control-based controller stack** so the planned trajectories can be executed in simulation.
3. Verify the closed loop: **MoveIt → FollowJointTrajectory → Gazebo → Joint States → MoveIt**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Brain, The Planner, and The Muscles"

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a robotic arm as a **human arm performing a task**:
> - **URDF (Unified Robot Description Format)** → The *skeleton* — defines bones (links) and joints, with their lengths, limits, and inertias.
> - **MoveIt** → The *brain + cerebellum* — decides *where* the hand should go, plans a collision-free path, and times the motion.
> - **Controllers (ros_control)** → The *muscles and motor neurons* — convert the brain's high-level plan into actual low-level current/voltage signals that drive each joint motor.
> - **Gazebo** → A *virtual gym* where the robot practices its movements in a safe, simulated world before touching real hardware.

Without the controllers, MoveIt is a "planner with no mouth" — it knows the trajectory mathematically but cannot make the robot execute it. **Adding controllers is what closes the feedback loop** between the abstract plan and the physical (or simulated) motion.

### 1.3 Key Standardised Metrics & Constants

- **Update Rate of controller_manager**: typically **1 kHz** (1000 Hz) for hardware, **1000 Hz** default in Gazebo.
- **Trajectory execution tolerance**: default `0.01` (position), `0.01` (velocity) in `FollowJointTrajectory` controller.
- **Planning pipeline default**: `OMPL` (Open Motion Planning Library).
- **Standard solver**: `KDL` (Kinematics and Dynamics Library) by default; alternatives: `TRAC-IK`, `IKFast`, `MoveItKinematics`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 Architecture of the MoveIt + Controller Stack

The complete simulation pipeline is composed of **four interacting layers**. Understanding each layer is essential for the KTU viva and for debugging integration issues.

### Layer 1 — Description Layer (URDF + Xacro + Transmissions)
The **URDF** describes the kinematic chain. To make joints actuatable, we must declare **transmissions** (typically `transmission_interface/SimpleTransmission` or `FourBarLinkageTransmission`) that bind a joint to an **actuator interface** (`position`, `velocity`, or `effort`).

### Layer 2 — Hardware Interface (ros_control)
`ros_control` provides a generic **HardwareInterface** abstraction. The two main implementations in simulation are:
- `gazebo_ros_control` plugin (in `.urdf.xacro` of the robot, inside `<gazebo><plugin .../></gazebo>`)
- `gazebo_ros2_control` (the ROS 2 equivalent; for KTU 2024, the ROS 1 pipeline is most commonly examined)

### Layer 3 — Controller Manager
`controller_manager` spawns, configures, switches, and loads controllers declared in a YAML file (`controllers.yaml`). For a manipulator we typically spawn:
1. `joint_state_controller` (`joint_state_controller/JointStateController`) — publishes `/joint_states`.
2. `arm_controller` (`position_controllers/JointTrajectoryController` or `effort_controllers/JointTrajectoryController`) — accepts `FollowJointTrajectory` goals from MoveIt.

### Layer 4 — Planning Layer (MoveIt move_group)
`move_group` subscribes to `/joint_states`, plans paths in collision-aware configuration space, and publishes **timed trajectories** to the `FollowJointTrajectory` action server exposed by `arm_controller`. The `MoveItControllerManager` plugin (`moveit_simple_controller_manager`) handles the action-server handshake.

## 2.2 The MoveIt Setup Assistant — What it Generates

When you click **"Generate Package"** in the MoveIt Setup Assistant, it produces the following files:

| File / Folder | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `config/*.yaml` | Kinematics solver, OMPL planner, planning pipelines, joint limits, controllers |
| `config/*.srdf` | Semantic Robot Description Format — virtual joints, planning groups, poses, end-effectors |
| `launch/*.launch` | `demo.launch`, `move_group.launch`, `planning_context.launch`, `setup_assistant.launch` |
| `config/kinematics.yaml` | Specifies IK solver and attempt timeout (e.g., `KDL` with `0.05` s) |
| `config/joint_limits.yaml` | Overrides URDF joint limits during planning |
| `config/controllers.yaml` | Maps MoveIt controller names → action types (`FollowJointTrajectory`) |

## 2.3 Controllers.yaml — The Bridge File

> [!NOTE]
> The `controllers.yaml` file inside the MoveIt config folder is the **single most important file for the KTU experiment record**. It tells MoveIt which ROS action topic to call for execution.

A canonical snippet:

```yaml
controller_list:
  - name: arm_controller
    action_ns: follow_joint_trajectory
    type: FollowJointTrajectory
    default: true
    joints:
      - shoulder_pan_joint
      - shoulder_lift_joint
      - elbow_joint
      - wrist_1_joint
      - wrist_2_joint
      - wrist_3_joint
```

The `controller_manager`-side `controllers.yaml` is a *different* file (often `ros_controllers.yaml`) loaded by the spawner:

```yaml
arm_controller:
  type: position_controllers/JointTrajectoryController
  joints:
    - shoulder_pan_joint
    - shoulder_lift_joint
    - elbow_joint
    - wrist_1_joint
    - wrist_2_joint
    - wrist_3_joint
  constraints:
    stopped_velocity_tolerance: 0.05
    goal_time: 0.6
```

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Parameter Sheet

| Concept | Expression / Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward Kinematics | $T_{0}^{n}(\mathbf{q}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} {}^{i-1}T_{i}(q_i)$ | Homogeneous 4x4 matrix | $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ joint vector |
| Inverse Kinematics | Solve $T_{0}^{n}(\mathbf{q}) = T_{\text{goal}}$ for $\mathbf{q}$ | rad or m | Solved numerically by KDL/TRAC-IK |
| Sampling time (controller loop) | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | s | Default $f_s = 1000$ Hz $\Rightarrow T_s = 0.001$ s |
| Trajectory tolerance (default) | $\epsilon_{pos} = 0.01$, $\epsilon_{vel} = 0.01$ | rad, rad/s | Set in controller YAML |
| Position controller law | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_d \dot{e}(t) + K_i \int e(\tau)d\tau$ | N·m or V | PID on joint error |
| Effort controller (gravity comp) | $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ | N·m | Computed via `gazebo_ros_control/KDL` plugin |
| Damped Least-Squares IK | $\Delta q = J^{T}(JJ^{T} + \lambda^{2}I)^{-1} \cdot e_{cart}$ | rad | Used in KDL when Jacobian is near-singular |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The MoveIt + ros_control pipeline is the **de-facto industry standard** for robotic arm research and light industrial deployment. It is used in:
- **Mobile manipulators** (e.g., Fetch, Toyota HSR) for pick-and-place.
- **Surgical robots** (e.g., research prototypes of the da Vinci).
- **Warehouse robotics** (e.g., Amazon Robotics manipulators) for bin picking.
- **Collaborative arms** (Universal Robots, Franka Panda) whose driver stacks expose `FollowJointTrajectory` actions that this same MoveIt pipeline can call.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Procedure, Derivations & Execution Logic

## 3.1 Phase 1 — Launch the MoveIt Setup Assistant

Open a terminal and source the workspace, then launch the wizard. The Setup Assistant is a Qt-based GUI that walks through **six configuration screens**.

```bash
source /opt/ros/noetic/setup.bash
source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash

roslaunch moveit_setup_assistant setup_assistant.launch
```

**Step 1 — Start**: Click *Create New MoveIt Configuration Package* and point it to your robot's `URDF` or `xacro` file (e.g., `src/my_arm_description/urdf/my_arm.urdf` or `my_arm.urdf.xacro`).

> [!NOTE]
> If using xacro, you must process it first: `xacro my_arm.urdf.xacro > my_arm.urdf`, or supply the xacro macro arguments in the Setup Assistant's "Load Xacro" field.

**Step 2 — Self-Collisions**: The wizard samples 10,000 random configurations and computes a *default collision matrix* that disables adjacent-link collision checks (e.g., link1↔link2) because they are physically bolted together. Click **"Regenerate Default Collision Matrix"** → **"Save"**.

**Step 3 — Virtual Joints**: Define how the robot base is anchored to the world. For a *fixed-base* arm, create a virtual joint of type `fixed` linking `base_link` to `world`. For a *mobile manipulator*, use `planar` or `floating` between `base_footprint` and `world`.

**Step 4 — Planning Groups**: This is the heart of the configuration. You define a `group` (e.g., `arm_group`) by either:
- **Kinematic chain**: select base link → tip link (e.g., `base_link` → `gripper_link`).
- **Joint collection**: list individual joints.
Then assign:
- **Kinematic solver**: `KDL` (default), `TRAC-IK` (recommended), or `IKFast`.
- **Ompl planner**: `RRTConnectkConfigDefault` (good default).

**Step 5 — Robot Poses**: Pre-define named configurations (e.g., `home`, `ready`, `up`) by setting joint values. These are useful for repeating the same "reset" pose.

**Step 6 — End Effectors**: Optionally declare a gripper group as a sub-group of the arm.

**Step 7 — Generate Package**: Click *Browse* → pick a path inside `~/catkin_ws/src/` → name it `my_arm_moveit_config` → *Generate*. The wizard writes ~25 files.

## 3.2 Phase 2 — Add Transmissions to the URDF

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without a **transmission** tag in the URDF, the controller_manager cannot bind a hardware interface to a joint. The arm will exist geometrically but will not actuate.

For each actuated joint, insert a transmission block:

```xml
<transmission name="trans_shoulder_pan">
  <type>transmission_interface/SimpleTransmission</type>
  <joint name="shoulder_pan_joint">
    <hardwareInterface>hardware_interface/PositionJointInterface</hardwareInterface>
  </joint>
  <actuator name="motor_shoulder_pan">
    <hardwareInterface>hardware_interface/PositionJointInterface</hardwareInterface>
    <mechanicalReduction>1</mechanicalReduction>
  </actuator>
</transmission>
```

## 3.3 Phase 3 — Attach the Gazebo ros_control Plugin

Append the following block inside the `<robot>` tag of the URDF (or, preferably, in a `my_arm.urdf.xacro`):

```xml
<gazebo>
  <plugin name="gazebo_ros_control" filename="libgazebo_ros_control.so">
    <robotNamespace>/</robotNamespace>
    <robotSimType>gazebo_ros_control/DefaultRobotHWSim</robotSimType>
  </plugin>
</gazebo>
```

This plugin spawns a `controller_manager` instance bound to the simulated joints.

## 3.4 Phase 4 — Create the Controller YAML and Launch File

Create a `config/ros_controllers.yaml` in your robot's description package:

```yaml
my_arm:
  # Publish all joint states -----------------------------------
  joint_state_controller:
    type: joint_state_controller/JointStateController
    publish_rate: 50

  # Position Trajectory Controller -----------------------------
  arm_controller:
    type: position_controllers/JointTrajectoryController
    joints:
      - shoulder_pan_joint
      - shoulder_lift_joint
      - elbow_joint
      - wrist_1_joint
      - wrist_2_joint
      - wrist_3_joint
    gains:
      shoulder_pan_joint:   {p: 1000.0, d: 1.0, i: 0.0, i_clamp: 1.0}
      shoulder_lift_joint:  {p: 1000.0, d: 1.0, i: 0.0, i_clamp: 1.0}
      elbow_joint:          {p: 1000.0, d: 1.0, i: 0.0, i_clamp: 1.0}
      wrist_1_joint:        {p: 1000.0, d: 1.0, i: 0.0, i_clamp: 1.0}
      wrist_2_joint:        {p: 1000.0, d: 1.0, i: 0.0, i_clamp: 1.0}
      wrist_3_joint:        {p: 1000.0, d: 1.0, i: 0.0, i_clamp: 1.0}
    state_publish_rate:  50
    action_monitor_rate: 10
```

Then create a launch file `launch/my_arm_gazebo_controllers.launch` that spawns both controllers into `controller_manager`:

```xml
<launch>
  <node name="controller_spawner" pkg="controller_manager" type="spawner"
        respawn="false" output="screen"
        args="joint_state_controller arm_controller"/>
  <node name="robot_state_publisher" pkg="robot_state_publisher" type="robot_state_publisher"
        respawn="false" output="screen">
    <param name="publish_frequency" value="50"/>
  </node>
</launch>
```

## 3.5 Phase 5 — Build and Launch

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws
catkin_make
source devel/setup.bash
```

Launch the **Gazebo simulation** of the arm:

```bash
roslaunch my_arm_gazebo my_arm.launch
```

In a second terminal, spawn the controllers:

```bash
roslaunch my_arm_gazebo my_arm_gazebo_controllers.launch
```

In a third terminal, launch the MoveIt demo:

```bash
roslaunch my_arm_moveit_config demo.launch
```

Inside RViz, drag the **interactive marker** on the end-effector, set a goal, and click **"Plan and Execute"**. The trajectory will flow: **MoveIt → FollowJointTrajectory action → arm_controller → Gazebo simulated joints → /joint_states feedback → MoveIt**.

## 3.6 Verification Commands

```bash
rostopic list
# Expected topics include:
#   /arm_controller/follow_joint_trajectory/goal
#   /arm_controller/follow_joint_trajectory/result
#   /joint_states
#   /gazebo/link_states
#   /move_group

rosservice call /controller_manager/list_controllers
# Expected output:
#   joint_state_controller[joint_state_controller/JointStateController] running
#   arm_controller[position_controllers/JointTrajectoryController] running

rostopic echo /joint_states | head -20
# Should show non-zero changing values when the arm moves.
```

## 3.7 PID Control Law Derivation (Why the `gains` Block Exists)

For each joint, `gazebo_ros_control` implements a position PID controller. The commanded torque is computed as:

$$
u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

where the error is the difference between the **planned joint position** $q_{\text{plan}}(t)$ (interpolated from the trajectory) and the **measured joint position** $q_{\text{meas}}(t)$:

$$
e(t) = q_{\text{plan}}(t) - q_{\text{meas}}(t)
$$

The discrete-time form (sampled at $T_s = 1/1000$ s) is:

$$
u_k = K_p e_k + K_i T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e_j + \frac{K_d}{T_s}(e_k - e_{k-1})
$$

For a position-controlled arm without gravity load, $K_p$ can be high (e.g., 1000) and $K_d$ small (1.0) to give critically-damped behaviour. For an effort controller with gravity compensation, $K_p$ is typically lower (100–500) since the gravity term is already supplied by `gazebo_ros_control/KDL`.

## 3.8 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Controller to Choose?

| Controller Type | Best For | Trajectory Type | Hardware Interface |
|---|---|---|---|
| `position_controllers/JointTrajectoryController` | Lightweight arms, position-mode servos | `FollowJointTrajectory` (position) | `PositionJointInterface` |
| `effort_controllers/JointTrajectoryController` | Torque-controlled arms, dynamic contact | `FollowJointTrajectory` (effort) | `EffortJointInterface` |
| `velocity_controllers/JointVelocityController` | Wheels, conveyor tracking | Direct velocity command | `VelocityJointInterface` |
| `joint_state_controller/JointStateController` | Always required — publishes `/joint_states` | N/A | All interfaces |
| `gripper_controller/GripperController` | Parallel-jaw grippers with simple open/close | Action: `GripperCommand` | `PositionJointInterface` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — MoveIt ⇄ Controller ⇄ Gazebo Closed Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[USER GOAL in RViz] --> B[move_group Node]
    B --> C[Planning Pipeline OMPL KDL]
    C --> D[Collision Checking FCL Octomap]
    D --> E[Trajectory Output FollowJointTrajectory Action]
    E --> F[arm_controller JointTrajectoryController]
    F --> G[controller_manager]
    G --> H[Hardware Interface PositionJointInterface]
    H --> I[Gazebo Physics Engine ODE Bullet]
    I --> J[Simulated Joint Encoders]
    J --> K[joint_state_controller]
    K --> L[/joint_states Topic]
    L --> B
    L --> M[robot_state_publisher TF Tree]
    M --> N[RViz Visualization]
    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style F fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style I fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style N fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 MoveIt Setup Assistant Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Tab Load URDF] --> S2[Self Collisions Sample 10000 Configs]
    S2 --> S3[Virtual Joints Anchor Base]
    S3 --> S4[Planning Groups Define Kinematic Chain]
    S4 --> S5[Robot Poses Predefined Joint Values]
    S5 --> S6[End Effectors Optional Sub group]
    S6 --> S7[Author Information Maintainer]
    S7 --> S8[Generate Package SRDF YAML Launch]
    S8 --> S9[my_arm_moveit_config Folder]
    style S1 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style S4 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style S8 fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style S9 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Controller File Relationship Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    URDF[URDF Xacro] -- transmission block --> CI[Controller Manager]
    RY[ros_controllers.yaml] -- spawner --> CI
    MY[moveit controllers.yaml] -- moveit_simple_controller_manager --> MG[move_group]
    MG -- FollowJointTrajectory action --> CI
    CI -- PositionJointInterface --> GAZ[Gazebo Plugin gazebo_ros_control]
    GAZ -- simulated encoders --> JS[joint_states]
    JS -- feedback --> MG
    JS -- feedback --> RSP[robot_state_publisher]
    style URDF fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style MG fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style CI fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style GAZ fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Information-Flow Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant RViz
    participant MoveGroup
    participant ControllerMgr
    participant ArmCtrl
    participant Gazebo
    User->>RViz: drag interactive marker
    RViz->>MoveGroup: set pose goal
    MoveGroup->>MoveGroup: plan collision free path
    MoveGroup->>ControllerMgr: action goal FollowJointTrajectory
    ControllerMgr->>ArmCtrl: spawns JointTrajectoryController
    ArmCtrl->>Gazebo: position command per timestep
    Gazebo->>ArmCtrl: encoder feedback
    ArmCtrl->>MoveGroup: result trajectory completed
    MoveGroup->>RViz: animate execution marker
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** What is the MoveIt Setup Assistant and list any **four** files it generates.

> **Model Answer:**
> The MoveIt Setup Assistant is a graphical configuration tool used to generate a `moveit_config` package for any robot whose URDF is available. It walks the user through loading the URDF, configuring self-collisions, virtual joints, planning groups, robot poses, and end-effectors, then bundles all configuration into a single ROS package. **Four generated files/folders** are: (1) `config/*.srdf` (semantic description with planning groups), (2) `config/kinematics.yaml` (IK solver configuration), (3) `config/ompl_planning.yaml` (planner parameters), (4) `launch/demo.launch` (RViz + MoveIt demo launcher).

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between `position_controllers/JointTrajectoryController` and `effort_controllers/JointTrajectoryController`.

> **Model Answer:**
> The **position** controller commands joint *positions* (with an internal PID that drives the simulated motor to the target). It uses the `PositionJointInterface` and is suitable for servos that natively accept position setpoints — it is the **most common choice in KTU lab simulations** for arms like the UR5 or a 6-DOF educational arm.
> The **effort** controller commands joint *torques/forces* (interface `EffortJointInterface`). It requires gravity compensation, often provided by `gazebo_ros_control/KDL`, and is used for torque-controlled arms that need compliant behaviour or to handle contact dynamics.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ⬇️ **Question A (14 Marks)**  *(Internal Choice Option 1)*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question A]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete architecture** required to simulate motion planning of a 6-DOF robotic arm using MoveIt in Gazebo. Describe the roles of `move_group`, `controller_manager`, and `gazebo_ros_control`. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write the **step-by-step procedure** to create a MoveIt package using the MoveIt Setup Assistant and add a `FollowJointTrajectory` controller to the URDF. Show the relevant URDF transmission snippet and `ros_controllers.yaml` configuration. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The closed-loop architecture is shown in the diagram of Section 4.1 above. The functional pipeline is:

1. **User / RViz** — Operator drags the interactive marker representing the end-effector pose. The interactive marker publishes a `PoseStamped` to `/rviz_moveit_motion_planning_display/robot_interaction_interactive_marker_topic/update`.
2. **move_group (Planning Layer)** — Receives the goal pose, queries the **Planning Scene** (URDF + octomap + attached objects), invokes the **OMPL planner** to compute a collision-free joint-space path, and time-parameterises it into a `Trajectory` message.
3. **Controller Manager (ros_control)** — Spawns controllers declared in `ros_controllers.yaml`. For a manipulator we need a `joint_state_controller` (publishes `/joint_states`) and an `arm_controller` (exposes a `FollowJointTrajectory` action server).
4. **gazebo_ros_control plugin (Hardware Layer)** — Reads the position command from `arm_controller` every simulation tick (default 1 kHz), updates the simulated joint angle, and reports the new encoder value back to `joint_state_controller`.
5. **Feedback** — `/joint_states` closes the loop: MoveIt monitors execution; `robot_state_publisher` republishes TF so RViz can animate the arm.

**Roles summarised**:

| Node | Role |
|---|---|
| `move_group` | High-level planning, IK, collision, trajectory generation |
| `controller_manager` | Lifecycle (load/start/switch) of controllers |
| `gazebo_ros_control` | Simulated hardware interface; bridges physics engine to ros_control |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Launch the assistant**:
```bash
roslaunch moveit_setup_assistant setup_assistant.launch
```
