**Step 2** — Load the URDF, generate the self-collision matrix, define a `fixed` virtual joint, create a planning group `arm_group` using the *Kinematic Chain* method (base_link → gripper_link), set KDL as the IK solver, add a `home` pose with all joints = 0.

**Step 3** — Click *Generate Package* into `~/catkin_ws/src/my_arm_moveit_config`.

**Step 4 — Add a transmission to the URDF** for every actuated joint (model shown in Section 3.2):
```xml
<transmission name="trans_elbow">
  <type>transmission_interface/SimpleTransmission</type>
  <joint name="elbow_joint">
    <hardwareInterface>hardware_interface/PositionJointInterface</hardwareInterface>
  </joint>
  <actuator name="motor_elbow">
    <hardwareInterface>hardware_interface/PositionJointInterface</hardwareInterface>
    <mechanicalReduction>1</mechanicalReduction>
  </actuator>
</transmission>
```

**Step 5 — Attach the Gazebo control plugin** at the end of the URDF:
```xml
<gazebo>
  <plugin name="gazebo_ros_control" filename="libgazebo_ros_control.so">
    <robotNamespace>/</robotNamespace>
  </plugin>
</gazebo>
```

**Step 6 — Write `config/ros_controllers.yaml`** with `joint_state_controller` and `arm_controller` of type `position_controllers/JointTrajectoryController` (full file shown in Section 3.4).

**Step 7 — Write the spawner launch file** (Section 3.4) and a Gazebo launch file that loads the URDF via `robot_description` parameter.

**Step 8 — Build and launch**:
```bash
catkin_make
roslaunch my_arm_gazebo my_arm.launch
roslaunch my_arm_gazebo my_arm_gazebo_controllers.launch
roslaunch my_arm_moveit_config demo.launch
```

A successful demo where the end-effector moves to a clicked RViz goal confirms correct integration.

### ⬇️ **Question B (14 Marks)**  *(Internal Choice Option 2)*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question B]**
> **(a)** Explain the role of the `controllers.yaml` file inside the MoveIt config package. Show the contents of a sample `controllers.yaml` that maps a 6-DOF arm group to a `FollowJointTrajectory` action. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A student is unable to make the Gazebo-simulated arm move when clicking "Plan and Execute" in RViz. Diagnose **four probable root causes** and describe the verification command for each. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The `controllers.yaml` (in `my_arm_moveit_config/config/`) is consumed by the `moveit_simple_controller_manager` plugin loaded inside `move_group.launch`. It tells MoveIt:
- The **list of controllers** it can talk to.
- The **action namespace** under `/<controller_name>/...`.
- The **action type** — almost always `FollowJointTrajectory` for arms.
- The **list of joints** that the controller governs.
- A `default: true` flag identifying which controller to use when `move_group` has multiple options.

Sample content for a 6-DOF arm:
```yaml
controller_list:
  - name: arm_controller
    action_ns: follow_joint_trajectory
    type: FollowJointTrajectory
    default: true
    joints:
      - shoulder_pan_joint
      - shoulder_lift_joint
      - elbow_joint
      - wrist_1_joint
      - wrist_2_joint
      - wrist_3_joint
```

Note: This file is *separate* from the controller-manager-side `ros_controllers.yaml`. Both must agree on the controller name (`arm_controller`) and on the joint list, otherwise MoveIt will plan successfully but the execution will fail with a "controller not connected" error.

#### Model Solution for (b):

| Probable Cause | Verification Command | Expected Healthy Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Missing transmission in URDF** — `controller_manager` cannot bind to the joint. | `rosrun controller_manager spawner arm_controller` | Should print `Loaded arm_controller` (not "could not load"). |
| **`gazebo_ros_control` plugin not attached to URDF** — no hardware interface exists. | `rostopic echo /gazebo/link_states` and check that joints respond to commands. | Joints should move. |
| **`controllers.yaml` joint-name mismatch** — MoveIt and `controller_manager` disagree. | `rosservice call /controller_manager/list_controllers` and compare with MoveIt's `controllers.yaml`. | Joint lists must match exactly. |
| **`FollowJointTrajectory` action server not advertised** — MoveIt times out. | `rostopic list \vert grep follow_joint_trajectory` | Should show `/arm_controller/follow_joint_trajectory`. |

Other common issues include (i) wrong namespace in `<robotNamespace>`, (ii) `controller_manager` not running because the `gazebo_ros_control` plugin failed to load, and (iii) the URDF containing a `continuous` joint but a `position` controller.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks**
> - ❌ Confusing the two `controllers.yaml` files (MoveIt-side vs `controller_manager`-side). Always state *which file* you are editing.
> - ❌ Forgetting to declare a **transmission** in the URDF. A complete URDF for *simulation* requires BOTH `<joint>` definitions AND a matching `<transmission>`.
> - ❌ Naming mismatches (case-sensitivity matters in YAML). `Arm_Controller` ≠ `arm_controller`.
> - ❌ Omitting the `joint_state_controller`. Without it, `/joint_states` is silent, MoveIt cannot monitor execution, and `robot_state_publisher` produces no TF.
> - ❌ Editing `my_arm_moveit_config/config/controllers.yaml` but *not* `my_arm_gazebo/config/ros_controllers.yaml` (or vice versa). Both are required.
> - ❌ Running `roslaunch` in the wrong namespace, so RViz shows the robot in `/` but the controllers spawn under `/my_arm`.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- **MoveIt Setup Assistant** is the standard way to bootstrap a `moveit_config` package.
- The MoveIt config package contains **SRDF, kinematics.yaml, ompl_planning.yaml, controllers.yaml** and launch files.
- **Controllers bridge MoveIt (planner) ↔ Gazebo (physics)** — without them, planning succeeds but execution does not.
- Two YAML files must be coordinated: MoveIt's `controllers.yaml` (action-side) and `ros_controllers.yaml` (controller-manager-side).
- A **transmission** must be declared for every actuated joint in the URDF.
- The `gazebo_ros_control` plugin must be inserted in the URDF to bind simulated joints to `ros_control`.
- Default control loop rate is **1 kHz**; default tolerance is **0.01** for position and velocity.
- `joint_state_controller` (always required) publishes `/joint_states`.
- `position_controllers/JointTrajectoryController` is the standard choice for educational arms.
- `FollowJointTrajectory` is the canonical MoveIt→controller action interface.
- **Verification mantra**: `rostopic list` → `rosservice call /controller_manager/list_controllers` → RViz *Plan and Execute* → check `/joint_states` moves.
- **Common bug**: planning OK in RViz, but the robot does not move in Gazebo → 90 % of the time it is a missing **transmission**, **gazebo_ros_control plugin**, or **controller spawner**.
- **Internal choice (Part B)** in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE always offers two full 14-mark questions; prepare both MoveIt-internals and integration-debugging variants.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plan a path for a 3DOF Robotic Arm and execute the same
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL607 - ROS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Plan a path for a 3DOF Robotic Arm and execute the same

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**Path Planning** for a 3 Degrees of Freedom (3DOF) Robotic Arm is the computational process of generating a continuous, collision-free, and time-parameterized sequence of joint configurations (or Cartesian poses) that moves the end-effector from a **start pose** to a **goal pose**, subject to kinematic, dynamic, and environmental constraints. In the **Robot Operating System (ROS)** framework, this is realized through the integration of kinematic solvers (Forward & Inverse Kinematics), trajectory generators (Polynomial Interpolators), and the `move_group` node provided by **MoveIt!**, which communicates joint commands back to the robot via ROS topics and the TF (Transform) tree.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module Experiment 2):**
> A 3DOF arm operates in a 3-dimensional workspace $(x, y, z)$. Its pose is uniquely described by three joint variables $(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3)$. The planning problem is therefore a mapping from **Task Space** $\rightarrow$ **Joint Space**, executed through ROS pipelines.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to pick up a glass of water from a table. You do not think in terms of muscle contractions; you think in terms of **"where the hand needs to go"** (Cartesian path) and **"how to move my shoulder, elbow, and wrist to get there smoothly"** (joint trajectory).

- **The Blindfolded Person** = The Robot Controller
- **The Glass Location** = Goal Pose in Cartesian Space
- **Shoulder/Elbow/Wrist Angles** = Joint Variables $(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3)$
- **The Smooth Motion** = Trajectory (generated by cubic/quintic polynomials)

> [!NOTE]
> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**: The number of independent parameters that define the configuration of a mechanism. For a planar 3R manipulator, DOF = 3 because the end-effector can reach any point $(x, y)$ with one orientation, plus the base can rotate to span the full 3D space.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Gravity**: $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$ (relevant for gravity compensation, though not used in pure kinematic planning).
- **Standard Workspace Sampling Rate**: **100 Hz** (ROS default for `/joint_states` updates).
- **Trajectory Execution Frequency (MoveIt)**: **Typically 50 Hz** on standard hardware.
- **DH Parameter Convention**: Denavit-Hartenberg (1955) — standard for assigning coordinate frames to serial linkages.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

Path planning in ROS for a 3DOF arm is a **three-stage pipeline**:

1. **Scene Loading & TF Broadcasting**: The URDF (Unified Robot Description Format) is parsed. The `robot_state_publisher` broadcasts joint states to the TF tree.
2. **Kinematic Solving**: MoveIt's kinematic plugins (KDL, IKFast, or TRAC-IK) compute either:
   - **Forward Kinematics (FK)**: Joint angles $\rightarrow$ End-effector pose.
   - **Inverse Kinematics (IK)**: End-effector pose $\rightarrow$ Joint angles.
3. **Trajectory Generation & Execution**: The path is discretized into waypoints using interpolators; each waypoint is sent as a `FollowJointTrajectory` action goal to the controller.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 3DOF specifically for KTU labs?** It is mathematically tractable (closed-form IK exists), has a clear 3D workspace, and avoids the singularities and redundancy of 6+ DOF arms — making it ideal for board examination derivations.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | DH Transformation Matrix | $A_i = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta_i & -s\theta_i c\alpha_i & s\theta_i s\alpha_i & a_i c\theta_i \\ s\theta_i & c\theta_i c\alpha_i & -c\theta_i s\alpha_i & a_i s\theta_i \\ 0 & s\alpha_i & c\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $c\theta = \cos\theta$, $s\theta = \sin\theta$ |
| 2 | End-effector Position (3DOF planar) | $x = L_2 c_1 c_2 + L_3 c_1 c_{23}$ | $c_{23} = \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3)$ |
| 3 | End-effector Position (Y) | $y = L_2 s_1 c_2 + L_3 s_1 c_{23}$ | $s_1 = \sin\theta_1$ |
| 4 | End-effector Position (Z) | $z = d_1 + L_2 s_2 + L_3 s_{23}$ | $s_{23} = \sin(\theta_2 + \theta_3)$ |
| 5 | IK Angle $\theta_1$ | $\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(y, x)$ | Base swivel |
| 6 | IK Angle $\theta_3$ (cosine law) | $\theta_3 = \text{atan2}(\sqrt{1-D^2}, D)$, $D = \frac{x^2+y^2+(z-d_1)^2 - L_2^2 - L_3^2}{2 L_2 L_3}$ | Elbow up/down |
| 7 | Cubic Polynomial (Joint Trajectory) | $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ | Smooth start/stop |
| 8 | Trajectory Coefficients | $a_0 = q_0$, $a_1 = 0$, $a_2 = \frac{3(q_f - q_0)}{T^2}$, $a_3 = \frac{-2(q_f - q_0)}{T^3}$ | Zero velocity BCs |
| 9 | Linear Cartesian Velocity | $v = \frac{\Delta P}{T}$ | $m/s$ for straight-line |
| 10 | Joint Velocity Bound | $\vert \dot{q}_i \vert \le \dot{q}_{i,\max}$ | Trapezoidal profile check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**: This pipeline is the backbone of industrial **pick-and-place robots** (KUKA, ABB, FANUC), **surgical manipulators** (da Vinci System), and **collaborative robots (cobots)** like UR5. The 3DOF subsystem often forms the *positioning stage* of these larger systems, with additional wrist joints handling orientation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics for a Standard 3DOF Arm

**Assumed DH Table (typical KTU lab arm configuration):**

| Joint $i$ | $\theta_i$ | $d_i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ |
|-----------|------------|-------|-------|------------|
| 1 | $\theta_1$ | $d_1$ (base height) | 0 | $90°$ |
| 2 | $\theta_2$ | 0 | $L_2$ | $0°$ |
| 3 | $\theta_3$ | 0 | $L_3$ | $0°$ |

**Step 1: Construct individual transformation matrices.**

$$
A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & 0 & s_1 & 0 \\ s_1 & 0 & -c_1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & d_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} c_2 & -s_2 & 0 & L_2 c_2 \\ s_2 & c_2 & 0 & L_2 s_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
A_3 = \begin{bmatrix} c_3 & -s_3 & 0 & L_3 c_3 \\ s_3 & c_3 & 0 & L_3 s_3 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Multiply $A_1 \cdot A_2$ first (the intermediate wrist frame).**

$$
A_1 A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 c_2 & -c_1 s_2 & s_1 & c_1 L_2 c_2 \\ s_1 c_2 & -s_1 s_2 & -c_1 & s_1 L_2 c_2 \\ s_2 & c_2 & 0 & d_1 + L_2 s_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Explanation of logic*: The first two columns of the product represent the rotated basis vectors; the fourth column is the position vector of frame 2's origin in frame 0. The third row reflects the elevation $d_1 + L_2 \sin\theta_2$.

**Step 3: Multiply $(A_1 A_2) \cdot A_3$ to obtain the full end-effector transform $T_{0}^{3}$.**

$$
T_{0}^{3} = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 c_{23} & -c_1 s_{23} & s_1 & c_1(L_2 c_2 + L_3 c_{23}) \\ s_1 c_{23} & -s_1 s_{23} & -c_1 & s_1(L_2 c_2 + L_3 c_{23}) \\ s_{23} & c_{23} & 0 & d_1 + L_2 s_2 + L_3 s_{23} \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Extract the end-effector position vector.**

$$
P_{ee} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} c_1(L_2 c_2 + L_3 c_{23}) \\ s_1(L_2 c_2 + L_3 c_{23}) \\ d_1 + L_2 s_2 + L_3 s_{23} \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Conversion logic*: The fourth column of the homogeneous matrix $T_{0}^{3}$ (rows 1–3) gives the position of the end-effector origin relative to the base frame. This is the standard **Forward Kinematics result** for a 3DOF RRR arm with the first joint rotating the planar arm in the $xy$-plane.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Cubic Polynomial Trajectory

**Given**: Start joint angle $q_0$, final joint angle $q_f$, total time $T$, zero initial and final velocity.

**Step 1: Write the general cubic polynomial and its derivative.**

$$
q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3
$$

$$
\dot{q}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2
$$

**Step 2: Apply the four boundary conditions.**

$$
q(0) = q_0 \Rightarrow a_0 = q_0
$$

$$
q(T) = q_f \Rightarrow a_0 + a_1 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 = q_f
$$

$$
\dot{q}(0) = 0 \Rightarrow a_1 = 0
$$

$$
\dot{q}(T) = 0 \Rightarrow a_1 + 2 a_2 T + 3 a_3 T^2 = 0
$$

**Step 3: Solve the resulting linear system.**

From $a_1 = 0$ and $a_0 = q_0$, the remaining equations reduce to:

$$
q_0 + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 = q_f
$$

$$
2 a_2 T + 3 a_3 T^2 = 0
$$

Solving the second equation for $a_2$:

$$
a_2 = -\frac{3}{2} a_3 T
$$

Substituting into the first equation:

$$
q_0 - \frac{3}{2} a_3 T^3 + a_3 T^3 = q_f
$$

$$
q_0 - \frac{1}{2} a_3 T^3 = q_f
$$

$$
a_3 = \frac{2(q_0 - q_f)}{T^3}
$$

Back-substituting:

$$
a_2 = -\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{2(q_0 - q_f)}{T^3} \cdot T = \frac{3(q_f - q_0)}{T^2}
$$

**Step 4: Final compact form of the cubic trajectory.**

$$
q(t) = q_0 + \frac{3(q_f - q_0)}{T^2} t^2 - \frac{2(q_f - q_0)}{T^3} t^3
$$

*Conversion logic*: This equation guarantees $C^1$ continuity (continuous position and velocity) at the waypoint. It is the **default trajectory type** in MoveIt for point-to-point motion and is the most tested concept in KTU viva voce.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Path Planning Execution Steps in ROS

| Stage | ROS Component | Message/Service Type | Output |
|-------|---------------|----------------------|--------|
| 1. Load Robot Model | `robot_description` (URDF on parameter server) | `std_msgs/String` (XML) | Robot kinematic tree |
| 2. Current State | `joint_state_publisher` | `sensor_msgs/JointState` | Joint angles |
| 3. TF Broadcasting | `robot_state_publisher` | `tf2_msgs/TFMessage` | Coordinate frames |
| 4. Goal Specification | RViz / Python script | `moveit_msgs/Constraints` | Goal pose |
| 5. Plan Computation | `move_group` node | `moveit_msgs/MotionPlanRequest` | Trajectory |
| 6. Trajectory Execution | Controller Manager | `control_msgs/FollowJointTrajectory` | Motor commands |
| 7. Feedback | Joint controllers | `control_msgs/JointTrajectoryControllerState` | Status updates |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ROS 3DOF Path Planning Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[URDF Robot Description] --> B[robot_state_publisher]
    B --> C[TF Tree Broadcast]
    C --> D[MoveIt move_group Node]
    E[Goal Pose in RViz or Script] --> D
    F[Planning Scene] --> D
    D --> G[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    G --> H[Trajectory Generator Cubic Polynomial]
    H --> I[Collision Checking OMPL / STOMP]
    I --> J{Plan Valid}
    J -- Yes --> K[Execute FollowJointTrajectory Action]
    J -- No --> L[Replan with Different Planner]
    L --> D
    K --> M[ros_control Controllers]
    M --> N[Hardware Joint Actuators]
    N --> O[End Effector Reaches Goal]
```

### 4.2 Joint Space vs Cartesian Space Planning Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Specifies Start and Goal] --> B{Path Type}
    B -- Option 1 --> C[Cartesian Linear Path]
    C --> C1[Interpolate Straight Line in xyz]
    C1 --> C2[Apply IK at Each Waypoint]
    C2 --> D[Joint Trajectory]
    B -- Option 2 --> E[Joint Space Path]
    E --> E1[Interpolate Each Joint Angle]
    E1 --> E2[No IK Required]
    E2 --> D
    D --> F[Send to Controllers]
    F --> G[Arm Executes Smooth Motion]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of MoveIt Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer
        A1[URDF XML]
        A2[SRDF Semantic Description]
        A3[OMPL Planning Library]
    end
    subgraph ProcessingLayer
        B1[Kinematics Plugin KDL or IKFast]
        B2[Collision Detection FCL]
        B3[Trajectory Smoothing]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer
        C1[moveit_msgs RobotTrajectory]
        C2[control_msgs FollowJointTrajectory]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> D1[Hardware Execution]
```

### 4.4 3DOF Arm Kinematic Schematic (Top-Down Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J1[Base Joint theta1 Rotation about Z] --> L1[Link 1 Length d1 Vertical]
    L1 --> J2[Shoulder Joint theta2 Rotation about X]
    J2 --> L2[Link 2 Length L2]
    L2 --> J3[Elbow Joint theta3 Rotation about X]
    J3 --> L3[Link 3 Length L3]
    L3 --> EE[End Effector Position x y z]
    style J1 fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style J2 fill:#fc9,stroke:#333
    style J3 fill:#9cf,stroke:#333
    style EE fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of a robotic manipulator. With respect to a 3DOF planar arm, explain the role of Forward and Inverse Kinematics in path planning.*

**Model Answer:**
Degrees of Freedom (DOF) is the number of independent joint parameters required to completely specify the configuration of a manipulator in 3D space. A 3DOF arm has three revolute joints $(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3)$ that uniquely position the end-effector at any reachable point $(x, y, z)$.

- **Forward Kinematics (FK)**: Maps joint angles $\rightarrow$ Cartesian pose using the homogeneous transformation $T = A_1 A_2 A_3$. Used to verify the current end-effector position during execution.
- **Inverse Kinematics (IK)**: Maps desired Cartesian pose $\rightarrow$ joint angles. Used to convert user-specified goal points (in $xyz$) into joint commands that the controller can execute.

In ROS/MoveIt, FK is computed via `robot_state_publisher` and IK via the kinematic plugin (KDL/IKFast), forming the **planning-execution bridge**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*What is a cubic polynomial trajectory? Why is it preferred over a linear trajectory for joint motion?*

**Model Answer:**
A cubic polynomial trajectory is a time-parameterized joint motion function of the form:
$$q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$
with coefficients determined by boundary conditions on position and velocity at $t=0$ and $t=T$.

**Why preferred over linear:**
1. **Zero velocity at start and end** — ensures smooth start/stop without jerks.
2. **Continuous velocity profile** — reduces mechanical stress on actuators and gears.
3. **Better tracking accuracy** — controllers can follow the smooth derivative profile easily.
4. **Avoids discontinuous acceleration** — a linear trajectory has instantaneous velocity changes, which are physically impossible to track.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Derive the Forward Kinematics expression for a 3DOF RRR manipulator with the following DH parameters. Clearly state the position vector of the end-effector. **(7 Marks)**

| Joint | $\theta_i$ | $d_i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ |
|-------|------------|-------|-------|------------|
| 1 | $\theta_1$ | $d_1$ | 0 | $90°$ |
| 2 | $\theta_2$ | 0 | $L_2$ | $0°$ |
| 3 | $\theta_3$ | 0 | $L_3$ | $0°$ |

**(b)** A 3DOF arm must move from joint configuration $q_0 = [0°, 30°, 45°]$ to $q_f = [90°, 60°, 90°]$ in $T = 4$ seconds using a cubic polynomial trajectory for **joint 2**. Derive the trajectory equation $q_2(t)$ and compute $q_2(2)$ and $\dot{q}_2(2)$. **(7 Marks)**

##### **Model Solution to (a):**

We construct the three DH matrices $A_1, A_2, A_3$ (as derived in Section 3.1) and multiply them sequentially:

$$
T_{0}^{3} = A_1 A_2 A_3
$$

$$
P_{ee} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} c_1(L_2 c_2 + L_3 c_{23}) \\ s_1(L_2 c_2 + L_3 c_{23}) \\ d_1 + L_2 s_2 + L_3 s_{23} \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the **end-effector position vector** in the base frame.

##### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1:** Identify boundary conditions for joint 2:
- $q_0 = 30° = \pi/6$ rad
- $q_f = 60° = \pi/3$ rad
- $T = 4$ s
- $\dot{q}(0) = \dot{q}(T) = 0$

**Step 2:** Compute coefficients using the derived cubic formula:

$$
a_0 = q_0 = \frac{\pi}{6}
$$

$$
a_1 = 0
$$

$$
a_2 = \frac{3(q_f - q_0)}{T^2} = \frac{3(\pi/3 - \pi/6)}{16} = \frac{3(\pi/6)}{16} = \frac{\pi}{32} \, rad/s^2
$$

$$
a_3 = \frac{-2(q_f - q_0)}{T^3} = \frac{-2(\pi/6)}{64} = -\frac{\pi}{192} \, rad/s^3
$$

**Step 3:** Trajectory equation:

$$
q_2(t) = \frac{\pi}{6} + \frac{\pi}{32} t^2 - \frac{\pi}{192} t^3
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $q_2(2)$:

$$
q_2(2) = \frac{\pi}{6} + \frac{\pi}{32}(4) - \frac{\pi}{192}(8)
$$

$$
q_2(2) = \frac{\pi}{6} + \frac{\pi}{8} - \frac{\pi}{24}
$$

Converting to a common denominator (LCD = 24):

$$
q_2(2) = \frac{4\pi}{24} + \frac{3\pi}{24} - \frac{\pi}{24} = \frac{6\pi}{24} = \frac{\pi}{4} = 45°
$$

**Step 5:** Compute $\dot{q}_2(2)$:

$$
\dot{q}_2(t) = \frac{\pi}{16} t - \frac{\pi}{64} t^2
$$

$$
\dot{q}_2(2) = \frac{\pi}{16}(2) - \frac{\pi}{64}(4) = \frac{\pi}{8} - \frac{\pi}{16} = \frac{\pi}{16} \, rad/s \approx 0.196 \, rad/s
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Explain the complete ROS architecture for executing a 3DOF arm path plan. List all the major ROS nodes, topics, and message types involved. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 3DOF arm with link lengths $L_2 = L_3 = 1$ m and base height $d_1 = 0.5$ m, compute the joint angles required to position the end-effector at point $P = (1.0, 0.0, 1.5)$ m using Inverse Kinematics. Assume the arm operates in the $xz$-plane. **(7 Marks)**

##### **Model Solution to (a):**

The complete ROS architecture for 3DOF arm path planning consists of the following layered components:

| Layer | Node / Component | Function |
|-------|------------------|----------|
| Description | URDF + xacro | Defines links, joints, visual/collision geometry |
| State | `joint_state_publisher` | Publishes `/joint_states` |
| State | `robot_state_publisher` | Broadcasts TF transforms |
| Planning | `move_group` (MoveIt) | Solves IK, plans, generates trajectory |
| Planning | OMPL plugin | Sampling-based motion planning (RRT, PRM) |
| Execution | `controller_manager` | Loads and manages joint controllers |
| Execution | `joint_trajectory_controller` | Receives `FollowJointTrajectory` action |
| Visualization | RViz | Displays robot, TF, planned path, goal poses |

**Message flow**:
- Input: `moveit_msgs/MotionPlanRequest` (goal pose)
- Output: `trajectory_msgs/JointTrajectory` (time-stamped joint waypoints)
- Action: `control_msgs/FollowJointTrajectory` (sent to controller)

**Execution loop**:
1. User launches `roslaunch myarm_moveit_config demo.launch`
2. RViz shows the interactive marker for the end-effector.
3. User drags the marker to the goal pose.
4. MoveIt's `move_group` solves IK, plans with OMPL, and computes a smooth trajectory.
5. On `Execute`, the trajectory is sent as an action to `joint_trajectory_controller`.
6. The controller publishes motor commands at 100 Hz until completion.

##### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1:** Compute $\theta_1$ (base swivel):

$$
\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(y, x) = \text{atan2}(0.0, 1.0) = 0°
$$

**Step 2:** Reduce to a 2-link planar problem in the $r$-$z$ plane:

$$
r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = \sqrt{1.0^2 + 0.0^2} = 1.0 \, m
$$

$$
z' = z - d_1 = 1.5 - 0.5 = 1.0 \, m
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $D$ using the cosine law for the elbow:

$$
D = \frac{r^2 + (z')^2 - L_2^2 - L_3^2}{2 L_2 L_3} = \frac{1.0 + 1.0 - 1.0 - 1.0}{2(1)(1)} = 0
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $\theta_3$:

$$
\theta_3 = \text{atan2}(\sqrt{1 - D^2}, D) = \text{atan2}(1, 0) = 90° = \frac{\pi}{2} \, rad
$$

**Step 5:** Compute $\theta_2$:

$$
\theta_2 = \text{atan2}(z', r) - \text{atan2}(L_3 \sin\theta_3, L_2 + L_3 \cos\theta_3)
$$

$$
\theta_2 = \text{atan2}(1.0, 1.0) - \text{atan2}(1.0 \cdot 1, 1.0 + 1.0 \cdot 0)
$$

$$
\theta_2 = 45° - 45° = 0° = 0 \, rad
$$

**Final Joint Configuration:** $q = [0°, 0°, 90°]$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of $\alpha_i$**: Students often write $\alpha_1 = 90°$ but forget to correctly apply it in $A_1$, leading to a sign error in the $z$-coordinate. Always re-derive $A_1$ when $\alpha = 90°$.
> 2. **Radian vs Degree**: Cubic polynomial coefficients $a_2, a_3$ **must be in radians** before plugging into $T$ (seconds). Mixing degrees and radians is the #1 cause of wrong numerical answers in KTU scripts.
> 3. **Atan2 Domain**: Always use `atan2(y, x)` (not `atan(y/x)`) to handle all four quadrants correctly. KTU examiners specifically check for this.
> 4. **Boundary Condition for Velocity**: Forgetting $\dot{q}(0) = 0$ leads to the **trivial linear trajectory** $q(t) = q_0 + (q_f - q_0) \cdot t/T$, which is worth **0 marks** in a cubic polynomial question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **3DOF arm** = 3 revolute joints; its end-effector pose is fully defined by $(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3)$; its position is the **fourth column** of $T_0^3$.
- **DH parameters** are the **mandatory first step** in any FK derivation — KTU almost always gives the DH table.
- **Forward Kinematics** is **deterministic and unique**; **Inverse Kinematics** may have **multiple solutions** (elbow up/down).
- **Cubic polynomial trajectory** is the **default in MoveIt** for point-to-point motion; coefficients depend only on $q_0, q_f, T$.
- **Quintic polynomial** (5th order) is used when **acceleration continuity** is also required — not in KTU 3DOF scope.
- **ROS execution flow**: URDF $\rightarrow$ TF $\rightarrow$ MoveIt `move_group` $\rightarrow$ `FollowJointTrajectory` action $\rightarrow$ controller $\rightarrow$ hardware.
- **Key ROS topics**: `/joint_states`, `/joint_trajectory`, `/tf`, `/move_group/goal`.
- **Always convert degrees to radians** when computing trajectory coefficients; examiners penalize mixed units.
- **MoveIt's default planner**: OMPL (RRT-Connect) for sampling-based planning; Cartesian planner for straight-line paths.
- **Collision checking** in MoveIt uses FCL (Flexible Collision Library) and is run **before** execution — a valid plan means *no self-collision* and *no environment collision*.
- **Common lab setup**: `roslaunch pkg_name demo.launch` (single command starts everything in simulation with RViz).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Move the 3DOF arm to a desired goal point
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL607 - ROS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Move the 3DOF arm to a desired goal point

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In KTU 2024 Scheme Robotics & ROS terminology, the experiment **"Move the 3DOF arm to a desired goal point"** refers to the closed-loop **inverse-kinematic point-to-point motion planning** problem, where a **3 Degrees-Of-Freedom (3DOF)** robotic manipulator must compute a valid set of joint angles $(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3)$ such that its end-effector pose (position in 3D Cartesian space) coincides exactly with a user-defined **goal point** $\mathbf{p}_g = (x_g, y_g, z_g)$.

The motion task is decomposed into three sequential engineering sub-problems:

1. **Forward Kinematics (FK):** Mapping joint space $\mathbf{q} \rightarrow$ Cartesian space $\mathbf{x}$ via the transformation matrix ${}^{0}\mathbf{T}_{3}(\theta_1,\theta_2,\theta_3)$.
2. **Inverse Kinematics (IK):** Mapping the goal $\mathbf{x}_g \rightarrow$ joint space solution $\mathbf{q}^*$.
3. **Trajectory Generation:** Interpolating joint angles from current $\mathbf{q}_c$ to goal $\mathbf{q}^*$ using smooth functions (linear, cubic, or quintic polynomials).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCBRL607 - Module 2):**
> The student must be able to **derive** the FK from **Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) parameters**, **solve** the IK analytically, and **validate** the motion using an ROS-compatible simulator (Gazebo / RViz) or MATLAB Robotics Toolbox.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Arm Reaching for a Cup"

Imagine you are sitting at a desk and want to pick up a coffee cup placed on the table at a specific coordinate.

| Human Arm Action | 3DOF Robotic Arm Equivalent |
|------------------|------------------------------|
| Your **shoulder** rotates left/right | **Joint 1 (Base yaw)** $\theta_1$ |
| Your **shoulder** lifts the arm up/down | **Joint 2 (Shoulder pitch)** $\theta_2$ |
| Your **elbow** straightens/bends | **Joint 3 (Elbow pitch)** $\theta_3$ |
| You visually locate the cup | **Goal point** $(x_g, y_g, z_g)$ |
| Your brain commands muscles to angles | **Inverse Kinematics solver** |
| You smoothly extend your arm | **Joint-space trajectory generator** |

Your brain does **not** calculate raw angles — it instead solves an *inverse problem* in fractions of a second. The 3DOF arm must be programmed to perform this same inverse computation deterministically using algebraic geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** in a serial manipulator equals the number of *independent* joints. A 3DOF arm in **3D space** can therefore position its end-effector anywhere on a 2D sphere-like manifold, but **cannot** control orientation independently — only position $(x, y, z)$.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

* **Link lengths:** $L_1$ (base height), $L_2$ (upper arm), $L_3$ (forearm) — typically **measured in meters (m)** or **millimeters (mm)**.
* **Joint limits:** $-\pi \le \theta_i \le \pi$ (radians).
* **Workspace reachability:** $r_{\max} = L_2 + L_3$ (maximum radial reach).
* **Singularity distance:** $\vert L_2 - L_3 \vert \le r \le L_2 + L_3$ for valid elbow-up/down configurations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Reference 3DOF Manipulator Geometry

We adopt the **RRR (Revolute-Revolute-Revolute)** planar-elbow configuration, which is the canonical test-bed used in KTU lab manuals.

* **Joint 1:** Rotates about base $z$-axis → moves the arm in the horizontal plane.
* **Joint 2:** Rotates about a shoulder axis parallel to $y$ → lifts the arm vertically.
* **Joint 3:** Rotates about an elbow axis parallel to $y$ → extends/retracts the forearm.

```
         z │
           │  L1 (vertical column)
           │┌───┐
           ││ θ2│
           │└─┬─┘
             │
             │ L2 (upper arm)
             │
             ●─────► y
            ╱ θ3
          ╱
        ╱ L3 (forearm)
      ●
    End-Effector (x,y,z)
```

### 2.2 Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) Parameter Table

For the chosen geometry, the standard DH parameters are:

| Joint $i$ | $\theta_i$ | $d_i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ |
|-----------|------------|-------|-------|------------|
| 1 | $\theta_1$ | $L_1$ | $0$ | $\pi/2$ |
| 2 | $\theta_2$ | $0$ | $L_2$ | $0$ |
| 3 | $\theta_3$ | $0$ | $L_3$ | $0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\alpha_1 = \pi/2$?** Because Joint 2's axis of rotation is **perpendicular** to Joint 1's axis. The twist angle $\alpha$ captures this 90° offset, which is the geometric reason the arm can fold vertically.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL — Read Carefully:**
> Throughout the markdown table below, the vertical bar `|` has been replaced with `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown parsing. When handwriting answers in the exam, students naturally write $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|---------------|
| 1 | ${}^{0}\mathbf{T}_{3} = {}^{0}\mathbf{T}_{1}\,{}^{1}\mathbf{T}_{2}\,{}^{2}\mathbf{T}_{3}$ | Composite FK transform |
| 2 | $\begin{bmatrix} c\theta & -s\theta\,c\alpha & s\theta\,s\alpha & a\,c\theta \\ s\theta & c\theta\,c\alpha & -c\theta\,s\alpha & a\,s\theta \\ 0 & s\alpha & c\alpha & d \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Standard DH transform ${}^{i-1}\mathbf{T}_i$ |
| 3 | $x = (L_2\,c\theta_2 + L_3\,c(\theta_2+\theta_3))\,c\theta_1$ | End-effector $x$-coordinate |
| 4 | $y = (L_2\,c\theta_2 + L_3\,c(\theta_2+\theta_3))\,s\theta_1$ | End-effector $y$-coordinate |
| 5 | $z = L_1 + L_2\,s\theta_2 + L_3\,s(\theta_2+\theta_3)$ | End-effector $z$-coordinate |
| 6 | $\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(y_g,\, x_g)$ | Base yaw from goal projection on XY |
| 7 | $r = \sqrt{x_g^2 + y_g^2}$ | Radial distance in XY plane |
| 8 | $D = \dfrac{r^2 + (z_g-L_1)^2 - L_2^2 - L_3^2}{2\,L_2\,L_3}$ | Cosine law intermediate |
| 9 | $\theta_3 = \text{atan2}(\pm\sqrt{1-D^2},\, D)$ | Elbow angle (2 solutions) |
| 10 | $\theta_2 = \text{atan2}(z_g-L_1,\, r) - \text{atan2}(L_3 s\theta_3,\, L_2+L_3 c\theta_3)$ | Shoulder pitch |
| 11 | $r_{\max} = L_2 + L_3$ | Maximum reachable radius |
| 12 | $\tau = t_f - t_0$ | Total motion duration |
| 13 | $\theta_i(t) = \theta_{i,c} + (\theta_{i,g}-\theta_{i,c})\left[3\left(\tfrac{t-t_0}{\tau}\right)^2 - 2\left(\tfrac{t-t_0}{\tau}\right)^3\right]$ | Cubic trajectory interpolation |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

* **Industrial pick-and-place robots** (FANUC LR-Mate, ABB IRB) — every cup placed on a conveyor uses this exact IK math at $\approx 100$ Hz.
* **Surgical manipulators** (da Vinci) — pre-computed IK ensures the tool tip reaches the incision port while avoiding tissue.
* **Space robotics** (Canadarm on ISS) — IK is solved in real-time for grappling free-flying satellites.
* **Humanoid robots** (ASIMO, Atlas) — IK is the inner loop of every walking step.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Forward Kinematics (FK) from DH Parameters

We derive the end-effector position formulas step by step.

#### Step 1 — Individual link transformations

Using the standard DH row from the cheat sheet, each link's homogeneous transform is constructed.

For **Link 1** ($\alpha_1 = \pi/2$, so $c\alpha = 0$, $s\alpha = 1$):

$$
{}^{0}\mathbf{T}_{1} =
\begin{bmatrix}
c\theta_1 & 0 & s\theta_1 & 0 \\
s\theta_1 & 0 & -c\theta_1 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0 & L_1 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

For **Link 2** ($\alpha_2 = 0$, so $c\alpha = 1$, $s\alpha = 0$):

$$
{}^{1}\mathbf{T}_{2} =
\begin{bmatrix}
c\theta_2 & -s\theta_2 & 0 & L_2\,c\theta_2 \\
s\theta_2 & c\theta_2 & 0 & L_2\,s\theta_2 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

For **Link 3** ($\alpha_3 = 0$):

$$
{}^{2}\mathbf{T}_{3} =
\begin{bmatrix}
c\theta_3 & -s\theta_3 & 0 & L_3\,c\theta_3 \\
s\theta_3 & c\theta_3 & 0 & L_3\,s\theta_3 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Logic Note:** Notice that $d_1 = L_1$ produces the constant vertical offset in the $z$-column, while $a_2 = L_2$ and $a_3 = L_3$ produce the radial link extensions in the $x$ and $y$ columns.

#### Step 2 — Compose ${}^{1}\mathbf{T}_{2}$ and ${}^{2}\mathbf{T}_{3}$

We first compute the wrist-frame transform ${}^{1}\mathbf{T}_{3} = {}^{1}\mathbf{T}_{2}\cdot{}^{2}\mathbf{T}_{3}$.

Multiplying the upper-left $3\times 3$ rotation blocks:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{23} &=
\begin{bmatrix}
c\theta_2\,c\theta_3 - s\theta_2\,s\theta_3 & -(c\theta_2\,s\theta_3 + s\theta_2\,c\theta_3) & 0 \\
s\theta_2\,c\theta_3 + c\theta_2\,s\theta_3 & c\theta_2\,c\theta_3 - s\theta_2\,s\theta_3 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} c(\theta_2+\theta_3) & -s(\theta_2+\theta_3) & 0 \\ s(\theta_2+\theta_3) & c(\theta_2+\theta_3) & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying the position columns:

$$
\mathbf{p}_{23} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
L_2\,c\theta_2 + L_3\,c(\theta_2+\theta_3) \\
L_2\,s\theta_2 + L_3\,s(\theta_2+\theta_3) \\
0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Logic Note:** The $c(\theta_2+\theta_3)$ and $s(\theta_2+\theta_3)$ identities are the trigonometric sum rules: $\cos(A+B) = \cos A\cos B - \sin A\sin B$ and $\sin(A+B) = \sin A\cos B + \cos A\sin B$. These simplify the wrist pose dramatically.

#### Step 3 — Compose with ${}^{0}\mathbf{T}_{1}$ to obtain ${}^{0}\mathbf{T}_{3}$

The final end-effector position in the base frame is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= (L_2\,c\theta_2 + L_3\,c(\theta_2+\theta_3))\,c\theta_1 \\
y &= (L_2\,c\theta_2 + L_3\,c(\theta_2+\theta_3))\,s\theta_1 \\
z &= L_1 + L_2\,s\theta_2 + L_3\,s(\theta_2+\theta_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Note:** The factor $(L_2\,c\theta_2 + L_3\,c(\theta_2+\theta_3))$ is the **planar reach** $r_{xz}$, which is then rotated into the XY plane by multiplying by $\cos\theta_1$ (x-component) and $\sin\theta_1$ (y-component). This decouples the problem into a *vertical plane sub-problem* (controlled by $\theta_2, \theta_3$) and a *horizontal rotation* (controlled by $\theta_1$).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Inverse Kinematics (IK)

Given the goal $\mathbf{p}_g = (x_g, y_g, z_g)$, we must solve the three non-linear equations derived above for $(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3)$.

#### Step 1 — Solve for $\theta_1$ (Base yaw)

Divide the $x$-equation by the $y$-equation:

$$
\frac{y_g}{x_g} = \frac{\sin\theta_1}{\cos\theta_1} = \tan\theta_1
$$

Using the two-argument arctangent (which handles all four quadrants correctly):

$$
\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(y_g,\, x_g)
$$

> **Logic Note:** $\text{atan2}$ is **mandatory** in robotics — using $\arctan(y_g/x_g)$ loses sign information and fails when $x_g = 0$. This is a 1-mark trap in the KTU exam.

#### Step 2 — Project the goal onto the vertical plane

Compute the radial distance:

$$
r = \sqrt{x_g^2 + y_g^2}
$$

The vertical offset from the base height:

$$
z' = z_g - L_1
$$

The 3D problem is now reduced to a **2D problem in the r-z' plane**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= L_2\,c\theta_2 + L_3\,c(\theta_2+\theta_3) \\
z' &= L_2\,s\theta_2 + L_3\,s(\theta_2+\theta_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Solve for $\theta_3$ via Law of Cosines

The distance squared from shoulder to goal in the r-z' plane is $r^2 + z'^2$. By the law of cosines on the triangle formed by links $L_2$, $L_3$, and the goal vector:

$$
r^2 + z'^2 = L_2^2 + L_3^2 + 2\,L_2\,L_3\,c\theta_3
$$

Solving for $c\theta_3$:

$$
c\theta_3 = D = \frac{r^2 + z'^2 - L_2^2 - L_3^2}{2\,L_2\,L_3}
$$

The two-argument arctangent gives both valid configurations:

$$
\theta_3 = \text{atan2}\left(\pm\sqrt{1 - D^2},\; D\right)
$$

> **Logic Note:** The $\pm$ corresponds to **elbow-up** ($+\sqrt{1-D^2}$) and **elbow-down** ($-\sqrt{1-D^2}$). KTU exam expects you to *mention* both configurations exist. The choice depends on obstacles or joint-limit constraints.

The **reachability condition** is:

$$
-1 \le D \le 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \vert L_2 - L_3 \vert \le \sqrt{r^2 + z'^2} \le L_2 + L_3
$$

#### Step 4 — Solve for $\theta_2$ using auxiliary angle

Let $k_1 = L_2 + L_3\,c\theta_3$ and $k_2 = L_3\,s\theta_3$. Then the 2D equations become:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= k_1\,c\theta_2 - k_2\,s\theta_2 \\
z' &= k_2\,c\theta_2 + k_1\,s\theta_2
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a rotation matrix acting on $(\cos\theta_2, \sin\theta_2)$, so:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} r \\ z' \end{bmatrix} = 
\begin{bmatrix} k_1 & -k_2 \\ k_2 & k_1 \end{bmatrix} 
\begin{bmatrix} c\theta_2 \\ s\theta_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Inverting the matrix (its determinant is $k_1^2 + k_2^2 = L_2^2 + L_3^2 + 2L_2 L_3 c\theta_3$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
c\theta_2 &= \frac{k_1\,r + k_2\,z'}{k_1^2 + k_2^2} \\
s\theta_2 &= \frac{-k_2\,r + k_1\,z'}{k_1^2 + k_2^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\theta_2 = \text{atan2}(z',\, r) - \text{atan2}(L_3\,s\theta_3,\, L_2 + L_3\,c\theta_3)
$$

> **Logic Note:** The first term $\text{atan2}(z',r)$ is the *direction* from base to goal; the second term is the *correction* for the elbow's offset. This elegant decomposition is a high-yield formula for the KTU 14-mark question.

---

### 3.3 Trajectory Generation Between Current and Goal Joint Angles

Once $(\theta_1^*, \theta_2^*, \theta_3^*)$ are computed, we cannot teleport the arm — it must move smoothly. The **cubic polynomial** trajectory for joint $i$ is:

$$
\theta_i(t) = \theta_{i,c} + (\theta_{i,g} - \theta_{i,c}) \left[ 3 s^2 - 2 s^3 \right], \quad s = \frac{t - t_0}{t_f - t_0}
$$

with boundary conditions $\theta_i(t_0) = \theta_{i,c}$ and $\theta_i(t_f) = \theta_{i,g}$, and zero start/end velocities. For non-zero start/end velocities, a **quintic polynomial** is used.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Moving the 3DOF Arm to a Goal

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User defines Goal Point px,py,pz] --> B[Workspace Reachability Check]
    B -- Reachable --> C[Compute theta1 via atan2 yg,xg]
    B -- Out of Range --> Z[Reject Goal: Singularity Flag]
    C --> D[Project Goal to rz plane]
    D --> E[Compute D via Cosine Law]
    E --> F[Compute theta3 with plus or minus sqrt]
    F --> G[Compute theta2 via Auxiliary Angle]
    G --> H[Set Joint Angles q star]
    H --> I[Trajectory Generator: Cubic Spline]
    I --> J[Publish Joint Commands to ROS Topic]
    J --> K[Gazebo Simulator or Real 3DOF Arm]
    K --> L[Encoder Feedback Current q]
    L --> M[Controller Compares qc vs q star]
    M -- Error --> N[PID Output: Corrective Torque]
    N --> K
    M -- Within Threshold --> P[Goal Reached Flag]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> The flow is a **closed-loop control pipeline**. The forward direction (top-to-bottom) handles *motion planning*, while the upward arrow (M → N → K) represents the *feedback control loop* that corrects tracking errors in real time.

### 4.2 Coordinate Frame Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F0[Frame 0: Base] -- T01 --> F1[Frame 1: Shoulder]
    F1 -- T12 --> F2[Frame 2: Elbow]
    F2 -- T23 --> F3[Frame 3: End Effector]
    F3 -- Extract px,py,pz --> GOAL[Goal Point in Cartesian Space]
```

> **Logic Note:** Each $T_{ij}$ is a $4\times 4$ homogeneous transform from the previous DH parameter row. Their chain product $T_{03} = T_{01}\cdot T_{12}\cdot T_{23}$ is the forward-kinematic map.

### 4.3 IK Solution Tree (Multiple Configurations)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G[Goal pxg,pyg,pzg] --> Q1{theta1 unique}
    Q1 --> T1A[theta1 = atan2 yg,xg]
    Q1 --> T1B[theta1 = theta1 plus pi Flip back]
    T1A --> Q2{theta3 sign choice}
    Q2 --> T3P[theta3 up: Elbow Up]
    Q2 --> T3M[theta3 down: Elbow Down]
    T3P --> T2A[theta2 computed]
    T3M --> T2B[theta2 computed]
    T1B --> Q3{theta3 sign choice}
    Q3 --> T3P2[theta3 up]
    Q3 --> T3M2[theta3 down]
    T3P2 --> T2C[theta2 computed]
    T3M2 --> T2D[theta2 computed]
```

> **Logic Note:** For a non-redundant 3DOF arm, there are up to **4 valid IK solutions** per reachable goal. The selection logic (closest to current joint angles, avoiding joint limits, avoiding obstacles) is a research topic in itself.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Define Inverse Kinematics. Why is the IK problem considered harder than the Forward Kinematics problem for a serial manipulator?**

**Model Answer:**
Inverse Kinematics (IK) is the process of computing the joint variables $(\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n)$ required for the end-effector to reach a desired Cartesian pose $(x, y, z)$. It is the **inverse** of Forward Kinematics (FK), which maps joint angles to Cartesian position.

IK is harder than FK because:
1. The FK equations are **non-linear** trigonometric functions, and inverting them analytically may be **impossible** or yield **multiple solutions**.
2. There may be **zero, one, or infinitely many** IK solutions for the same goal (singularities, redundancy).
3. A goal may be **outside the workspace**, requiring a reachability check.
4. The solution depends on **joint-limit constraints**, which makes it a constrained non-linear problem.

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the four Denavit-Hartenberg parameters and state the geometric meaning of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Geometric Meaning |
|-----------|--------|-------------------|
| Link length | $a_i$ | Distance along $x_i$ from $z_i$ to $z_{i+1}$ |
| Link twist | $\alpha_i$ | Angle about $x_i$ from $z_i$ to $z_{i+1}$ |
| Link offset | $d_i$ | Distance along $z_i$ from $x_{i-1}$ to $x_i$ |
| Joint angle | $\theta_i$ | Angle about $z_i$ from $x_{i-1}$ to $x_i$ |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the forward kinematics expressions for the end-effector position $(x, y, z)$ of a 3DOF RRR manipulator whose DH parameters are given below. **(7 marks)**

| Joint $i$ | $\theta_i$ | $d_i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ |
|-----------|------------|-------|-------|------------|
| 1 | $\theta_1$ | $L_1$ | $0$ | $\pi/2$ |
| 2 | $\theta_2$ | $0$ | $L_2$ | $0$ |
| 3 | $\theta_3$ | $0$ | $L_3$ | $0$ |

**(b)** Given the goal point $\mathbf{p}_g = (0.4,\, 0.3,\, 0.5)$ m and link lengths $L_1 = 0.2$ m, $L_2 = 0.3$ m, $L_3 = 0.2$ m, compute the joint angles $(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3)$ required to reach the goal. Use the elbow-up configuration. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to **Section 3.1, Steps 1–3** of these notes. Final answer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= (L_2\,c\theta_2 + L_3\,c(\theta_2+\theta_3))\,c\theta_1 \\
y &= (L_2\,c\theta_2 + L_3\,c(\theta_2+\theta_3))\,s\theta_1 \\
z &= L_1 + L_2\,s\theta_2 + L_3\,s(\theta_2+\theta_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** Compute step-by-step:

**Step 1 — Base yaw:**

$$
\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(0.3,\, 0.4) = 0.6435 \text{ rad} = 36.87^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — Radial projection:**

$$
r = \sqrt{0.4^2 + 0.3^2} = \sqrt{0.25} = 0.5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
z' = z_g - L_1 = 0.5 - 0.2 = 0.3 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Cosine law intermediate:**

$$
D = \frac{r^2 + z'^2 - L_2^2 - L_3^2}{2\,L_2\,L_3} = \frac{0.25 + 0.09 - 0.09 - 0.04}{2(0.3)(0.2)} = \frac{0.21}{0.12} = 1.75
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **D > 1 means the goal is UNREACHABLE!** The given goal lies outside the workspace since $D$ should lie in $[-1, 1]$ for a real $\theta_3$ to exist. The KTU examiner will likely award 0 marks if you silently compute $\sqrt{1 - 1.75^2}$ without acknowledging the singularity. **Always state reachability check first.**

For demonstration, let us **revise the goal** to a reachable point, e.g., $\mathbf{p}_g = (0.35,\, 0.0,\, 0.45)$ m (which makes $\theta_1 = 0$).

Re-compute: $r = 0.35$, $z' = 0.25$.

$$
D = \frac{0.1225 + 0.0625 - 0.09 - 0.04}{0.12} = \frac{0.055}{0.12} = 0.4583
$$

**Step 4 — Elbow angle (up configuration):**

$$
\theta_3 = \text{atan2}(+\sqrt{1 - 0.4583^2},\, 0.4583) = \text{atan2}(0.8887,\, 0.4583) = 1.093 \text{ rad} = 62.6^\circ
$$

**Step 5 — Shoulder angle:**

$$
\theta_2 = \text{atan2}(0.25,\, 0.35) - \text{atan2}(0.2 \sin 62.6^\circ,\, 0.3 + 0.2\cos 62.6^\circ)
$$

$$
= 0.6202 - \text{atan2}(0.1777,\, 0.3917) = 0.6202 - 0.4246 = 0.1956 \text{ rad} = 11.21^\circ
$$

**Final Joint Angles (elbow-up):**

$$
\boxed{\theta_1 = 0^\circ, \quad \theta_2 = 11.21^\circ, \quad \theta_3 = 62.6^\circ}
$$

**Verification (FK substitution):**
$x = (0.3 \cos 11.21^\circ + 0.2 \cos 73.81^\circ)\cdot 1 = (0.2943 + 0.0557) = 0.35$ m ✓
$z = 0.2 + 0.3 \sin 11.21^\circ + 0.2 \sin 73.81^\circ = 0.2 + 0.0583 + 0.1923 = 0.4506$ m ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **workspace reachability** for a 3DOF arm. How is the maximum and minimum reachable radius determined? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a flowchart, describe the **algorithm** to move a 3DOF arm from its current configuration to a user-specified goal point using inverse kinematics and cubic-polynomial trajectory generation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** **Workspace Reachability:**
The workspace of a 3DOF RRR manipulator is the set of all points in 3D space that the end-effector can physically reach.

* **Maximum reachable radius** (arm fully extended):
$$
r_{\max} = L_2 + L_3
$$
* **Minimum reachable radius** (arm fully folded):
$$
r_{\min} = \vert L_2 - L_3 \vert
$$

For any goal point $\mathbf{p}_g = (x_g, y_g, z_g)$, the distance from the shoulder to the goal must satisfy:

$$
r_{\min}^2 \le (x_g^2 + y_g^2) + (z_g - L_1)^2 \le r_{\max}^2
$$

If this inequality is violated, the goal is **unreachable** and the IK has no real solution.

* **Inner sphere of unreachable points:** Radius $\vert L_2 - L_3 \vert$ around the shoulder — the elbow cannot fold tightly enough.
* **Outer sphere of unreachable points:** Radius $L_2 + L_3$ — the arm cannot extend further.

**(b)** **Algorithm Flowchart:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B[Read Goal pxg,pyg,pzg from User]
    B --> C[Compute r and z prime]
    C --> D{Reachability Check}
    D -- Out of Range --> E[Display Error and Stop]
    D -- OK --> F[Compute theta1]
    F --> G[Compute D for Elbow]
    G --> H[Compute theta3 plus and minus]
    H --> I[Select Elbow Up Configuration]
    I --> J[Compute theta2 via Auxiliary Angle]
    J --> K[Set q star]
    K --> L[Initialize Cubic Spline Interpolator]
    L --> M[Loop: At each time step compute theta of t]
    M --> N[Publish to ROS Topic]
    N --> O{Goal Reached at tf}
    O -- No --> M
    O -- Yes --> P[Stop Motors and Exit]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the reachability check** loses 2 marks even if IK is otherwise correct.
> 2. **Using `atan2` vs `atan`** — the wrong function fails at $x = 0$ and loses quadrant information.
> 3. **Not validating the solution** by substituting back into FK — examiners award 1 bonus mark for verification.
> 4. **Ignoring joint limits** — if $\theta_i$ computed falls outside $[-\pi, \pi]$, it must be wrapped or rejected.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **3DOF arm** = 3 revolute joints (RRR) controlling **position only**, not orientation.
- **DH parameters** uniquely define the geometry; remember: $\alpha_1 = \pi/2$ for vertical folding.
- **Forward Kinematics** chain rule: ${}^{0}\mathbf{T}_{n} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} {}^{i-1}\mathbf{T}_i(\theta_i, d_i, a_i, \alpha_i)$.
- **End-effector position** decouples into a base-yaw rotation $\theta_1$ and a 2D planar IK in the $(r, z')$ plane.
- **Base yaw** is always $\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(y_g, x_g)$ — never use plain $\arctan$.
- **Elbow angle** comes from the **Law of Cosines**, $D = \frac{r^2+z'^2-L_2^2-L_3^2}{2L_2L_3}$; check $\vert D \vert \le 1$.
- **Two IK solutions** exist per configuration: **elbow-up** ($+\sqrt{1-D^2}$) and **elbow-down** ($-\sqrt{1-D^2}$).
- **Workspace bounds:** $\vert L_2 - L_3 \vert \le \sqrt{r^2+z'^2} \le L_2 + L_3$.
- **Trajectory** must use cubic (or quintic) polynomial to avoid jerk; linear interpolation is jerky and unrealistic.
- **Closed-loop feedback** is essential — open-loop IK is brittle under load, friction, or modeling errors.
- **In ROS**, joint commands are published to `/joint_trajectory_controller/command` and visualized in **RViz** using the **TF** tree.
- **Always verify** your IK answer by plugging $(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3)$ back into FK and confirming $(x, y, z)$ matches the goal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Attach 2DOF gripper as the end effector of 3DOF arm and execute gripping operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL607 - ROS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Attach 2DOF gripper as the end effector of 3DOF arm and execute gripping operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**End Effector Attachment** in ROS (Robot Operating System) refers to the process of logically and physically integrating a peripheral manipulation device—in this case, a **2 Degrees of Freedom (2DOF) Gripper**—as the terminal link of a serial robotic manipulator chain (a **3DOF arm**), such that the combined system becomes a single, coherent robot described in a Unified Robot Description Format (URDF) or SDF file, and operates under a unified ROS control graph.

A **2DOF Gripper** in the KTU 2024 ROS syllabus context is typically a parallel-jaw mechanism actuated by:
- **One prismatic joint** (linear open/close motion of the jaws), OR
- **Two coupled revolute joints** (rotational finger pivoting, mirrored symmetrically)

A **3DOF Arm** is a serial manipulator with three independently actuated joints (commonly base yaw + shoulder pitch + elbow pitch), providing full spatial positioning of its tip in 3D space (x, y, z) but without independent orientation control (roll/pitch/yaw of the tool).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition — "End Effector":** The device at the end of a robotic arm designed to interact with the environment. In this experiment, the gripper is the end effector, attached to the last link of the 3DOF arm using a *fixed* URDF joint (for static mounting) plus its own actuated joints.

> [!NOTE]
> **Total DOF after attachment:** The combined manipulator-gripper system exhibits **5 DOF** (3 from the arm + 2 from the gripper). The arm provides *positioning* (3 translational); the gripper provides *grasping aperture* (1) and *in-hand manipulation/rotation* (1).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine your own **human arm**:

- Your **shoulder, elbow, and wrist** act like the 3DOF arm (3 actuated joints that move your hand through space).
- Your **fingers** (thumb + index, opening and closing) act like the 2DOF gripper.

When you pick up a pen from a table, your **arm positions** your hand over the pen, and your **fingers grip** it. The "attachment" in this analogy is your **wrist joint**—the structural and logical bridge that allows the hand to follow wherever the arm goes.

In ROS, this "wrist joint" is implemented as a **URDF `<joint type="fixed">`** between the arm's last link and the gripper's base link, combined with a **TF (transform) tree** where every frame (link) has a parent.

**Real-world parallel:** Industrial pick-and-place robots in factories (e.g., FANUC LR-Mate, ABB IRB) always have a gripper mounted on the arm's end flange. ROS lets us simulate this exact architecture before deploying on real hardware.

## 1.3 Key Physical & System Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Number of independent movements a mechanism can perform. Arm: **3**, Gripper: **2**, Combined: **5**.
> - **URDF (Unified Robot Description Format):** XML-based file format used in ROS to describe a robot's kinematic, dynamic, and visual properties.
> - **TF (Transform) Library:** ROS library maintaining the time-varying relationship between coordinate frames in a tree structure.
> - **ROS Control:** Hardware-abstracted controller framework using `controller_manager`, `joint_state_controller`, and position/velocity/effort controllers.
> - **Gazebo:** 3D physics-based simulator tightly integrated with ROS for testing robot behaviors.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The workflow of attaching a 2DOF gripper to a 3DOF arm and executing gripping operations decomposes into **5 logical stages**:

### Stage 1 — Robot Description (URDF Authoring)
- The original URDF contains the 3DOF arm: `base_link → shoulder_pan → shoulder_lift → elbow → wrist_link`.
- A new **gripper sub-assembly** is appended: `gripper_base → left_finger`, `gripper_base → right_finger`.
- A `<joint name="arm_to_gripper" type="fixed">` rigidly mounts the gripper's base to the arm's terminal link (`wrist_link`).
- Each finger is actuated by a `<joint type="revolute">` (or `prismatic`) with defined `<limit>` tags.

### Stage 2 — TF Tree Construction
- The TF tree is automatically built from the URDF parent-child link relationships.
- `robot_state_publisher` node subscribes to `/joint_states` and broadcasts the transforms.
- Each gripper finger's pose is published at the configured `update_rate` (default **30 Hz**).

### Stage 3 — Controller Configuration
- `controller_manager` loads the YAML config defining:
  - `arm_position_controller` (position mode for the 3 arm joints)
  - `gripper_controller` (position mode for the 2 gripper joints)
  - `joint_state_controller` (publishes `/joint_states`)
- `ros_control` transmits commands to Gazebo's `gazebo_ros_control` plugin (simulation) or to real servos (hardware).

### Stage 4 — Gripping Operation Execution
- A **client node** (Python/C++) publishes joint trajectory goals to move the arm to a pre-grasp pose.
- The gripper fingers are commanded to an *open* position (e.g., `θ_gripper = 0.0 rad`).
- After reaching the object, fingers close to a *closed* position (e.g., `θ_gripper = 0.5 rad`).
- The arm lifts the object, transports it, and releases by opening the gripper.

### Stage 5 — Visualization & Verification
- **RViz** displays the robot model, TF frames, and the planned/executed trajectory.
- **Gazebo** simulates physics: contact forces, friction, gravity on the grasped object.
- **rqt_graph** visualizes the ROS node and topic connectivity.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' behind the fixed joint + actuated fingers design:** A *fixed* parent joint is the cleanest way to represent a *static mechanical coupling* (bolted flange) without adding a virtual DOF that would complicate kinematics. The actuated child joints carry the gripping DOFs.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are essential for KTU 2024 ESE problems on this experiment. Memorize the **form**, the **variables**, and the **units**.

### Table 1: Kinematic, Geometric, and Control Equations

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | End-Effector Position (3DOF Arm) | $P_{ee} = f(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3)$ | $P_{ee}$: 3D position vector (m) |
| 2 | x-coordinate | $x = (L_1 \cos\theta_2 + L_2 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3))\cos\theta_1$ | $L_1, L_2$: link lengths (m); $\theta_i$: joint angle (rad) |
| 3 | y-coordinate | $y = (L_1 \cos\theta_2 + L_2 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3))\sin\theta_1$ | $\theta_1$: base rotation |
| 4 | z-coordinate | $z = L_0 + L_1 \sin\theta_2 + L_2 \sin(\theta_2 + \theta_3)$ | $L_0$: base height (m) |
| 5 | Gripper Aperture (parallel jaws) | $d = 2 \, r \, \sin(\alpha \div 2)$ | $d$: opening width (m); $r$: finger length (m); $\alpha$: finger angle (rad) |
| 6 | Grip Force on Object | $F = 2 \, F_f \cos(\alpha \div 2)$ | $F$: net gripping force (N); $F_f$: per-finger force (N) |
| 7 | Static Friction Condition (no-slip grasp) | $\mu \, F \geq m \, g$ | $\mu$: friction coeff.; $m$: object mass (kg); $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| 8 | Homogeneous Transform Composition | $T_{world}^{gripper} = T_{world}^{base} \cdot T_{base}^{s1} \cdot T_{s1}^{s2} \cdot T_{s2}^{elbow} \cdot T_{elbow}^{wrist} \cdot T_{wrist}^{gripper}$ | Each $T_i \in SE(3)$ |
| 9 | Standard SE(3) Matrix | $T = \begin{bmatrix} R & p \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad R \in SO(3), \ p \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ | $R$: rotation; $p$: translation |
| 10 | PID Control Law | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $K_p, K_i, K_d$: PID gains; $e$: error |
| 11 | Joint Velocity Bound (ros_control) | $\dot{q}_{min} \leq \dot{q} \leq \dot{q}_{max}$ | rad/s for revolute; m/s for prismatic |
| 12 | Workspace Volume (spherical approx.) | $V \approx \dfrac{4}{3} \pi \, L_{reach}^{3}$ | $L_{reach} = L_1 + L_2$ (m) |

> [!NOTE]
> In Table 1, all `\div` and `\cdot` symbols are LaTeX-safe—**no vertical pipe characters** are used inside table cells, preserving markdown rendering.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Automation:** Pick-and-place of components on PCBs, packaging lines, bin-picking.
- **Medical Robotics:** Surgical tool changers where the gripper is a different end effector for each procedure.
- **Space Robotics:** Canadarm on the ISS uses an "end effector" that latches onto grapple fixtures on payloads.
- **Service Robotics:** Domestic robots (e.g., grasping cups, opening doors) use the same arm+gripper architecture.
- **Research & Education:** This exact 3DOF+2DOF configuration is a standard benchmark in academic labs for testing new grasp planning algorithms.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Forward Kinematics of the 3DOF Arm

We derive the position of the **wrist link** (the mounting point of the 2DOF gripper) using the standard Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) convention.

### 3.1.1 DH Parameter Table

| Joint $i$ | $a_i$ (link length) | $\alpha_i$ (twist) | $d_i$ (offset) | $\theta_i$ (variable) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (base yaw) | $0$ | $\pi \div 2$ | $L_0$ | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 (shoulder) | $L_1$ | $0$ | $0$ | $\theta_2$ |
| 3 (elbow) | $L_2$ | $0$ | $0$ | $\theta_3$ |

> [!NOTE]
> $L_0$ is the vertical offset from the ground plane to the shoulder joint axis. $L_1, L_2$ are the upper-arm and forearm lengths.

### 3.1.2 Individual Homogeneous Transformations

For a revolute joint, the standard DH transformation is:

$$
T_i^{i-1} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \cos\theta_i \\
\sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \sin\theta_i \\
0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Transformation for Joint 1** (substitute $\alpha_1 = \pi/2$, $a_1 = 0$, $d_1 = L_0$):

$$
T_1^{0} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & 0 & \sin\theta_1 & 0 \\
\sin\theta_1 & 0 & -\cos\theta_1 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0 & L_0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Transformation for Joint 2** (substitute $\alpha_2 = 0$, $a_2 = L_1$, $d_2 = 0$):

$$
T_2^{1} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & L_1 \cos\theta_2 \\
\sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 & L_1 \sin\theta_2 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Transformation for Joint 3** (substitute $\alpha_3 = 0$, $a_3 = L_2$, $d_3 = 0$):

$$
T_3^{2} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_3 & -\sin\theta_3 & 0 & L_2 \cos\theta_3 \\
\sin\theta_3 & \cos\theta_3 & 0 & L_2 \sin\theta_3 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.1.3 Composing $T_3^0 = T_1^0 \cdot T_2^1 \cdot T_3^2$

Multiplying the position column of $T_3^2$ by $T_2^1$:

$$
p_2 = T_2^1 \cdot p_3 = \begin{bmatrix} L_1 \cos\theta_2 + L_2 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3) \\ L_1 \sin\theta_2 + L_2 \sin(\theta_2 + \theta_3) \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Then multiplying $T_1^0$ by $p_2$:

$$
p_1 = T_1^0 \cdot p_2 = \begin{bmatrix} (L_1 \cos\theta_2 + L_2 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3)) \cos\theta_1 \\ (L_1 \cos\theta_2 + L_2 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3)) \sin\theta_1 \\ L_0 + L_1 \sin\theta_2 + L_2 \sin(\theta_2 + \theta_3) \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **forward kinematics** result. The wrist position in the world frame is fully described by $(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3)$. This becomes the *base frame* for the 2DOF gripper attached at the wrist.

## 3.2 Derivation — Gripper Aperture as a Function of Joint Angle

A parallel-jaw gripper has two identical fingers pivoting about a common base. Each finger is a rigid link of length $r$ rotating by angle $\alpha$ from the closed (vertical) position.

**Aperture equation** (distance between inner finger tips):

$$
d(\alpha) = 2 \, r \, \sin\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)
$$

**Derivation steps:**

**Step 1:** Place the finger pivot at the origin. One finger tip is at position $(r \sin(\alpha/2), r \cos(\alpha/2))$; the other (mirrored) is at $(-r \sin(\alpha/2), r \cos(\alpha/2))$.

**Step 2:** Euclidean distance between the two tips:

$$
d = \sqrt{(2r \sin(\alpha/2))^2 + 0^2} = 2 r \sin(\alpha/2)
$$

**Step 3:** At $\alpha = 0$ (fully closed): $d = 0$. At $\alpha = \alpha_{max}$ (fully open): $d_{max} = 2 r \sin(\alpha_{max}/2)$.

> [!NOTE]
> For a URDF definition, the `<limit lower="0.0" upper="0.5"/>` tag on the gripper revolute joint typically corresponds to $\alpha \in [0, 0.5]$ rad, mapping to an aperture range of $[0, 2r \sin(0.25)]$ meters.

## 3.3 Derivation — Grasp Force & Friction Condition

**Why the fingers must apply minimum force:** A real grasp is held by *static friction* between the finger pads and the object surface. Without sufficient normal force, the object slips.

**Step 1:** Each finger exerts a normal force $F_f$ on the object. The friction force at each contact is $\mu F_f$ (Coulomb friction model).

**Step 2:** Two contacts contribute; total upward friction (supporting weight) is $2 \mu F_f$.

**Step 3:** Equilibrium (object does not slip):

$$
2 \mu F_f \geq m g
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the minimum per-finger force:

$$
F_{f,min} = \frac{m g}{2 \mu}
$$

**Step 5:** Required actuator torque $\tau$ at the finger pivot (link of length $r$):

$$
\tau_{min} = F_{f,min} \cdot r = \frac{m g \, r}{2 \mu}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why KTU lab experiments ask students to **vary object mass** and observe slip — it teaches the practical limit of grip force and motivates force-feedback control (force-sensitive resistors, tactile sensors).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Stages to ROS Components

| Stage | ROS Component | Topic/Service | File/Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| Robot Description | URDF/Xacro parser | `/robot_description` parameter | `robot.urdf.xacro` |
| TF Publishing | `robot_state_publisher` | `/joint_states` (sub) | Launch file |
| Joint State Aggregation | `joint_state_controller` | `/joint_states` (pub) | `controller_manager` |
| Arm Position Control | `position_controllers/JointTrajectoryController` | `/arm_controller/command` | YAML config |
| Gripper Control | `position_controllers/JointGroupPositionController` | `/gripper_controller/command` | YAML config |
| Physics Simulation | Gazebo + `gazebo_ros_control` | `/gazebo/model_states` | `.world` file |
| Trajectory Execution | `MoveIt!` (optional) | `/move_group` action | MoveIt config package |
| Visualization | RViz | TF, RobotModel, Marker | `.rviz` config |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Architecture — ROS Node-Topic Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[URDF Xacro File] --> B[robot_state_publisher]
    C[joint_state_controller] --> D[/joint_states topic/]
    D --> B
    B --> E[/tf and /tf_static topics/]
    E --> F[RViz Visualization]
    E --> G[Gazebo Simulator]
    G --> H[gazebo_ros_control Plugin]
    H --> I[arm_position_controller]
    H --> J[gripper_position_controller]
    K[User Client Node] --> L[/arm_controller/command/]
    L --> I
    K --> M[/gripper_controller/command/]
    M --> J
    I --> N[Arm Joints actuation]
    J --> O[Gripper Fingers actuation]
    N --> P[3DOF Arm movement]
    O --> Q[2DOF Gripper open/close]
    P --> R[Object grasping in Gazebo]
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Contact and friction forces]
    S --> T[Task success or slip detection]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram represents the **information flow** of the experiment. Arrows show topic publication/subscription, action goals, and physical actuation paths. Node IDs are alphanumeric and label-safe per Mermaid v10+ syntax.

## 4.2 Kinematic Chain — URDF Link-Joint Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    base[base_link] --> j1[Revolute joint 1: theta1]
    j1 --> shoulder[shoulder_link]
    shoulder --> j2[Revolute joint 2: theta2]
    j2 --> upperArm[upper_arm_link]
    upperArm --> j3[Revolute joint 3: theta3]
    j3 --> forearm[forearm_link]
    forearm --> wrist[wrist_link]
    wrist --> jfix[Fixed joint: arm_to_gripper]
    jfix --> gripBase[gripper_base_link]
    gripBase --> jg1[Revolute joint 4: alpha left]
    jg1 --> fingerL[left_finger_link]
    gripBase --> jg2[Revolute joint 5: alpha right]
    jg2 --> fingerR[right_finger_link]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **fixed joint** `jfix` is the critical attachment point—it establishes a rigid mechanical coupling without introducing a virtual DOF. Both finger joints (4 and 5) are actuated and constitute the 2DOF gripper.

## 4.3 State Machine — Gripping Operation Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Home
    Home --> PreGrasp: Move arm to approach pose
    PreGrasp --> GripperOpen: Open gripper fully
    GripperOpen --> ApproachObject: Lower arm to object
    ApproachObject --> CloseGripper: Actuate fingers
    CloseGripper --> GraspCheck: Force feedback check
    GraspCheck --> Lift: Grasp success
    GraspCheck --> Retry: Slip detected
    Retry --> CloseGripper
    Lift --> Transport: Move to target
    Transport --> Release: Open gripper
    Release --> Home: Return to home
    Home --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This finite state machine (FSM) is the **control logic backbone** typically implemented in a Python or C++ ROS node using `smach`, a simple state machine library, or a custom FSM class.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Why It Matters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Input / Script] --> B[Planning Layer]
    B --> C[Trajectory Generation]
    C --> D[Controller Manager]
    D --> E[Hardware Abstraction Layer]
    E --> F[Simulated Joints in Gazebo]
    E --> G[Real Servos on Hardware]
    F --> H[Physics Engine Feedback]
    H --> D
    G --> I[Encoder Feedback]
    I --> D
    D --> J[State Publisher]
    J --> K[TF Tree]
    K --> L[RViz Display]
    K --> M[MoveIt Planning Scene]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture** shows *how* abstraction layers in ROS decouple high-level planning from low-level actuation. This is a key conceptual diagram for KTU 2024 14-mark answers that ask "explain the ROS control architecture for the gripper-arm system."

---
