<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**Q: Define the term "end effector" in the context of a robotic manipulator. With respect to this experiment, identify which part of the combined arm-gripper system is the end effector and state its DOF contribution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **end effector** is the device mounted at the terminal link of a robotic arm that physically interacts with the environment to perform the intended task (grasping, welding, painting, etc.).

In the given experiment, the **2DOF gripper** is the end effector attached to the last link (`wrist_link`) of the 3DOF arm via a *fixed* URDF joint. The gripper contributes **2 DOF** to the overall system: one for finger aperture (open/close) and one for in-hand rotation/secondary articulation. The combined system therefore exhibits **5 DOF** (3 from the arm + 2 from the gripper).

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for identification, 1 mark for DOF count and justification.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]`
**Q: What is the role of the `joint_state_controller` and `robot_state_publisher` nodes in this experiment? Name the topics each subscribes to and publishes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **`joint_state_controller`**: A `ros_control` controller that aggregates the current positions, velocities, and efforts of all robot joints into a single message.
  - **Subscribes to:** Individual joint commands/feedback from Gazebo or hardware.
  - **Publishes to:** `/joint_states` topic (`sensor_msgs/JointState` message).

- **`robot_state_publisher`**: A ROS node that consumes `/joint_states` and the URDF to compute and broadcast the **TF transforms** between every parent and child link.
  - **Subscribes to:** `/joint_states` (and reads `/robot_description` parameter from parameter server).
  - **Publishes to:** `/tf` and `/tf_static` topics (`tf2_msgs/TFMessage`).

These two nodes together build the kinematic tree in TF, enabling RViz visualization, MoveIt planning, and accurate state estimation.

*(1.5 marks for first node, 1.5 marks for second.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Part B question carries internal choice. **Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.** Each is 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### ❑ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) With a neat diagram, explain the procedure to attach a 2DOF gripper as the end effector of a 3DOF arm in ROS. Describe the URDF structure, joint types used, and the TF tree that results. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part a — 7 marks):**

**Step 1 — URDF Hierarchy (2 marks):**
The URDF describes the robot as a tree of links connected by joints. The 3DOF arm portion contains:
- `base_link`, `shoulder_link`, `upper_arm_link`, `forearm_link`, `wrist_link` connected by three `revolute` joints (axes Z, Y, Y respectively) carrying variables $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$.

The 2DOF gripper sub-assembly contains:
- `gripper_base_link`, `left_finger_link`, `right_finger_link` connected by two `revolute` joints (mirrored about the YZ-plane) with variables $\alpha_L, \alpha_R$.

The gripper is attached using a `joint name="arm_to_gripper" type="fixed"` between `wrist_link` (parent) and `gripper_base_link` (child). This is a *static mechanical coupling* with no extra DOF.

**Step 2 — Joint Limit Definition (2 marks):**
Each actuated joint specifies `<limit lower="..." upper="..." effort="..." velocity="..."/>`. Example for gripper:

```xml
<joint name="gripper_left_joint" type="revolute">
  <parent link="gripper_base_link"/>
  <child link="left_finger_link"/>
  <axis xyz="0 0 1"/>
  <limit lower="0.0" upper="0.5" effort="10.0" velocity="1.0"/>
</joint>
```

**Step 3 — TF Tree (2 marks):**
The resulting TF tree is a single chain:
```
base_link → shoulder_link → upper_arm_link → forearm_link → wrist_link → gripper_base_link → {left_finger_link, right_finger_link}
```
Broadcast by `robot_state_publisher` at the configured rate (default 30 Hz).

**Step 4 — Visualization (1 mark):**
The TF tree is verified in RViz using the TF display plugin. All frames must be visible and move synchronously when joint commands are published.

---

**Q: (b) Derive the forward kinematics equations for the 3DOF arm to compute the position of the wrist link (where the gripper is mounted). Assume link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, base height $L_0$, and joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part b — 7 marks):**

Using the DH parameters established in Section 3.1.1, we compute $T_3^0 = T_1^0 \cdot T_2^1 \cdot T_3^2$.

The final wrist position vector (first three rows, last column of $T_3^0$) is:

$$
x = (L_1 \cos\theta_2 + L_2 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3)) \cos\theta_1
$$

$$
y = (L_1 \cos\theta_2 + L_2 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3)) \sin\theta_1
$$

$$
z = L_0 + L_1 \sin\theta_2 + L_2 \sin(\theta_2 + \theta_3)
$$

**Detailed derivation steps (as in Section 3.1.3):**

1. Compute $T_1^0$ with $\alpha_1 = \pi/2, a_1 = 0, d_1 = L_0$. The Y-rotation isolates the base yaw from the shoulder pitch.
2. Compute $T_2^1$ with $\alpha_2 = 0, a_2 = L_1, d_2 = 0$. This is a planar rotation-translation in the XZ plane.
3. Compute $T_3^2$ with $\alpha_3 = 0, a_3 = L_2, d_3 = 0$. Same form as $T_2^1$ but with $\theta_3$.
4. Multiply $T_2^1 \cdot T_3^2$ to obtain the forearm-to-base displacement, then multiply by $T_1^0$ to rotate it about the world Z-axis.

**Verification (1 mark):** When $\theta_2 = \theta_3 = 0$ (arm fully stretched horizontally at shoulder height), $x^2 + y^2 = (L_1 + L_2)^2$ and $z = L_0$ — matching the geometric expectation.

When $\theta_1 = 0, \theta_2 = \pi/2, \theta_3 = 0$ (arm pointing straight up), $x = 0, y = 0, z = L_0 + L_1$ — also correct.

---

### ❑ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: (a) Explain the ROS control architecture used to execute gripping operations in this experiment. List the controllers used, the topics they communicate on, and provide the state machine logic. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part a — 7 marks):**

**Architecture overview (2 marks):**
The control architecture is layered:
- **Top layer — Client/Planning:** A user script or MoveIt! plan generator that decides *what* the gripper and arm should do.
- **Middle layer — Controller Manager:** The `controller_manager` loads, configures, and switches between controllers.
- **Bottom layer — Hardware Abstraction:** `gazebo_ros_control` plugin (in simulation) or real servo drivers (on hardware) that translate commands into joint torques.

**Controllers and topics (3 marks):**
| Controller | Type | Topic (cmd) | Topic (state) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `joint_state_controller` | Read-only aggregator | — | `/joint_states` |
| `arm_position_controller` | `position_controllers/JointTrajectoryController` | `/arm_controller/command` | `/arm_controller/state` |
| `gripper_position_controller` | `position_controllers/JointGroupPositionController` | `/gripper_controller/command` | `/gripper_controller/state` |

**State machine logic (2 marks):**
```
HOME → APPROACH → GRIPPER_OPEN → DESCEND → GRIPPER_CLOSE → LIFT → TRANSPORT → RELEASE → HOME
```
Each transition is triggered by a service call, action goal, or feedback confirmation (e.g., force threshold reached, target pose achieved).

---

**Q: (b) A 2DOF gripper with finger length $r = 0.08$ m must grasp an object of mass $m = 0.5$ kg. The coefficient of static friction is $\mu = 0.4$, and the finger half-angle $\alpha = 0.25$ rad. Compute (i) the gripper aperture, (ii) the minimum per-finger gripping force to prevent slip, and (iii) the minimum actuator torque. Use $g = 9.81$ m/s². (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part b — 7 marks):**

**Given:** $r = 0.08$ m, $m = 0.5$ kg, $\mu = 0.4$, $\alpha = 0.25$ rad, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(i) Gripper aperture (2 marks):**

$$
d = 2 \, r \, \sin\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = 2 \times 0.08 \times \sin(0.125)
$$

$$
\sin(0.125) \approx 0.1247
$$

$$
d = 0.16 \times 0.1247 = 0.01995 \text{ m} \approx 0.02 \text{ m} = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Minimum per-finger gripping force (3 marks):**

From the no-slip equilibrium condition:

$$
2 \mu F_f \geq m g
$$

$$
F_{f,min} = \frac{m g}{2 \mu} = \frac{0.5 \times 9.81}{2 \times 0.4} = \frac{4.905}{0.8} = 6.131 \text{ N}
$$

**(iii) Minimum actuator torque (2 marks):**

$$
\tau_{min} = F_{f,min} \cdot r = 6.131 \times 0.08 = 0.4905 \text{ N·m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a real URDF, the `<effort>` limit of the gripper joint must be set such that the motor can deliver at least $\tau_{min}$. For example, if `effort="1.0"`, this grasp is feasible; if `effort="0.4"`, the object will slip.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this experiment's exam questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the fixed joint (–2 marks):** Many students attach the gripper directly using a `revolute` joint, which would *introduce an unwanted 6th DOF*. Always use `type="fixed"` for the static mount and add separate `revolute` joints only for the actuated fingers.
>
> 2. **Confusing TF with `/joint_states` (–1 mark):** TF transforms are *spatial relationships between frames*, while `/joint_states` is a *time series of joint values*. `robot_state_publisher` bridges the two but they are NOT the same topic.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to define `<limit>` tags (–1 mark):** Without `<limit>`, the URDF will not load in Gazebo or the controllers will reject commands outside the unconstrained range. Always specify lower, upper, effort, and velocity.
>
> 4. **Sign errors in the friction equation (–1 mark):** The factor of 2 in $2 \mu F_f$ comes from *two* contact points (one per finger). Do not omit it.
>
> 5. **Mixing up $\sin(\alpha/2)$ and $\sin(\alpha)$ (–1 mark):** The aperture is $2r \sin(\alpha/2)$, not $2r \sin(\alpha)$. Use half-angle.
>
> 6. **Skipping the kinematic chain step (–2 marks):** In 14-mark derivations, you must show the matrix multiplication of at least two joints and clearly state the DH parameters used. Skipping the intermediate step loses credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU 2024 Prep)**

- ☐ A **2DOF gripper** contributes 2 actuated joints; a **3DOF arm** contributes 3 actuated joints; total = **5 DOF**.
- ☐ The gripper is attached to the arm's terminal link via a **`type="fixed"` URDF joint** — never via a `revolute` or `prismatic` joint.
- ☐ The **TF tree** is a single chain rooted at `base_link`; all gripper fingers are descendants of the wrist link.
- ☐ **`robot_state_publisher`** broadcasts TF using `/joint_states` and the URDF; it does NOT control the robot.
- ☐ **`joint_state_controller`** is a read-only aggregator; it is required for TF and RViz to function.
- ☐ **`position_controllers/JointGroupPositionController`** is the typical choice for gripper control (single setpoint).
- ☐ **`position_controllers/JointTrajectoryController`** is the typical choice for arm control (smooth trajectory).
- ☐ Forward kinematics of the 3DOF arm: $x, y, z$ depend on $(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3)$ and link lengths $L_0, L_1, L_2$.
- ☐ Gripper aperture: $d = 2 r \sin(\alpha / 2)$.
- ☐ Grasp force condition: $F_{f,min} = m g / (2 \mu)$; torque: $\tau_{min} = F_{f,min} \cdot r$.
- ☐ Standard grasp FSM: `HOME → APPROACH → OPEN → DESCEND → CLOSE → LIFT → TRANSPORT → RELEASE → HOME`.
- ☐ Gazebo + `gazebo_ros_control` is used for physics-based simulation; RViz is used for visual confirmation.
- ☐ MoveIt! is the optional but recommended trajectory planning layer for the arm portion of the experiment.
- ☐ Always define `<limit>` (lower, upper, effort, velocity) for every actuated joint in the URDF.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Execute Pick and Place Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL607 - ROS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Execute Pick and Place Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Pick and Place Operation** in the context of the Robot Operating System (ROS) is defined as a canonical robotic manipulation task wherein an end-effector (gripper) is commanded to **approach**, **grasp**, **transport**, and **release** a target object from a source location to a destination location, orchestrated through a coordinated pipeline of **ROS nodes**, **topics**, **services**, and **action servers** (typically leveraging the **MoveIt!** motion planning framework).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory framework for PCBRL607 (ROS), the experiment titled *"Execute Pick and Place Operation"* requires the student to design, simulate, and execute a complete manipulation stack — from perception (object pose estimation) to motion planning (collision-free trajectory generation) to execution (joint-space control of a robotic arm).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module Experiment 2 evaluates the student's ability to integrate **MoveIt!**, **Gazebo**, and the **TF (Transform) library** to perform a closed-loop pick-and-place sequence on a simulated or hardware manipulator (commonly the **Franka Emika Panda** or **Universal Robots UR5**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Arm

Imagine you are sitting at a desk with a coffee cup on your left and you want to move it to the right side. Your brain executes a sequence **without you consciously thinking about it**:

| Human Step | Robotic Equivalent (ROS) |
|------------|-------------------------|
| **Eyes** locate the cup | Camera / Depth Sensor Node publishes image |
| **Brain** estimates the cup's 3D position | Object detection node computes pose → broadcasts via TF |
| **Arm** plans a path avoiding the monitor | MoveIt! motion planner computes collision-free trajectory |
| **Hand** reaches and grasps | Gripper action server closes fingers |
| **Arm** lifts and moves the cup | Joint trajectory controller executes planned path |
| **Hand** releases the cup | Gripper action server opens fingers |

The robotic system **replicates this biological pipeline** through modular, decoupled software components communicating over the **ROS Computation Graph** using a publish-subscribe (topics) and request-reply (services/actions) messaging architecture.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics and Physical Constants

In any KTU lab record, you must document the following **standard engineering metrics** for a pick-and-place experiment:

- **Cycle Time (T_c):** Total time from initiation to release, typically **5–15 seconds** for a standard pick-place cycle.
- **Positional Accuracy:** Repeatability of grasp point, typically **±0.1 mm** for industrial arms.
- **Payload Capacity:** Maximum object weight, e.g., **3 kg** for the Franka Panda.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DoF):** Number of independent joints, e.g., **7-DoF** for the Panda arm.
- **Workspace Volume:** Reachable 3D region, e.g., **0.85 m radius** for the Panda.

> [!NOTE]
> The **TCP (Tool Center Point)** is the reference point at the gripper's tip where all Cartesian motions are defined. All pick-and-place goals are specified in the **base frame** (typically `base_link` or `world`) using the **TF tree**.

## 1.4 Why Pick and Place? — Real-World Industrial Relevance

Pick-and-place is the **#1 most-deployed robotic task globally**, forming the backbone of:

1. **Manufacturing Assembly Lines** (PCB component placement, automotive part fitting).
2. **Warehouse Automation** (Amazon Kiva robots, bin-picking systems).
3. **Pharmaceutical Packaging** (vial sorting and tray loading).
4. **Agricultural Robotics** (fruit harvesting, sorting).
5. **Disaster Response** (debris removal in hazardous environments).

Understanding how to execute this in ROS is the **gateway skill** to all advanced manipulation research.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ROS Manipulation Stack — Architectural Layers

A pick-and-place operation in ROS is decomposed into **five functional layers**, each implemented as one or more ROS nodes:

### Layer 1: Perception Pipeline
- Captures RGB-D (color + depth) data from an Intel RealSense, Azure Kinect, or Astra camera.
- Publishes **PointCloud2** messages on the `/camera/depth/points` topic.
- Runs object detection (YOLO, PCL segmentation) to estimate the object's **6-DoF pose** (position + orientation).

### Layer 2: Transform Broadcasting (TF)
- The estimated pose is published as a transformation from `base_link` → `object_link` via the **TF2 broadcaster**.
- MoveIt! and the robot controller **subscribe** to TF to know where the object is in world coordinates.

### Layer 3: Motion Planning (MoveIt!)
- Receives a **PoseGoal** (target position + orientation of the TCP).
- Uses sampling-based planners (**OMPL** — Open Motion Planning Library) such as **RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree)** or **PRM (Probabilistic Roadmap)**.
- Performs **collision checking** against an **Octomap** (3D voxel grid of the environment) loaded into the **Planning Scene**.
- Outputs a **collision-free trajectory** as a series of waypoints (joint positions, velocities, accelerations).

### Layer 4: Trajectory Execution
- The trajectory is sent to the **FollowJointTrajectory Action Server** on the robot driver (`/arm_controller/follow_joint_trajectory`).
- The controller (typically `ros_control` with `position_controllers/JointTrajectoryController`) commands individual joint motors.

### Layer 5: Gripper Control
- A separate action server (`/gripper_controller/gripper_cmd`) controls the parallel-jaw gripper.
- Commands include `open`, `close`, and `grasp` (with force feedback).

## 2.2 Core Algorithmic Steps (The "Why" and "How")

**Step 1 — Object Detection & Pose Estimation:**
- *Why:* The robot needs to know *where* the object is in 3D space.
- *How:* Point Cloud Library (PCL) filters the table plane (RANSAC), clusters remaining points (Euclidean clustering), and fits a bounding box or geometric primitive.

**Step 2 — Approach Pose Generation:**
- *Why:* The gripper must align with the object's principal axis for a stable grasp.
- *How:* The grasp pose is computed by **offsetting** the object pose by the gripper's pre-defined **grasp offset vector** along the approach direction (typically $-Z$ of the gripper).

**Step 3 — Inverse Kinematics (IK):**
- *Why:* The planner needs joint angles (configuration space) to reach the Cartesian goal.
- *How:* The **KDL** (Kinematics and Dynamics Library) or **TRAC-IK** solver computes $\mathbf{q} = f^{-1}(\mathbf{x})$ for a desired end-effector pose $\mathbf{x}$.

**Step 4 — Path Planning with Collision Avoidance:**
- *Why:* The arm must not collide with the table, the object itself, or itself.
- *How:* RRT* samples random configurations in C-space and connects them while checking collisions via FCL (Flexible Collision Library).

**Step 5 — Trajectory Smoothing & Time Parameterization:**
- *Why:* Raw paths are jagged; robots need smooth, time-optimal trajectories respecting velocity/acceleration limits.
- *How:* **Iterative Parabolic Time Parameterization** or **Time-Optimal Trajectory Generation (TOPP)** smooths the path.

**Step 6 — Execution & Gripper Actuation:**
- *Why:* The gripper must close at the right moment and open at the destination.
- *How:* Synchronized action calls via `actionlib.SimpleActionClient` with appropriate timing.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations form the **mathematical backbone** of pick-and-place in ROS. Memorize them for the 14-mark derivations.

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Use Case |
|---|---------|----------|-------------------|----------|
| 1 | **Forward Kinematics (FK)** | $T_{base}^{ee}(\mathbf{q}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i(q_i)$ | $T \in SE(3)$, $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Map joint angles → end-effector pose |
| 2 | **Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) Transform** | $A_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\theta_i, \alpha_i, a_i, d_i$: DH parameters | Build kinematic chain |
| 3 | **Inverse Kinematics (IK)** | $\mathbf{q}^* = \arg\min_{\mathbf{q}} \;\left\| f(\mathbf{q}) - \mathbf{x}_{goal} \right\|^2$ | $\mathbf{q}^* \in \mathbb{R}^n$, $\mathbf{x}_{goal} \in SE(3)$ | Find joint angles for desired pose |
| 4 | **End-Effector Velocity (Jacobian)** | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = J(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | $J \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times n}$, $\dot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Relate joint rates to Cartesian rates |
| 5 | **Grasp Wrench Closure (Force-Closure)** | $\mathbf{G} \mathbf{f} = -\mathbf{w}_{ext}, \quad \exists \mathbf{f} > 0$ | $\mathbf{G}$: grasp matrix, $\mathbf{f}$: contact forces | Verify stable grasp |
| 6 | **RRT Growth Probability** | $P(\text{connect}) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}[\text{collision-free}(q_i)]$ | $n$: number of sampled nodes | Analyze planner performance |
| 7 | **Trajectory Quintic Polynomial** | $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$ | $t \in [0, T]$, $a_i$: polynomial coefficients | Smooth joint interpolation |
| 8 | **Cycle Time Constraint** | $T_c = t_{approach} + t_{grasp} + t_{transport} + t_{release}$ | All $t$ in seconds | Performance benchmarking |
| 9 | **Octomap Resolution** | $r = \frac{V_{workspace}}{N_{voxels}}$ | $V$ in m³, $N$: number of voxels | Tune collision map detail |
| 10 | **TF Transform Chain** | $T_{A}^{C} = T_{A}^{B} \cdot T_{B}^{C}$ | $4 \times 4$ homogeneous matrices | Coordinate frame conversion |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics of a 2-Link Planar Arm (Foundational Derivation)

To build intuition, let us derive the forward kinematics of a simplified **2-link planar manipulator** in 2D — the building block for understanding any 6/7-DoF arm.

### Setup
- Link 1 has length $L_1$ and joint angle $q_1$ (from horizontal $X$-axis).
- Link 2 has length $L_2$ and joint angle $q_2$ (relative to link 1).
- The end-effector position is $(x, y)$ in the base frame.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Starting from the base, the position of joint 2 (elbow) is:

$$
x_1 = L_1 \cos(q_1)
$$

$$
y_1 = L_1 \sin(q_1)
$$

The position of the end-effector (wrist) is the elbow position plus the contribution of link 2, which is rotated by the **absolute angle** $(q_1 + q_2)$:

$$
x = x_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) = L_1 \cos(q_1) + L_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)
$$

$$
y = y_1 + L_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2) = L_1 \sin(q_1) + L_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)
$$

**Logic:** Each link contributes a vector of length $L_i$ at angle $q_1 + \dots + q_i$ from the base frame. Summing these vectors via the **law of cosines chain rule** yields the end-effector pose.

In homogeneous transformation form:

$$
T_{base}^{ee} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(q_1+q_2) & -\sin(q_1+q_2) & 0 & L_1\cos(q_1) + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ \sin(q_1+q_2) & \cos(q_1+q_2) & 0 & L_1\sin(q_1) + L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Inverse Kinematics (Analytical Solution for 2-Link Arm)

Given a desired end-effector position $(x_d, y_d)$, we solve for $(q_1, q_2)$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Law of Cosines** to find $q_2$:

The squared distance from base to end-effector is:

$$
x_d^2 + y_d^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos(q_2)
$$

Solving for $\cos(q_2)$:

$$
\cos(q_2) = \frac{x_d^2 + y_d^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}
$$

This yields two solutions (elbow-up and elbow-down):

$$
q_2 = \pm \arccos\!\left( \frac{x_d^2 + y_d^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $q_1$** using the atan2 formulation:

$$
q_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(y_d, x_d) - \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(L_2 \sin(q_2),\; L_1 + L_2 \cos(q_2)\right)
$$

**Logic:** The first `atan2` gives the angle from base to goal; the second `atan2` is the offset due to the elbow's bend. Subtracting yields the shoulder angle.

## 3.3 Derivation: Quintic Polynomial Trajectory (Time-Optimal Smoothing)

To move a joint from $q_i$ (initial) to $q_f$ (final) in time $T$ with zero initial and final velocities and accelerations (a common constraint for smooth pick-and-place lifts):

**Boundary conditions:**

$$
q(0) = q_i, \quad \dot{q}(0) = 0, \quad \ddot{q}(0) = 0
$$

$$
q(T) = q_f, \quad \dot{q}(T) = 0, \quad \ddot{q}(T) = 0
$$

**Polynomial form:**

$$
q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5
$$

**Apply boundary conditions** to solve for coefficients:

From $q(0) = q_i$:

$$
a_0 = q_i
$$

From $\dot{q}(0) = 0$ (derivative: $\dot{q}(t) = a_1 + 2a_2 t + 3a_3 t^2 + 4a_4 t^3 + 5a_5 t^4$):

$$
a_1 = 0
$$

From $\ddot{q}(0) = 0$ (second derivative: $\ddot{q}(t) = 2a_2 + 6a_3 t + 12a_4 t^2 + 20a_5 t^3$):

$$
a_2 = 0
$$

From $q(T) = q_f$:

$$
a_3 + a_4 T + a_5 T^2 = \frac{q_f - q_i}{T^3}
$$

From $\dot{q}(T) = 0$:

$$
3a_3 + 4a_4 T + 5a_5 T^2 = 0
$$

From $\ddot{q}(T) = 0$:

$$
6a_3 + 12a_4 T + 20a_5 T^2 = 0
$$

**Solving the linear system** (after substituting $h = q_f - q_i$):

$$
a_3 = \frac{10h}{T^3}, \quad a_4 = \frac{-15h}{T^4}, \quad a_5 = \frac{6h}{T^5}
$$

**Final compact form:**

$$
q(t) = q_i + (q_f - q_i)\left[10\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^3 - 15\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^4 + 6\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^5\right]
$$

This guarantees $C^2$-continuity (continuous position, velocity, and acceleration) — critical for **mechanical stability** and **actuator longevity**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Full Pick-and-Place Execution Pipeline

| Stage | ROS Message Type | Mathematical Operation | Constraint Check | Output |
|-------|------------------|------------------------|------------------|--------|
| **1. Perception** | `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` | Plane segmentation + clustering | Min cluster size > threshold | Object centroid |
| **2. Pose Estimation** | `geometry_msgs/PoseStamped` | PCA on point cloud | Eigenvalue ratio < 2.0 | 6-DoF pose |
| **3. TF Broadcast** | `tf2_msgs/TFMessage` | $T_{world}^{obj} = T_{world}^{cam} \cdot T_{cam}^{obj}$ | TF tree connectivity | Updated TF tree |
| **4. Grasp Synthesis** | `geometry_msgs/PoseStamped` | Apply grasp offset vector | Gripper width < object diameter | Pre-grasp + grasp pose |
| **5. MoveIt! Planning** | `moveit_msgs/MotionPlanRequest` | RRT* in C-space | Collision-free, joint limits | Trajectory |
| **6. Time Parametrization** | `trajectory_msgs/JointTrajectory` | Quintic polynomial fit | $\vert\dot{q}\vert \leq \dot{q}_{max}$, $\vert\ddot{q}\vert \leq \ddot{q}_{max}$ | Time-stamped trajectory |
| **7. Execution** | `control_msgs/FollowJointTrajectory` | Joint servo control | Tracking error < 2° | Joint torques |
| **8. Gripper Close** | `control_msgs/GripperCommand` | PD control on finger position | Force feedback > threshold | Grasp confirmation |
| **9. Lift & Transport** | (Re-plan with held object) | Updated Octomap | No new collisions | New trajectory |
| **10. Release** | `control_msgs/GripperCommand` (open) | Position control | Object drop detection | Task complete |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ROS Computation Graph for Pick and Place

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete node-to-node communication architecture** for a pick-and-place operation. It shows which nodes publish, which subscribe, and which provide services/actions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeCam["camera_node"] -- PointCloud2 --> nodeFilter["pcl_filter_node"]
    nodeFilter -- PointCloud2 --> nodeSeg["plane_segmentation_node"]
    nodeSeg -- PointCloud2 --> nodeCluster["euclidean_cluster_node"]
    nodeCluster -- PoseStamped --> nodePose["pose_estimation_node"]
    nodePose -- TFMessage --> nodeTF["tf2_broadcaster"]
    nodeTF -- transform updates --> nodeMoveit["move_group_node"]
    nodeUser["user_script"] -- PoseGoal service --> nodeMoveit
    nodeMoveit -- MotionPlanRequest --> nodePlanner["ompl_planner"]
    nodePlanner -- collision check --> nodeFCL["fcl_collision_checker"]
    nodeFCL -- Octomap query --> nodeOcto["octomap_server"]
    nodePlanner -- trajectory --> nodeExec["trajectory_execution"]
    nodeExec -- FollowJointTrajectory action --> nodeCtrl["arm_controller"]
    nodeCtrl -- joint commands --> nodeGazebo["gazebo_simulation"]
    nodeUser -- GripperCommand action --> nodeGrip["gripper_controller"]
    nodeGrip -- finger position --> nodeGazebo
    nodeGazebo -- joint states --> nodeCtrl
    nodeCtrl -- feedback --> nodeExec
    nodeExec -- result --> nodeUser
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = topic publish/subscribe. Rectangular nodes = ROS nodes. Action arrows trigger long-running tasks (motion, gripper). The `user_script` (your Python program) is the **orchestrator** that calls MoveIt! and the gripper in the correct sequence.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Pick-and-Place Pipeline

This diagram provides a **layered, functional view** of the same system, suitable for your lab record introduction.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["PERCEPTION LAYER"]
        A1["RGB-D Camera"]
        A2["Point Cloud Filtering"]
        A3["Object Detection & Pose Estimation"]
    end

    subgraph B["REASONING LAYER"]
        B1["TF2 Transform Library"]
        B2["Grasp Pose Synthesis"]
        B3["MoveIt Motion Planner"]
    end

    subgraph C["EXECUTION LAYER"]
        C1["Trajectory Smoother"]
        C2["Joint Trajectory Controller"]
        C3["Gripper Controller"]
    end

    subgraph D["FEEDBACK LAYER"]
        D1["Joint State Publisher"]
        D2["Force/Torque Sensor"]
        D3["Octomap Updates"]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1 --> C2
    B2 --> C3
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    D1 --> B3
    D2 --> C3
    D3 --> B3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Closed-loop verification:** Notice the feedback arrows from Layer D back to Layer B. This represents the **sense-plan-act** cycle — if the object slips (detected by force sensor) or the environment changes (new Octomap), the planner **re-plans** online.

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Temporal Flow of a Single Pick-and-Place Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Script
    participant M as MoveIt
    participant P as Planner OMPL
    participant C as Arm Controller
    participant G as Gripper Controller
    participant S as Simulator Gazebo

    U->>M: setPoseTarget(approach_pose)
    M->>P: plan()
    P-->>M: trajectory
    M-->>U: success
    U->>C: execute(trajectory)
    C->>S: send joint commands
    S-->>C: joint states
    C-->>U: execution complete
    U->>G: close_gripper()
    G-->>U: grasp confirmed
    U->>M: setPoseTarget(transport_pose)
    M->>P: plan() with attached object
    P-->>M: trajectory
    M-->>U: success
    U->>C: execute(trajectory)
    C->>S: send joint commands
    S-->>C: joint states
    C-->>U: execution complete
    U->>G: open_gripper()
    G-->>U: release confirmed
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Pick and Place operation in the context of ROS. List the four main phases of the operation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Pick and Place operation** in ROS is a robotic manipulation task where a robot arm uses a gripper to grasp an object from a source location and release it at a destination location, coordinated through ROS nodes, topics, and the MoveIt! motion planning framework.

The four main phases are:
1. **Approach** — Moving the end-effector to a pre-grasp pose near the object.
2. **Grasp** — Closing the gripper to securely hold the object.
3. **Transport** — Lifting and moving the object to the target location.
4. **Release** — Opening the gripper to deposit the object.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing 4 phases, 1 mark for brief description of each.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of the TF2 library in a ROS pick-and-place system? Why is the `base_link → tool0` transform critical?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **TF2 library** manages the coordinate frame hierarchy of the robot, allowing any node to query the 3D pose of any link or object relative to any other frame in real-time without manual transformation.

The `base_link → tool0` transform is **critical** because:
1. It represents the **current end-effector (TCP) pose** relative to the robot's base, which is required for inverse kinematics to compute joint angles for any Cartesian goal.
2. MoveIt! uses it to **verify reachability** — if the goal pose is outside the reachable workspace, planning fails immediately.
3. It enables **closed-loop control** — the controller continuously updates this transform to track the planned trajectory.

*(Award 1 mark each for role of TF2, role of base_link→tool0, and importance/reachability.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Common mistakes students make in 14-mark pick-and-place questions:
> 1. **Confusing topics with services** — Remember: topics are for continuous streaming (joint states, point clouds); services and actions are for one-shot tasks (motion planning, gripper control).
> 2. **Forgetting the Planning Scene** — Many students describe MoveIt! without mentioning the **collision object** and **attached object** mechanisms, which are essential for transporting held items.
> 3. **Skipping feedback** — A complete answer must mention **force feedback** for grasp confirmation and **joint state feedback** for execution monitoring.
> 4. **No coordinate frame specification** — Always specify the frame (e.g., `base_link`, `world`, `object_frame`) when describing any pose.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete ROS architecture** for executing a pick-and-place operation on a 7-DoF manipulator. Describe the role of each major node. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **forward and inverse kinematics** of a 2-link planar manipulator. Show how the IK solution is used by MoveIt! to convert a Cartesian pick goal into joint trajectories. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) ROS Architecture (7 marks):**

The complete ROS architecture for a pick-and-place operation consists of **five inter-connected layers** (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):

**Layer 1 — Perception (1.5 marks):**
- `camera_node` publishes raw RGB-D data as `sensor_msgs/Image` and `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` on `/camera/depth/image_raw` and `/camera/depth/points`.
- A `pcl_processing_node` subscribes to the point cloud, applies a **voxel grid filter** for downsampling, performs **RANSAC plane segmentation** to remove the table, and runs **Euclidean clustering** to isolate the object.
- A `pose_estimation_node` fits a geometric primitive (cuboid, cylinder) to the cluster and publishes the 6-DoF pose as `geometry_msgs/PoseStamped`.

**Layer 2 — Transform Management (1 mark):**
- A `tf2_broadcaster` node publishes the transformation $T_{world}^{object}$ (object's pose in world frame) and the robot's own kinematic chain $T_{base}^{ee}$.

**Layer 3 — Motion Planning (2 marks):**
- The `move_group` node (the central MoveIt! component) wraps everything: it loads the URDF, the SRDF (Semantic Robot Description), the kinematic solver (KDL/TRAC-IK), and the OMPL planner.
- The user calls `move_group.setPoseTarget()` via a Python client. MoveIt! invokes the OMPL planner (RRTConnect or RRT*) to compute a collision-free trajectory using the **Planning Scene** (which contains the robot, the environment, and the object as a collision object).

**Layer 4 — Trajectory Execution (1.5 marks):**
- The computed trajectory (`trajectory_msgs/JointTrajectory`) is sent to the `FollowJointTrajectory` **action server** on `/arm_controller`.
- The `ros_control` stack (with `JointTrajectoryController`) commands individual joints at 100–1000 Hz.
- Feedback (`control_msgs/JointTrajectoryFeedback`) is published continuously.

**Layer 5 — Gripper Control (1 mark):**
- A separate action client calls `/gripper_controller/gripper_cmd` with `GripperCommand` (position, max_effort).
- The gripper closes until either the position is reached or the force exceeds the effort limit (indicating a successful grasp).

**Coordination Sequence:** A master Python script (the "orchestrator") calls MoveIt! to move to the pre-grasp, then issues a gripper close command, then calls MoveIt! again with the object **attached to the gripper** in the planning scene (so it travels with the arm), then finally opens the gripper at the destination.

**(b) Forward & Inverse Kinematics (7 marks):**

**Forward Kinematics (3.5 marks):**

For a 2-link planar arm with link lengths $L_1, L_2$ and joint angles $q_1, q_2$, the end-effector position is derived by **vector addition** of each link's contribution:

The position of joint 2 (elbow) in the base frame is:

$$
(x_1, y_1) = (L_1 \cos q_1,\; L_1 \sin q_1)
$$

The end-effector position is the elbow position plus link 2 rotated by the **absolute angle** $(q_1 + q_2)$:

$$
x_{ee} = L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)
$$

$$
y_{ee} = L_1 \sin q_1 + L_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)
$$

In homogeneous matrix form (treating the planar case with $z=0$):

$$
T_{base}^{ee} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(q_1+q_2) & -\sin(q_1+q_2) & L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ \sin(q_1+q_2) & \cos(q_1+q_2) & L_1\sin q_1 + L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This transformation $T$ maps the joint space $\mathbf{q} = (q_1, q_2)^T$ to the Cartesian space $\mathbf{x} = (x, y, \phi)^T$ where $\phi = q_1 + q_2$ is the end-effector orientation.

**Inverse Kinematics (3.5 marks):**

Given a desired Cartesian pose $(x_d, y_d)$, we solve for $(q_1, q_2)$.

**Step 1 — Find $q_2$** by squaring and summing the FK equations:

$$
x_d^2 + y_d^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos q_2
$$

Isolating $\cos q_2$:

$$
\cos q_2 = \frac{x_d^2 + y_d^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}
$$

The two solutions correspond to **elbow-up** and **elbow-down** configurations:

$$
q_2 = \pm \arccos\!\left( \frac{x_d^2 + y_d^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Find $q_1$** by computing the angle to the goal and subtracting the elbow offset:

$$
q_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(y_d, x_d) - \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(L_2 \sin q_2,\; L_1 + L_2 \cos q_2\right)
$$

**Connection to MoveIt! (extra clarity):** MoveIt! uses a generalized IK solver (KDL, TRAC-IK, or IKFast) that internally applies iterative numerical methods (like the Jacobian transpose or Damped Least Squares) to solve:

$$
\Delta \mathbf{q} = J^{-1}(\mathbf{q}) \, \Delta \mathbf{x}
$$

For 6/7-DoF arms, an analytical closed-form is often impossible, so numerical solvers iteratively minimize $\left\| f(\mathbf{q}) - \mathbf{x}_{goal} \right\|^2$ subject to joint limits, returning the joint trajectory that the controller will execute.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **MoveIt! motion planning pipeline** in detail. Describe the role of the Planning Scene, OMPL planner, and trajectory time parameterization in a pick-and-place task. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pick-and-place robot must lift a 0.5 kg object vertically by 20 cm in 2 seconds using a **quintic polynomial trajectory**. Derive the polynomial coefficients ensuring zero initial and final velocity and acceleration. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) MoveIt! Motion Planning Pipeline (7 marks):**

MoveIt! is the flagship motion planning framework in ROS, providing a unified interface for manipulation. The pipeline consists of **five sequential stages**:

**Stage 1 — Scene Loading (1 mark):**
The **Planning Scene** is a centralized world representation that contains:
- The robot itself (loaded from the URDF — Unified Robot Description Format).
- The environment (static collision objects from the SRDF — Semantic Robot Description Format).
- Dynamic objects (added programmatically via the `PlanningSceneInterface`, e.g., the target object to be picked).
- **Attached objects** (objects currently held by the gripper, which travel with the end-effector).

The scene is internally represented as an **Octomap** — a probabilistic 3D voxel grid for efficient collision queries.

**Stage 2 — Goal Specification (1 mark):**
The user (or a higher-level task planner) specifies a `PoseGoal` (target position + orientation) or a `JointGoal` (target joint configuration). For pick-and-place, a Cartesian pose in the `base_link` frame is typical.

**Stage 3 — Motion Planning (2 marks):**
MoveIt! delegates to **OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library)**, which provides sampling-based planners:
- **RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree):** Builds a tree from the start by sampling random configurations and extending toward them.
- **RRTConnect:** Grows two trees from start and goal, connecting them — faster for bimanual or constrained problems.
- **PRM (Probabilistic Roadmap):** Pre-computes a roadmap of collision-free configurations, then queries it for paths.

At each step, the planner queries the **FCL (Flexible Collision Library)** to verify that sampled configurations do not collide with any object in the Planning Scene.

**Stage 4 — Trajectory Smoothing & Time Parameterization (1.5 marks):**
The raw path (a sequence of waypoints) is **smoothed** by shortcutting and random perturbation. Then **time parameterization** assigns timestamps to each waypoint such that:
- Velocity limits $\vert\dot{q}_i\vert \leq \dot{q}_{i,max}$ are respected.
- Acceleration limits $\vert\ddot{q}_i\vert \leq \ddot{q}_{i,max}$ are respected.
- The trajectory is time-optimal (executed as fast as safely possible).

**Stage 5 — Execution (1.5 marks):**
The time-stamped `trajectory_msgs/JointTrajectory` is sent to the `FollowJointTrajectory` action server. The controller tracks it using PID or feed-forward + feedback control. If tracking error exceeds a threshold, the controller aborts and the orchestrator script can **re-plan**.

**Pick-and-Place Specifics:** After grasping, the orchestrator calls `scene.attach_object(object_id, gripper_link)` to add the object as an **attached collision object**. This ensures that during transport, the planner accounts for the object's geometry and the object moves with the gripper. After release, `scene.remove_attached_object()` and `scene.remove_world_object()` are called.

**(b) Quintic Polynomial Derivation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Initial position: $q_i = 0$ (reference, object starts at height 0)
- Final position: $q_f = 0.20$ m (lift 20 cm)
- Time duration: $T = 2$ s
- Mass $m = 0.5$ kg (informational; does not enter the kinematic polynomial)

**Constraints:** Zero initial and final velocity and acceleration (ensures smooth start/stop).

**Polynomial form:**

$$
q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5
$$

**Apply boundary conditions** (as derived in Section 3.3):

From $q(0) = 0$: $\quad a_0 = 0$

From $\dot{q}(0) = 0$: $\quad a_1 = 0$

From $\ddot{q}(0) = 0$: $\quad a_2 = 0$

**At $t = T = 2$ s:**

$$
q(2) = 0.20 \;\Rightarrow\; a_3(2)^3 + a_4(2)^4 + a_5(2)^5 = 0.20
$$

$$
8 a_3 + 16 a_4 + 32 a_5 = 0.20 \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

$$
\dot{q}(2) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 3a_3(2)^2 + 4a_4(2)^3 + 5a_5(2)^4 = 0
$$

$$
12 a_3 + 32 a_4 + 80 a_5 = 0 \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

$$
\ddot{q}(2) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 6a_3(2) + 12a_4(2)^2 + 20a_5(2)^3 = 0
$$

$$
12 a_3 + 48 a_4 + 160 a_5 = 0 \quad \text{...(iii)}
$$

**Solve the linear system** (i), (ii), (iii):

From (i): $a_3 = 0.025 - 2a_4 - 4a_5$

Substitute into (ii):
$$
12(0.025 - 2a_4 - 4a_5) + 32a_4 + 80a_5 = 0
$$
$$
0.30 - 24 a_4 - 48 a_5 + 32 a_4 + 80 a_5 = 0
$$
$$
0.30 + 8 a_4 + 32 a_5 = 0
$$
$$
a_4 = -0.0375 - 4 a_5 \quad \text{...(iv)}
$$

Substitute $a_3$ and $a_4$ into (iii):
$$
12(0.025 - 2a_4 - 4a_5) + 48a_4 + 160a_5 = 0
$$
$$
0.30 - 24 a_4 - 48 a_5 + 48 a_4 + 160 a_5 = 0
$$
$$
0.30 + 24 a_4 + 112 a_5 = 0
$$

Substitute (iv):
$$
0.30 + 24(-0.0375 - 4 a_5) + 112 a_5 = 0
$$
$$
0.30 - 0.90 - 96 a_5 + 112 a_5 = 0
$$
$$
-0.60 + 16 a_5 = 0
$$
$$
a_5 = 0.0375
$$

Back-substitute:
$$
a_4 = -0.0375 - 4(0.0375) = -0.0375 - 0.15 = -0.1875
$$
$$
a_3 = 0.025 - 2(-0.1875) - 4(0.0375) = 0.025 + 0.375 - 0.15 = 0.25
$$

**Final coefficients:**

$$
a_0 = 0, \quad a_1 = 0, \quad a_2 = 0, \quad a_3 = 0.25, \quad a_4 = -0.1875, \quad a_5 = 0.0375
$$

**Final trajectory equation:**

$$
q(t) = 0.25\,t^3 - 0.1875\,t^4 + 0.0375\,t^5 \quad \text{(meters, } t \in [0, 2] \text{ s)}
$$

**Verification at $t = 1$ s (midpoint):**

$$
q(1) = 0.25 - 0.1875 + 0.0375 = 0.10 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark \text{ (exactly half of 0.20 m, as expected for symmetric quintic)}
$$

This smooth, jerk-minimized trajectory is exactly the kind of motion profile that MoveIt!'s **Time-Optimal Trajectory Parameterization** would generate internally for the lift phase of a pick-and-place.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Exam**

### Key Definitions
- **Pick and Place:** Approach → Grasp → Transport → Release sequence.
- **MoveIt!:** ROS motion planning framework integrating OMPL, FCL, IK solvers.
- **OMPL:** Open Motion Planning Library (RRT, RRT*, PRM algorithms).
- **TF2:** Library for managing coordinate frame transformations.
- **TCP (Tool Center Point):** Reference point on the gripper for Cartesian goals.
- **Planning Scene:** World model with robot, environment, and attached objects.
- **Octomap:** Probabilistic 3D voxel grid for collision checking.

### Essential ROS Topics & Actions
- `/camera/depth/points` — `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2`
- `/tf`, `/tf_static` — `tf2_msgs/TFMessage`
- `/move_group` — `moveit_msgs/MotionPlanRequest` (action)
- `/arm_controller/follow_joint_trajectory` — `control_msgs/FollowJointTrajectory` (action)
- `/gripper_controller/gripper_cmd` — `control_msgs/GripperCommand` (action)
- `/joint_states` — `sensor_msgs/JointState`

### Critical Equations
- **FK:** $\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$ — joint angles to Cartesian pose
- **IK:** $\mathbf{q}^* = \arg\min_{\mathbf{q}} \left\| f(\mathbf{q}) - \mathbf{x}_{goal} \right\|^2$
- **Jacobian:** $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = J(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$
- **Quintic polynomial:** $q(t) = q_i + (q_f - q_i)\left[10\tau^3 - 15\tau^4 + 6\tau^5\right]$, $\tau = t/T$
- **TF chain:** $T_A^C = T_A^B \cdot T_B^C$

### Workflow to Memorize
1. **Sense** — Camera → Point Cloud → Object Pose
2. **Plan** — MoveIt! + OMPL + FCL → Collision-free trajectory
3. **Smooth** — Quintic spline / TOPP → Time-parameterized path
4. **Act** — Joint controllers + Gripper actuator
5. **Verify** — Force feedback, joint state feedback, re-plan if needed

### Common Exam Traps to Avoid
- ❌ Forgetting to **attach the object** to the gripper in the Planning Scene after grasping.
- ❌ Confusing `JointTrajectoryController` (continuous) with `PositionController` (single point).
- ❌ Specifying goals in the wrong coordinate frame.
- ❌ Omitting **collision object** specification for the table/object.
- ❌ Using `rostopic pub` (one-shot) for continuous streams — use publishers instead.

### Sample Python Orchestrator Skeleton (for your lab record)
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import rospy
import moveit_commander
from control_msgs.msg import GripperCommand, GripperCommandGoal
from actionlib import SimpleActionClient

rospy.init_node("pick_and_place_orchestrator")
moveit_commander.roscpp_initialize([])
arm = moveit_commander.MoveGroupCommander("panda_arm")
gripper = SimpleActionClient("/franka_gripper/gripper_action", GripperCommandAction)
gripper.wait_for_server()

# Phase 1: Approach
arm.set_pose_target(approach_pose)
arm.go(wait=True)

# Phase 2: Grasp
goal = GripperCommandGoal()
goal.command.position = 0.02  # 2 cm closure
goal.command.max_effort = 50.0
gripper.send_goal_and_wait(goal)

# Phase 3: Transport (with attached object)
arm.attach_object("target_cube")
arm.set_pose_target(transport_pose)
arm.go(wait=True)

# Phase 4: Release
arm.detach_object("target_cube")
goal.command.position = 0.08  # open
gripper.send_goal_and_wait(goal)
rospy.loginfo("Pick and Place Complete!")
```

### Numerical Constants to Remember
- **Franka Panda reach:** 0.85 m radius
- **Franka Panda payload:** 3 kg
- **Franka Panda DoF:** 7
- **Default planner:** RRTConnect
- **Default IK solver:** KDL (switch to TRAC-IK for better convergence)
- **Octomap resolution:** 0.02 m (typical for tabletop manipulation)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarisation with 2D navigation stack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL607 - ROS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarisation with 2D navigation stack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **2D Navigation Stack** in ROS is a collection of interconnected software packages that enable a mobile robot to autonomously navigate from a start pose to a goal pose in a known 2D environment while avoiding obstacles. It is formally defined as a **probabilistic planning framework** that integrates perception (laser/sonar), localization, mapping, and motion control through a unified topic-based communication layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** In ROS 2 (Humble/Iron/Jazzy), the equivalent framework is officially called **Nav2** (Navigation 2), whereas in ROS 1 (Noetic) it is called the **`navigation` stack** with the central node being `move_base`. Both are referred to in KTU exam questions as the *2D navigation stack*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Self-Driving Car for Indoor Robots"

Imagine you enter an unfamiliar shopping mall and you want to go to a specific shop:

1. **Map** — You look at the mall directory (static map).
2. **Localization** — You keep asking people "where am I?" and checking landmarks (AMCL).
3. **Global Path Planning** — You plan a high-level route: "Take the escalator, turn left, walk 50 m" (NavFn / Smac Planner).
4. **Local Path Planning** — While walking, you dynamically avoid a stroller or a slow-walking aunty (DWA / TEB / MPPI).
5. **Recovery** — If the corridor is blocked, you back up and try a different route (Recovery Behaviors).
6. **Velocity Output** — Your feet execute the motion: forward, turn, stop (`/cmd_vel`).

The ROS 2D navigation stack does **exactly this**, but mathematically and at 10–50 Hz in a real robot.

### 1.3 Core Components at a Glance

| Component (ROS 1) | Nav2 Equivalent (ROS 2) | Primary Role |
|---|---|---|
| `map_server` | `map_server` | Publishes the static occupancy grid map |
| `amcl` | `amcl` | Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization |
| `costmap_2d` | `nav2_costmap_2d` | 2D layered cost map (obstacles + inflation) |
| `move_base` | `bt_navigator` | Top-level state machine / Behavior Tree |
| `navfn / global_planner` | `planner_server` (NavFn, Smac) | Global path planning |
| `base_local_planner / dwa_local_planner` | `controller_server` (DWA, TEB, MPPI) | Local trajectory generation |
| `rotate_recovery`, `clear_costmap_recovery` | `recovery_server` | Recovery behaviors |
| `fake_localization` | `amcl` (built-in) | Odometry-based pose broadcast |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCBRL607 Module 2):** Students must be able to (i) launch the 2D nav stack using launch files, (ii) visualise the costmap in RViz, (iii) set a 2D Nav Goal, and (iv) inspect TF tree, topics, and parameter server interaction.

### 1.4 Standard Topics Exchanged (Required for Exam)

The stack communicates through a fixed set of ROS topics. The four mandatory ones you **must memorise** are:

- **`/map`** — `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` (the static map)
- **`/tf`, `/tf_static`** — coordinate transformations
- **`/cmd_vel`** — `geometry_msgs/Twist` (velocity commands to base)
- **`/scan`** — `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` (input from LIDAR)
- **`/odom`** — `nav_msgs/Odometry` (wheel odometry)
- **`/goal_pose`** (Nav2) or **`/move_base_simple/goal`** (ROS 1)

> [!TIP]
> If an exam question asks "Which topic does `move_base` *subscribe* to for sensor data?" the answer is `/scan` (LIDAR) — this is the **most commonly tested** topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — The Three-Layered Hierarchy

The 2D navigation stack operates in a strict three-layered architecture. Every KTU question on this topic traces back to these three layers.

**Layer 1 — Perception Layer**
- Reads `/scan` (LIDAR) and `/odom` (wheel encoders).
- Updates the local costmap with raw obstacle data at the sensor update rate (typically 10–40 Hz).

**Layer 2 — Decision Layer**
- **`amcl`** fuses laser scan + odometry + map → publishes `amcl_pose` (probabilistic robot pose).
- **`planner_server`** computes a global path on the static costmap using **Dijkstra / A***.
- **`controller_server`** tracks the path and outputs velocity commands at 20 Hz.

**Layer 3 — Actuation Layer**
- `/cmd_vel` is sent to the motor controller / `diff_drive_controller` / `ackermann_controller`.
- A safety watchdog (`/cmd_vel_safety`) may clamp velocities if obstacles are too close.

### 2.2 TF Tree — The Skeleton of Navigation

The TF (transform) tree is the **spatial backbone** of the navigation stack. Every node in the tree must be correctly broadcast or the stack will refuse to start.

```
map → odom → base_link → [laser_frame, camera_frame, imu_frame, wheel_frames]
```

- **`map → odom`** is published by `amcl` (corrects drift over time).
- **`odom → base_link`** is published by `robot_state_publisher` + `joint_state_publisher` (or wheel odometry node).
- **`base_link → laser_frame`** is a **static transform** with (x, y, z, roll, pitch, yaw) offsets from URDF.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Trap:** Students often confuse `map → odom` (published by AMCL) with `odom → base_link` (published by odometry). If asked "which node publishes the transform from `map` to `odom`?" — the **only correct answer is `amcl`**.

### 2.3 The Costmap — Mathematical Core

A costmap is a 2D grid where each cell holds a value in the range **[0, 255]**.

| Value | Meaning | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Free space | $c_{free}$ |
| 1–99 | Inscribed (decay) zone | $c_{inscribed}$ |
| 100 | Inflated (no-go) zone | $c_{inflation}$ |
| 255 | Lethal obstacle | $c_{lethal}$ |
| -1 (205) | Unknown | $c_{unknown}$ |

The **inflation layer** is computed as a decaying exponential from each obstacle:

$$
c(d) = \text{clamp}\left( \text{lerp\_factor} \cdot \exp\left( -1.0 \cdot \text{cost\_scaling\_factor} \cdot (d - \text{inscribed\_radius}) \right) \cdot 254.0, \; 0, \; 253 \right)
$$

where $d$ is the distance from the nearest lethal obstacle, and `cost_scaling_factor` is typically **5.0–20.0**. Cells with $c \ge 253$ are treated as lethal.

### 2.4 AMCL Localization — Monte Carlo Particle Filter

Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization estimates pose $\mathbf{x}_t = (x, y, \theta)$ by maintaining a set of weighted particles $\{\mathbf{x}_t^{(i)}, w_t^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$.

**Prediction (Motion Model):**
$$
\mathbf{x}_t^{(i)} \sim p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}^{(i)}, \mathbf{u}_t)
$$
where $\mathbf{u}_t = (v_x, v_y, \omega_z)$ is the odometry input.

**Update (Measurement Model):**
$$
w_t^{(i)} = p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t^{(i)}, m)
$$
where $m$ is the static map and $\mathbf{z}_t$ is the laser scan.

**Resampling:** Particles with $w_t^{(i)} < w_{threshold}$ are discarded and replaced (typically via low-variance resampling).

> [!NOTE]
> The number of particles in KTU lab experiments is **default = 5000** (`amcl/param/min_particles` = 1000, `max_particles` = 5000). Adaptive resampling (`kld_resampling = true`) is the standard setting.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Inflation cost | $c(d) = 254 \cdot e^{-\alpha(d - r_i)}$ | $\alpha$ = cost_scaling_factor, $r_i$ = inscribed_radius |
| DWA velocity sample range | $v \in [v_{min}, v_{max}]$, $\omega \in [\omega_{min}, \omega_{max}]$ | m/s, rad/s |
| DWA objective | $G(v,\omega) = \sigma(\alpha \cdot heading + \beta \cdot dist + \gamma \cdot vel)$ | weighted sum |
| A* heuristic (octile) | $h(n) = \max(\Delta x, \Delta y) + (\sqrt{2}-1)\min(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ | admissible heuristic |
| Path cost | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | $g$ = actual, $h$ = heuristic |
| Particle filter mean | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_t^{(i)} \mathbf{x}_t^{(i)}$ | weighted mean pose |
| Effective sample size | $N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum (w_t^{(i)})^2}$ | resampling trigger |
| Wheel odometry (diff-drive) | $\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta R - \Delta L}{b}$, $\Delta s = \frac{\Delta R + \Delta L}{2}$ | $b$ = wheelbase |
| TF chain | `map → odom → base_link → laser` | required depth = 4 |
| Default rate | planner: **1 Hz**, controller: **20 Hz**, costmap: **10 Hz** | ROS clock |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the KTU exam: **do not use the `|` symbol inside table cells**. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value or conditional notation if needed. The formulas above use clean LaTeX which compiles inside markdown safely.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 2D navigation stack is the **de-facto industry standard** for indoor autonomous mobile robots (AMR). It is used in:

- **Warehouse AGVs** (Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics) — fleet management + Nav2.
- **Hospital service robots** (TUG by Aethon) — elevator integration + Nav2.
- **Restaurant delivery robots** (Starship, Kiwibot) — sidewalk navigation.
- **Research platforms** (TurtleBot 3/4, Clearpath Husky, Jackal) — KTU lab default.

The shift from ROS 1 to ROS 2 is justified by **deterministic behaviour** required for safety-critical systems, achieved through the DDS (Data Distribution Service) middleware layer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: A* Path Cost Function (Global Planner)

The global planner uses **A\*** (or its multi-resolution variant **Smac Planner** in Nav2). The cost of node $n$ is:

$$
f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
$$

**Step 1 — Actual cost $g(n)$ from start:**
$$
g(n) = g(\text{parent}) + c(\text{parent}, n)
$$

**Step 2 — Transition cost $c(a, b)$ on the costmap:**
$$
c(a, b) = d(a, b) \cdot \left( 1 + \beta \cdot \text{costmap}(b) \right)
$$

where $d(a, b)$ is the Euclidean distance and $\beta$ is the **cost_factor** (default = 0.55).

**Step 3 — Heuristic $h(n)$ (Octile for 8-connected grid):**
$$
h(n) = \max(\Delta x, \Delta y) + (\sqrt{2} - 1) \cdot \min(\Delta x, \Delta y)
$$

This heuristic is **admissible** (never overestimates) and **consistent** (triangle inequality holds), guaranteeing optimal paths.

**Step 4 — Final optimality proof sketch:**
A* with consistent heuristic explores nodes in non-decreasing $f$ value, terminating at goal with $f(g) \le f(\text{any unexplored}) \le f^*$.

### 3.2 Derivation: DWA (Dynamic Window Approach) Objective Function

DWA samples velocities in a reachable window, then scores each by a weighted multi-objective function.

**Step 1 — Reachable velocity set in one cycle:**
$$
V_d = \{ (v, \omega) : v \in [v_a - \dot v \cdot \Delta t, \; v_a + \dot v \cdot \Delta t] \cap [v_{min}, v_{max}], \; \omega \in [\omega_a - \dot\omega \cdot \Delta t, \; \omega_a + \dot\omega \cdot \Delta t] \cap [\omega_{min}, \omega_{max}] \}
$$

**Step 2 — Admissible velocities (collision-free in prediction horizon):**
$$
V_a = \{ (v, \omega) \in V_d : \text{distToObstacle}(v, \omega) \ge \text{robot\_radius} \}
$$

**Step 3 — Scoring function (Trajectory Rollout / DWA):**
$$
G(v, \omega) = \sigma \Big( \alpha \cdot \text{heading}(v,\omega) + \beta \cdot \text{dist}(v,\omega) + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity}(v,\omega) \Big)
$$

where:
- $\text{heading}$ = alignment with global path
- $\text{dist}$ = distance to nearest obstacle
- $\text{velocity}$ = forward speed (encourages progress)
- $\sigma(\cdot)$ = normaliser to $[0, 1]$
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = tunable weights (default ~ 1.0, 0.2, 0.2)

**Step 4 — Command selection:**
$$
(v^*, \omega^*) = \arg\max_{(v,\omega) \in V_a} G(v, \omega)
$$

The chosen $(v^*, \omega^*)$ is published on `/cmd_vel` for one cycle ($\Delta t \approx 0.05$ s = 20 Hz).

### 3.3 Derivation: Differential-Drive Odometry Update

Given left and right wheel encoder deltas $\Delta L, \Delta R$ and wheelbase $b$:

**Step 1 — Forward displacement:**
$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta R + \Delta L}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Angular displacement:**
$$
\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta R - \Delta L}{b}
$$

**Step 3 — Pose update in world frame:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= x_t + \Delta s \cdot \cos\!\left( \theta_t + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2} \right) \\
y_{t+1} &= y_t + \Delta s \cdot \sin\!\left( \theta_t + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2} \right) \\
\theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t + \Delta \theta
\end{aligned}
$$

The mid-point integration reduces drift compared to naive Euler integration, especially during turns.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

| Algorithm | Used In | Complexity | Optimality | Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dijkstra | NavFn (Nav2) | $O(E + V \log V)$ | Optimal | None |
| A* | NavFn, Smac | $O(E)$ (with good $h$) | Optimal | Admissible $h$ |
| Smac Hybrid-A* | Smac Planner 2D | $O(b^d)$ | Sub-optimal (kinematic) | Ackermann / car-like |
| DWA | DWA Local Planner | $O(N_v \cdot N_\omega \cdot t_h)$ | Local optimum | Velocity bounds |
| TEB | TEB Local Planner | $O(N_{iter} \cdot N_{points})$ | Local optimum | Time-elastic band |
| MPPI | MPPI Controller | $O(K \cdot T)$ (GPU-friendly) | Stochastic optimal | Model-based |

### 3.5 Worked Example — Costmap Inflation Calculation

**Given:** A circular obstacle of radius $r = 0.3$ m. Inflation radius $r_i = 0.5$ m. Cost scaling factor $\alpha = 10.0$. Robot inscribed radius $r_{insc} = 0.3$ m.

**Find:** Cost at $d = 0.6$ m from obstacle center.

**Step 1 — Distance from obstacle boundary:**
$$
d_{boundary} = d - r = 0.6 - 0.3 = 0.3 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Distance from inflation edge:**
$$
d_{inflat} = d_{boundary} - r_i = 0.3 - 0.5 = -0.2 \text{ m}
$$

Since $d_{inflat} < 0$, the point is **inside the inflation zone** and is **inscribed (lethal)**.

**Step 3 — Recompute at $d = 1.0$ m:**
$$
d_{inflat} = (1.0 - 0.3) - 0.5 = 0.2 \text{ m}
$$

$$
c(0.2) = 254 \cdot e^{-10.0 \cdot 0.2} = 254 \cdot e^{-2.0} \approx 254 \cdot 0.1353 \approx 34.4
$$

This cell is **free** (cost = 34) but with a slight penalty.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Full 2D Navigation Stack Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sensors["Perception Layer"]
        LIDAR["LIDAR /scan"]
        ODOM["Wheel Encoders /odom"]
        IMU["IMU /imu"]
    end

    subgraph Costmap["Costmap 2D"]
        SL["Static Layer map"]
        OL["Obstacle Layer scan"]
        IL["Inflation Layer"]
        VL["Voxel Layer 3D"]
    end

    subgraph Localization["Localization"]
        AMCL["AMCL Particle Filter"]
    end

    subgraph Decision["Decision Layer"]
        GLOB["Global Planner NavFn / Smac"]
        LOC["Local Planner DWA / TEB / MPPI"]
        REC["Recovery Server"]
        BT["BT Navigator Behavior Tree"]
    end

    subgraph TF["TF Tree"]
        T1["map"]
        T2["odom"]
        T3["base_link"]
        T4["laser_frame"]
    end

    subgraph Actuators["Actuation Layer"]
        CMD["/cmd_vel Velocity Command"]
        MC["Motor Controller diff_drive"]
    end

    LIDAR --> OL
    ODOM --> AMCL
    ODOM --> T2
    IMU --> AMCL
    SL --> IL
    OL --> IL
    VL --> IL
    IL --> GLOB
    IL --> LOC
    AMCL --> T1
    T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4
    BT --> GLOB
    BT --> LOC
    BT --> REC
    GLOB --> BT
    LOC --> CMD
    CMD --> MC
```

### 4.2 TF Tree Dependency Graph

```mermaid
graph TD
    MAP["map world-fixed frame"]
    ODOM["odom continuous frame"]
    BASE["base_link robot center"]
    LASER["base_laser lidar mount"]
    IMU["imu_link"]
    WHEEL_L["wheel_left_link"]
    WHEEL_R["wheel_right_link"]
    CAM["camera_link"]

    MAP -->|"amcl"| ODOM
    ODOM -->|"robot_state_publisher"| BASE
    BASE -->|"static TF"| LASER
    BASE -->|"static TF"| IMU
    BASE -->|"static TF"| CAM
    BASE -->|"joint state"| WHEEL_L
    BASE -->|"joint state"| WHEEL_R
```

### 4.3 Behaviour Tree Sequence — Nav2 Goal Execution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Goal Received"] --> B["ComputePathToPose"]
    B --> C{"Path Found?"}
    C -->|"Yes"| D["FollowPath"]
    C -->|"No"| E["Recovery Behaviors"]
    D --> F{"Goal Reached?"}
    F -->|"No"| G["Controller Cycle 20Hz"]
    G --> D
    F -->|"Yes"| H["Success"]
    E --> I{"Recovery Successful?"}
    I -->|"Yes"| B
    I -->|"No"| J["Failure"]
```

### 4.4 Topic Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MS["map_server"] -->|"/map"| BT["bt_navigator"]
    LIDAR["/scan"] -->|sensor_msgs/LaserScan| BT
    ODOM["/odom"] -->|nav_msgs/Odometry| BT
    BT -->|"/plan"| RViz["RViz Visualiser"]
    BT -->|"/cmd_vel"| MC["Robot Base"]
    BT -->|"/navigate_to_pose"| BT
    GOAL["RViz 2D Nav Goal"] -->|"/goal_pose"| BT
    AMCL["amcl"] -->|"amcl_pose"| BT
```

> [!TIP]
> **Lab Note (KTU):** To visualise all topics dynamically during the experiment, run in three terminals:
> 1. `ros2 launch turtlebot3_navigation2 navigation2.launch.py` (launches the full stack)
> 2. `rviz2` (set Fixed Frame = `map`, add displays for Map, TF, RobotModel, Path, Costmap)
> 3. `ros2 topic list`, `ros2 node list`, `rqt_graph` (inspect the live graph)
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** 
*List the four mandatory ROS topics that the 2D navigation stack subscribes to for sensor input and publishes for actuator control. State the message type of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Topic Name | Direction | Message Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `/map` | Subscribed (from `map_server`) | `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` |
| 2 | `/scan` | Subscribed (from LIDAR) | `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` |
| 3 | `/odom` | Subscribed (from wheel odometry) | `nav_msgs/Odometry` |
| 4 | `/cmd_vel` | Published (to motor controller) | `geometry_msgs/Twist` |

(½ mark each for topic + direction, ½ mark for message type. Total = 3 marks.)

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of the `amcl` node in the 2D navigation stack? Briefly explain the algorithm it uses.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
`amcl` (Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization) **localises the robot** within a known static map by fusing laser scan data and odometry. It uses a **particle filter**, where each particle represents a hypothesised robot pose $(x, y, \theta)$ with a weight. The algorithm runs in 3 stages: **(1) Prediction** — particles are moved using the motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$, **(2) Update** — weights are recomputed from scan-to-map likelihood $w_t = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$, **(3) Resampling** — low-weight particles are replaced. The pose estimate is the weighted mean. (1 mark for role, 2 marks for algorithm.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*

**(a)** Draw and explain the TF tree required for the 2D navigation stack. Why is `map → odom` published by `amcl` and not by `odometry`? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The required TF tree is `map → odom → base_link → laser_frame` (and other sensors).

| Frame | Published By | Type | Update Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| `map` | World-fixed, origin of static map | Root | Static |
| `odom` | `amcl` (corrected for drift) | Continuous | 10–20 Hz |
| `base_link` | `robot_state_publisher` + odometry | Continuous | 50–100 Hz |
| `laser_frame` | Static transform from URDF | Static | One-shot |

**Why `map → odom` is from `amcl` (3 marks):**
- `amcl` **fuses** laser + odometry + map to correct long-term drift. 
- If `odometry` published `map → odom`, the frame would drift indefinitely due to wheel slip, IMU bias, etc.
- The standard convention is: **`odom` is accurate locally (smooth, continuous) but drifts globally; `map` is accurate globally but jumps discretely when `amcl` re-localises**.
- Therefore `amcl` is the only node that knows "where the robot truly is in the map" and broadcasts the correction.

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of the costmap 2D inflation layer. The inflation radius is 0.6 m and cost scaling factor is 8.0. Compute the cost at a point that is 0.4 m away from the boundary of a circular obstacle of radius 0.3 m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The costmap has 4 layers: Static, Obstacle, Inflation, and (optional) Voxel. The **inflation layer** adds a "buffer zone" around each lethal obstacle using a decaying exponential function:

$$
c(d) = 254 \cdot e^{-\alpha(d - r_i)}
$$

| Parameter | Symbol | Given Value |
|---|---|---|
| Inflation radius | $r_i$ | 0.6 m |
| Cost scaling factor | $\alpha$ | 8.0 |
| Obstacle radius | $r$ | 0.3 m |
| Distance from obstacle boundary | $d_{boundary}$ | 0.4 m |

**Calculation:**
Distance from inflation edge = $d_{boundary} - r_i = 0.4 - 0.6 = -0.2$ m.

Since this is **negative** (point is inside the inflation zone, closer than the inflation radius), the cell is treated as **inscribed / lethal (cost = 254)**. (Full marks = 7.)

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Differentiate between global and local path planning in the 2D navigation stack. Name the algorithms used for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Global Planner | Local Planner |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Static map + current pose + goal | Local costmap + global path segment |
| Frequency | ~1 Hz (re-plan on failure) | ~20 Hz (continuous) |
| Horizon | Full map | 1–3 m prediction window |
| Algorithm examples | Dijkstra (NavFn), A*, Smac Hybrid-A* | DWA, TEB, MPPI, RPP |
| Optimisation | Path length, clearance | Speed, heading, distance to obstacles |
| Output | Sequence of poses (`nav_msgs/Path`) | Velocity `(v, ω)` (`/cmd_vel`) |
| Failure mode | No path exists | Stuck / oscillation |

**Algorithms (2 marks):** Global = NavFn (Dijkstra), Smac Planner (A* / Hybrid A*). Local = DWA, TEB, MPPI, RPP.

**(b)** Explain the DWA (Dynamic Window Approach) algorithm with its objective function. Why is the velocity space restricted to a dynamic window? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

DWA samples velocities, simulates short trajectories, scores them, and picks the best.

**Three velocity spaces (3 marks):**
1. $V_s$ — All reachable velocities by the robot.
2. $V_d$ — Velocities reachable in **one control cycle** $\Delta t$ from current velocity (the "dynamic window"):
$$
V_d = \{ (v, \omega) : v \in [v_a - \dot v \Delta t, v_a + \dot v \Delta t] \cap [v_{min}, v_{max}], \; \omega \in [\omega_a - \dot\omega \Delta t, \omega_a + \dot\omega \Delta t] \cap [\omega_{min}, \omega_{max}] \}
$$
3. $V_a$ — Admissible (collision-free) subset of $V_d$ in prediction horizon.

**Objective function (2 marks):**
$$
G(v, \omega) = \sigma \big( \alpha \cdot \text{heading}(v, \omega) + \beta \cdot \text{dist}(v, \omega) + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity}(v, \omega) \big)
$$

**Why dynamic window restriction (2 marks):**
- **Hardware limits** — Motors cannot instantly jump to any velocity; $V_d$ respects acceleration bounds $\dot v, \dot\omega$.
- **Real-time feasibility** — Sampling full $V_s$ is computationally intractable; $V_d$ is a small, dense, reachable subset.
- **Safety** — Vω outside the window would require a full acceleration cycle, risking collision.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing `map → odom` publisher** — Students write "odometry" instead of "amcl". **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting message types** — KTU expects exact `package/MessageType` format (e.g., `sensor_msgs/LaserScan`). Vague answers like "laser data" get 0 for the message type sub-part.
> 3. **TF chain direction** — Writing `base_link → odom → map` (reversed) — full **−3 marks** in Part B.
> 4. **DWA vs TEB vs MPPI confusion** — Examiner deducts 1 mark for each swapped algorithm.
> 5. **Costmap values** — Writing cost = 100 for inflation. Correct range is **0–254 (or 255 for lethal)**. Inflation is a **continuous decay**, not a binary threshold.
> 6. **Skipping numerical units** — Forgetting "m/s", "rad/s", "Hz" loses ½ mark per omission in Part A.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **TF Tree:** `map → odom → base_link → laser_frame`. `map → odom` is **always** from `amcl`.
- ✅ **Mandatory Topics:** `/map`, `/scan`, `/odom`, `/cmd_vel`, `/goal_pose` (Nav2) or `/move_base_simple/goal` (ROS 1).
- ✅ **Mandatory Nodes:** `map_server`, `amcl`, `planner_server`, `controller_server`, `bt_navigator`, `recovery_server`, `costmap_2d`.
- ✅ **Costmap Value Range:** `0` (free) → `1–99` (inscribed) → `100` (inflated) → `253–254` (lethal) → `255` / `-1` (unknown).
- ✅ **Inflation Formula:** $c(d) = 254 \cdot e^{-\alpha (d - r_i)}$.
- ✅ **A* Optimality:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ with admissible $h$ guarantees shortest path.
- ✅ **DWA Objective:** $G = \sigma(\alpha \cdot \text{heading} + \beta \cdot \text{dist} + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity})$.
- ✅ **DWA Sampling:** Restrict velocities to dynamic window $V_d$ (one-cycle reachable set) for real-time feasibility.
- ✅ **AMCL Algorithm:** Particle filter — Predict → Update (likelihood from scan) → Resample.
- ✅ **Default Rates:** Planner = 1 Hz, Controller = 20 Hz, Costmap = 10 Hz, AMCL = 10 Hz.
- ✅ **Lab Launch Command (Nav2):** `ros2 launch turtlebot3_navigation2 navigation2.launch.py map:=$HOME/map.yaml`.
- ✅ **Costmap Layers:** Static (map), Obstacle (scan), Inflation (decay), Voxel (3D).
- ✅ **Recovery Server** in Nav2 handles: back-up, spin, wait, clear costmap.
- ✅ **Recovery behaviours** must be configured in the behaviour tree XML under `bt_navigator`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic ROS Navigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL607 - ROS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Basic ROS Navigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Basic ROS Navigation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Basic ROS Navigation** refers to the standardized framework within the Robot Operating System (ROS) that enables a mobile robot to autonomously move from a starting pose to a goal pose in a known or partially known environment while avoiding obstacles. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCBRL607 (ROS)**, the *Basic ROS Navigation* experiment is the second module experiment in which students are required to bring up the `navigation_stack` (commonly called the `nav_stack`) of ROS, configure its components on a simulated (Gazebo) or physical differential-drive robot, publish a static map, broadcast sensor data (LaserScan/PointCloud), and finally send a 2D Nav Goal via RViz.

The formal architectural definition of the **ROS Navigation Stack** is a collection of ROS nodes and algorithms that consume sensor data, odometry, and a static map to produce safe velocity commands (`/cmd_vel`) for a mobile base. The stack is built around the central `move_base` node, which acts as a **task scheduler and trajectory server**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the PCBRL607 (ROS) course outcomes, the student must be able to *demonstrate the implementation of a basic navigation pipeline on a simulated robot using the `move_base`, `amcl`, and RViz-based 2D Nav Goal interface*. The "Basic ROS Navigation" experiment specifically tests the ability to launch the stack, set up the **Transform (TF) tree**, configure **costmaps**, and verify goal-reaching behavior.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — How a Human Finds a Destination

Imagine you are a tourist in an unfamiliar city holding a paper map, wearing a smartwatch, and walking through crowded streets:

- The **paper map** is the **static map** (occupancy grid, 2D `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`).
- Your **smartwatch GPS** is the **localization system** (in ROS, this is **AMCL — Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization**), which continuously answers: *"Where am I on the map right now?"*
- The **route from your hotel to the museum that you traced with a marker** is the **global plan** (a long-horizon path computed by `global_planner` using algorithms like **A\*** or **Dijkstra** over the global costmap).
- The **immediate decisions** you make every second to dodge a scooter, step off a curb, or wait for a traffic light are the **local plan** (short-horizon trajectories produced by `local_planner` such as **DWA — Dynamic Window Approach** or **Trajectory Rollout**).
- The **music app on your phone** constantly telling you the *next instruction* is the `move_base` node coordinating the global and local plans and invoking **recovery behaviors** if you get stuck.

> [!NOTE]
> The same way the tourist uses a hierarchy of long-term planning (route) and short-term reactive decisions (dodging), ROS Navigation fuses a **global planner** and a **local planner** to balance goal-directedness with reactive obstacle avoidance.

## 1.3 Engineering Significance and Standard Metrics

Basic ROS Navigation is the *de-facto industry and academic standard* for 2D mobile robot autonomy. Engineering teams at companies like Fetch Robotics, Clearpath, and Robotnik deploy it on warehouse AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots). The performance of the navigation stack is quantified using the following **standard metrics** in ROS:

- **Path length (m):** Total Euclidean distance traveled from start to goal.
- **Path efficiency:** Ratio of optimal path length to actual path length (ideal = **1.0**).
- **Success rate (%):** Percentage of goals reached without manual intervention.
- **Mean time-to-goal (s):** Average completion time per navigation request.
- **Minimum clearance (m):** Smallest distance between the robot footprint and the nearest obstacle during the run.
- **Recovery success rate:** Frequency with which the planner successfully resolves stuck states (typical benchmark: **> 85%**).

> [!TIP]
> **Constants students must memorize for KTU viva/ESE:**
> - Default planner frequency of `local_planner`: **20 Hz** (i.e., replan every **0.05 s**).
> - Default controller frequency of `move_base`: **20 Hz**.
> - Global plan update frequency: **1.0 Hz** (configurable via `planner_frequency` parameter).
> - Default robot footprint inflation radius: **0.55 m** for a TurtleBot3 Burger.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of ROS Navigation

The Basic ROS Navigation stack is fundamentally built on **three input pillars** that must be present for `move_base` to operate:

| # | Pillar | ROS Topic / Source | Message Type | Purpose |
|---|--------|-------------------|--------------|---------|
| 1 | **Map** | `/map` (latched) | `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` | Static a-priori environment. |
