> - **Topic:** Execute AMCL Navigation in a known environment using a differentially driven mobile robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AMCL (Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization)** is a probabilistic localization algorithm for a robot moving in a **2D known environment**, implemented as a ROS node within the `amcl` package. It tracks the pose (position + orientation) of the robot against a pre-built static map using a **particle filter (KLD-sampling variant)**. When fused with the ROS **Navigation Stack** (`move_base`, `global_planner`, `local_planner`, `costmap_2d`, `map_server`), it enables a **differentially driven mobile robot** to autonomously plan and execute a collision-free path from a start pose to a goal pose.

### Key Terminology

- **Differential Drive Robot**: A wheeled mobile robot where two independently driven wheels on a common axis control linear and angular motion by varying their individual velocities.
- **Known Environment**: An environment for which a **2D occupancy grid map** has been pre-generated (using `gmapping`, `cartographer`, or `slam_toolbox`) and stored as a YAML + PGM pair.
- **AMCL**: Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization — fuses laser scan measurements with odometry using a particle filter that **adaptively resamples** based on the Kullback-Leibler Divergence (KLD).
- **Navigation Stack**: The suite of ROS nodes responsible for global path planning, local trajectory generation, and reactive obstacle avoidance.
- **Costmap**: A layered grid map (`static_map`, `obstacles`, `inflation`) used by planners to evaluate traversability.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Blindfolded Person in a Known House"**
>
> Imagine you are blindfolded and placed at a random spot inside a house whose floor plan you have memorized. You cannot see anything, but you can touch walls and feel their distance with a long stick (laser scanner). You also have a rough sense of your own steps (odometry). You keep guessing your position (each guess is a *particle*). Each guess that is "consistent" with where the walls actually are (per the laser) is rewarded; guesses that conflict are discarded. Over time, your guesses converge to your real position. **AMCL is this exact process** — particles = guesses, laser scan = stick, odometry = step count, map = memorized floor plan.
>
> The word **"Adaptive"** means the algorithm automatically changes the number of guesses (particles): few when the robot is confident, many when it is "kidnapped" or lost.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Wheelbase (L)** of a typical TurtleBot3 Burger = **0.160 m**
> - **Wheel radius (r)** of TurtleBot3 Burger = **0.033 m**
> - **Encoder resolution (TPR)** = **4096 ticks/rev** (typical DYNAMIXEL)
> - **Default AMCL particle count range** = **5000 (min)** to **30000 (max)**
> - **Update rate** of AMCL = **5 Hz (default)**
> - **Laser scan rate** of LDS-01 = **5 Hz (typical)**
> - **Inflation radius** for the robot footprint = **0.30 m** (default `costmap_2d`)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Broken Down Step-by-Step

### 2.1.1 Differential Drive Kinematics

A differential drive robot has two wheels (left `L` and right `R`) on a common axle separated by wheelbase `L`. The instantaneous center of rotation (ICR) lies on the axle line.

- The **linear velocity** of the robot equals the average of the two wheel velocities.
- The **angular velocity** arises from the difference in wheel velocities divided by the wheelbase.

### 2.1.2 Odometry Computation (Encoder-based)

Wheel encoders provide tick counts per sampling period. The incremental rotation is converted to wheel displacement `Δs_l` and `Δs_r`, which are then combined to estimate the change in pose.

### 2.1.3 AMCL Algorithm (Particle Filter)

1. **Initialization**: Spread `N` particles uniformly across the free space of the map (or around the initial pose if given).
2. **Prediction (Motion Update)**: Each particle is propagated using the odometry motion model `p(x_t | x_{t-1}, u_t)`.
3. **Measurement Update**: Each particle is weighted by the **likelihood** of the actual laser scan, given the particle's pose and the known map `p(z_t | x_t, m)`.
4. **Resampling (Adaptive KLD)**: Particles with low weight are discarded; high-weight particles are duplicated. The number of active particles is adapted online to keep KL-divergence between the true posterior and sample-based approximation below a threshold `ε`.
5. **Pose Estimate**: The mean (or weighted mean) and covariance of the particle cloud is published as the robot's pose on `/amcl_pose` and the transform `map → odom`.

### 2.1.4 The `move_base` Navigation Stack

`move_base` is the high-level coordinator. It:
- Maintains two **costmaps**: `global_costmap` (built from the static map + sensor data) and `local_costmap` (rolling window of local obstacles).
- Calls the **Global Planner** (`navfn` or `global_planner`) to compute a long-horizon path from start to goal on the global costmap.
- Calls the **Local Planner** (`base_local_planner` / `teb_local_planner` / `dwa_local_planner`) to follow the global path while avoiding dynamic obstacles, publishing velocity commands to `/cmd_vel`.
- **Recovers** from failures using configured recovery behaviors (rotate, clear costmaps).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it is the core of the 14-mark derivations.**

| # | Concept | Equation | Description / Units |
|---|---------|----------|--------------------|
| 1 | Forward Linear Velocity | $v = \dfrac{v_r + v_l}{2}$ | m/s; $v_r$, $v_l$ are wheel tangential speeds |
| 2 | Angular Velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{v_r - v_l}{L}$ | rad/s; $L$ = wheelbase in meters |
| 3 | Wheel Displacement per Tick | $\Delta s = \dfrac{2\pi r}{N_{ticks}}$ | m/tick |
| 4 | Pose Update from Odometry | $x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta s \cos\!\left(\theta_k + \dfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)$ | m |
| 5 | Pose Update from Odometry | $y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta s \sin\!\left(\theta_k + \dfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)$ | m |
| 6 | Pose Update from Odometry | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta\theta$ | rad |
| 7 | Mean of Two Wheel Displacements | $\Delta s = \dfrac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2}$ | m |
| 8 | Differential Rotation | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{L}$ | rad |
| 9 | ICC (Instantaneous Center) | $R = \dfrac{L}{2}\dfrac{v_r + v_l}{v_r - v_l}$ | m; $R \to \infty$ for pure translation |
| 10 | Particle Filter Importance Weight | $w_t^{(i)} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)}, m)$ | dimensionless likelihood |
| 11 | Effective Sample Size | $N_{eff} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(w_t^{(i)})^2}$ | particles |
| 12 | KLD Resampling Threshold | $\epsilon$-bound on KL-divergence | typically $\epsilon = 0.05$ |
| 13 | Laser Scan Likelihood (Gaussian) | $p(z_t^{(k)} \mid x_t, m) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z_t^{(k)} - z_{expected}^{(k)})^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | probability density |
| 14 | Inflation Layer | $d_{inflated} = d_{obstacle} - r_{robot} - d_{inflation}$ | m |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics)** rely on AMCL for pose tracking against a pre-mapped warehouse layout.
> - **Hospital delivery robots** use AMCL + move_base to traverse long, dynamic corridors.
> - **Restaurant service robots** (Pudu, Keenon) localize in restaurants built once and operated daily.
> - **Industrial AMRs** in semiconductor fabs use AMCL with reflective-marker-augmented maps for mm-level docking.
> - **Domestic robot vacuums (Roomba)** use a simplified AMCL variant on a differential drivetrain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Differential Drive Forward Kinematics

### Setup
Let the robot have wheelbase $L$ and wheel radius $r$. Let the angular velocities of the left and right wheels be $\dot{\phi}_l$ and $\dot{\phi}_r$ (rad/s). The tangential velocities of the contact points are:

$$
v_l = r \dot{\phi}_l, \qquad v_r = r \dot{\phi}_r
$$

### Step 1 — Linear Velocity
The robot's frame moves forward at the mean of the two wheel speeds (since both contribute to forward translation):

$$
v = \frac{v_r + v_l}{2} = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_r + \dot{\phi}_l)}{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Angular Velocity
The two wheels trace arcs of different radii. The differential displacement produces rotation about the ICC. By geometry, the angular velocity equals the velocity difference divided by the wheelbase:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_r - v_l}{L} = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_r - \dot{\phi}_l)}{L}
$$

### Step 3 — Inverse Kinematics (for `cmd_vel`)
To command the robot, we invert:

$$
v_r = v + \frac{\omega L}{2}, \qquad v_l = v - \frac{\omega L}{2}
$$

Then convert to wheel angular velocities:

$$
\dot{\phi}_r = \frac{1}{r}\left(v + \frac{\omega L}{2}\right), \qquad \dot{\phi}_l = \frac{1}{r}\left(v - \frac{\omega L}{2}\right)
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Odometry-based Pose Update (Exact Method)

### Setup
At time $k$, the robot pose is $P_k = (x_k, y_k, \theta_k)$. The encoder counts yield incremental wheel displacements $\Delta s_l$ and $\Delta s_r$.

### Step 1 — Average Forward Displacement

$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2}
$$

**Reasoning**: The midpoint of the axle advances by the mean of the two wheel arc-lengths (this is the exact arc-length average, not a linear average — a crucial distinction for board questions).

### Step 2 — Differential Rotation

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{L}
$$

**Reasoning**: The difference in arc lengths divided by axle length equals the small rotation angle.

### Step 3 — Mid-Pose Orientation

$$
\theta_{mid} = \theta_k + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}
$$

**Reasoning**: Using the midpoint orientation as the heading for the chord reduces odometric drift for curved paths.

### Step 4 — Position Update (Chord Approximation)

$$
x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta s \cdot \cos\!\left(\theta_k + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)
$$

$$
y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta s \cdot \sin\!\left(\theta_k + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)
$$

$$
\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta\theta
$$

### Step 5 — Special Cases
- **Pure Translation** ($v_r = v_l$): $\Delta\theta = 0$, so $x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta s \cos\theta_k$ and $y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta s \sin\theta_k$.
- **Pure Rotation** ($v_r = -v_l$): $\Delta s = 0$, so $x_{k+1} = x_k$ and $y_{k+1} = y_k$ and $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta\theta$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Numerical Worked Example (Board-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> A TurtleBot3 has wheelbase $L = 0.16$ m and wheel radius $r = 0.033$ m. In one sampling period, the left wheel encoder gives $\Delta s_l = 0.020$ m and the right wheel gives $\Delta s_r = 0.024$ m. If the previous pose is $P_k = (1.000, 2.000, 0.000)$ rad, compute the new pose $P_{k+1}$.

### Solution

**Step A** — Average displacement:

$$
\Delta s = \frac{0.024 + 0.020}{2} = 0.022 \text{ m}
$$

**Step B** — Differential rotation:

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{0.024 - 0.020}{0.16} = \frac{0.004}{0.16} = 0.025 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step C** — Mid-pose heading:

$$
\theta_{mid} = 0.000 + \frac{0.025}{2} = 0.0125 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step D** — New pose:

$$
x_{k+1} = 1.000 + 0.022 \cdot \cos(0.0125) \approx 1.000 + 0.022 \cdot 0.99992 \approx 1.02199 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y_{k+1} = 2.000 + 0.022 \cdot \sin(0.0125) \approx 2.000 + 0.022 \cdot 0.01250 \approx 2.000275 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\theta_{k+1} = 0.000 + 0.025 = 0.025 \text{ rad}
$$

**Result**: $P_{k+1} \approx (1.02199,\ 2.000275,\ 0.025)$ — a 2.2 cm advance with a 1.43° rightward turn.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — AMCL Particle Filter Update (Conceptual)

### Step 1 — Sampling Importance Resampling (SIR) Loop

$$
\overline{\text{Bel}}(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\,\text{Bel}(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}
$$

### Step 2 — Discrete Particle Approximation

For $N$ particles with importance weights $w_t^{(i)}$:

$$
x_t^{(i)} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_t), \qquad w_t^{(i)} = w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)}, m)
$$

### Step 3 — Normalize and Resample

$$
\tilde{w}_t^{(i)} = \frac{w_t^{(i)}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_t^{(j)}}, \qquad N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\tilde{w}_t^{(i)})^2}
$$

If $N_{eff} < N_{threshold}$, perform **stochastic universal sampling** to draw $N$ new particles with replacement proportional to $\tilde{w}_t^{(i)}$.

### Step 4 — Adaptive KLD Sampling
Maintain a histogram grid of the particle distribution. Add new particles only as long as the KL-divergence between the empirical and the true posterior stays below $\epsilon$. This **adapts** the number of particles online — hence the "A" in AMCL.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Node-to-Node Information Flow

| Stage | ROS Node / Topic | Message Type | Frequency | Source → Sink |
|-------|------------------|--------------|-----------|---------------|
| Map Loading | `map_server` → `/map` | `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` | Latched (once) | Static YAML + PGM |
| Odometry | `robot_state_publisher`, `diff_drive_controller` → `/odom` | `nav_msgs/Odometry` | 50 Hz | Wheels → Base |
| Laser Scan | LDS-01 driver → `/scan` | `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` | 5 Hz | Sensor |
| Localization | `amcl` → `/amcl_pose` | `geometry_msgs/PoseWithCovarianceStamped` | 5 Hz (default) | `/scan` + `/map` + `/odom` |
| TF (odom→base_footprint) | `diff_drive_controller` → `/tf` | `tf2_msgs/TFMessage` | 50 Hz | Joint states |
| TF (map→odom) | `amcl` → `/tf` | `tf2_msgs/TFMessage` | 5 Hz | AMCL correction |
| Velocity Command | `move_base` → `/cmd_vel` | `geometry_msgs/Twist` | 20 Hz | Local planner → Robot base |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Navigation Stack Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[map_server] -->|nav_msgs OccupancyGrid| C1[global_costmap]
    A1[map_server] -->|static map| B1[amcl node]
    A2[diff_drive_controller] -->|nav_msgs Odometry| B1[amcl node]
    A3[LaserScan LDS-01] -->|sensor_msgs LaserScan| B1[amcl node]
    A3[LaserScan LDS-01] -->|sensor_msgs LaserScan| C2[local_costmap]
    A2[diff_drive_controller] -->|nav_msgs Odometry| C2[local_costmap]
    B1[amcl node] -->|tf map to odom| D1[tf tree]
    A2[diff_drive_controller] -->|tf odom to base| D1[tf tree]
    C1[global_costmap] --> E1[move_base global_planner]
    C2[local_costmap] --> E2[move_base local_planner]
    F1[2D Nav Goal RViz] -->|actionlib goal| E1[move_base global_planner]
    E1[move_base global_planner] --> G1[move_base coordinator]
    E2[move_base local_planner] --> G1[move_base coordinator]
    G1[move_base coordinator] -->|geometry_msgs Twist| A4[diff_drive_controller]
    B1[amcl node] -->|pose with covariance| H1[RViz Pose Array]
    G1[move_base coordinator] -->|actionlib feedback| H1[RViz status]
```

## 4.2 AMCL Particle Filter Loop (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Init Particles on Map] --> S2[Receive Odometry u_t]
    S2 --> S3[Sample New Poses from Motion Model]
    S3 --> S4[Receive Laser Scan z_t]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Likelihood Weight for Each Particle]
    S5 --> S6[Compute Effective Sample Size Neff]
    S6 --> S7{Is Neff less than N threshold?}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Resample with KLD Adaptation]
    S7 -- No --> S9[Keep Particles]
    S8 --> S10[Publish Pose Estimate]
    S9 --> S10[Publish Pose Estimate]
    S10 --> S2[Receive Odometry u_t]
```

## 4.3 Experiment Execution Flow (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Step 1 Launch Gazebo with known world] --> P2[Step 2 Launch robot state publisher and diff drive controller]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Launch map server with saved map yaml]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Launch amcl node with localization config]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Launch move base with navigation config]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Open RViz with robot model and map]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Set initial pose using 2D Pose Estimate]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 Set 2D Nav Goal in RViz]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9 Observe global and local plan in RViz]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10 Robot drives to goal using cmd vel]
    P10 --> P11[Step 11 Verify amcl pose convergence and recovery]
```

## 4.4 TF Tree (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T1[map] -->|amcl correction| T2[odom]
    T2 -->|diff drive controller| T3[base_footprint]
    T3 -->|static joint| T4[wheel_left]
    T3 -->|static joint| T5[wheel_right]
    T3 -->|static joint| T6[base_scan LDS-01]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define AMCL. Why is it termed "Adaptive" in the context of mobile robot localization?**
`[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`

**Model Answer**:
- AMCL stands for **Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization**. It is a probabilistic algorithm that estimates a robot's pose (x, y, θ) within a known 2D occupancy grid map using a **particle filter**.
- It is termed "Adaptive" because it dynamically adjusts the number of particles used to represent the robot's belief. The number of particles is increased when the robot is uncertain (e.g., during global localization or kidnapping) and decreased when the robot is confident in its pose. This adaptation is achieved through **KLD-sampling** (Kullback-Leibler Divergence based sampling), which bounds the statistical error between the true posterior and the sample-based approximation by a threshold `ε`.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q: List the main ROS nodes and topics involved in executing AMCL navigation on a differential drive robot.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer**:
| Node | Purpose | Key Topic(s) |
|------|---------|--------------|
| `map_server` | Loads the saved static map | `/map` (OccupancyGrid) |
| `robot_state_publisher` | Publishes robot's URDF TF tree | `/tf` |
| `diff_drive_controller` | Drives the wheels, publishes odometry | `/odom`, `/cmd_vel` |
| `amcl` | Localizes the robot using particle filter | `/amcl_pose`, `/tf` (map→odom) |
| `move_base` | Coordinates global + local planning | `/move_base/goal` (action) |
| `global_planner` (e.g., `navfn`) | Long-range path on global costmap | `/move_base/GlobalPlan` |
| `local_planner` (e.g., `DWA`) | Reactive trajectory + velocity command | `/cmd_vel` |
| `costmap_2d` | Maintains costmaps | `/global_costmap`, `/local_costmap` |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the differential drive kinematic equations relating wheel velocities to robot's linear and angular velocity. Show the inverse mapping used to compute wheel commands from a desired `(v, ω)`.
**(b) [7 Marks]** With a numerical example, derive the exact odometry-based pose update equations for a differential drive robot, including the chord approximation.

`[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Wheelbase and Wheel Geometry**:
Let $L$ be the wheelbase (distance between two drive wheels) and $r$ the wheel radius. The wheels have angular velocities $\dot{\phi}_l$ and $\dot{\phi}_r$ in rad/s. The tangential (linear) velocities at the contact points are:

$$
v_l = r\,\dot{\phi}_l, \qquad v_r = r\,\dot{\phi}_r
$$

**2. Forward Linear Velocity**: The robot's center moves forward at the mean of the two wheel speeds:

$$
v = \frac{v_r + v_l}{2} = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_r + \dot{\phi}_l)}{2}
$$

**3. Angular Velocity**: The differential in wheel speeds rotates the robot about the ICC. The rotation rate equals velocity difference divided by wheelbase:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_r - v_l}{L} = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_r - \dot{\phi}_l)}{L}
$$

**4. Inverse Mapping (Forward Kinematics Inverse)**: Solving the two equations for $v_r$ and $v_l$:

$$
v_r = v + \frac{\omega L}{2}, \qquad v_l = v - \frac{\omega L}{2}
$$

Converting to wheel angular velocities:

$$
\dot{\phi}_r = \frac{1}{r}\!\left(v + \frac{\omega L}{2}\right), \qquad \dot{\phi}_l = \frac{1}{r}\!\left(v - \frac{\omega L}{2}\right)
$$

**5. Special Cases**:
- Pure translation: $v_r = v_l = v$, $\omega = 0$.
- Pure rotation: $v_r = -v_l = \omega L / 2$, $v = 0$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**1. Encoder-derived Incremental Displacements**: From encoder ticks $N_l$, $N_r$ and resolution $TPR$:

$$
\Delta s_l = \frac{2\pi r}{TPR}\,N_l, \qquad \Delta s_r = \frac{2\pi r}{TPR}\,N_r
$$

**2. Mean Displacement and Differential Rotation**:

$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2}, \qquad \Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{L}
$$

**3. Exact Pose Update (Chord Approximation)**:

$$
x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta s \cdot \cos\!\left(\theta_k + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)
$$

$$
y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta s \cdot \sin\!\left(\theta_k + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)
$$

$$
\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta\theta
$$

**4. Numerical Example**:
Given $L = 0.16$ m, $r = 0.033$ m, $\Delta s_l = 0.020$ m, $\Delta s_r = 0.024$ m, and $P_k = (1.000,\ 2.000,\ 0.000)$ rad:

$$
\Delta s = \frac{0.020 + 0.024}{2} = 0.022 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{0.024 - 0.020}{0.16} = 0.025 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\theta_{mid} = 0.000 + \frac{0.025}{2} = 0.0125 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
x_{k+1} = 1.000 + 0.022 \cdot \cos(0.0125) = 1.000 + 0.022 \cdot 0.99992 \approx 1.02199 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y_{k+1} = 2.000 + 0.022 \cdot \sin(0.0125) = 2.000 + 0.022 \cdot 0.01250 \approx 2.00028 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\theta_{k+1} = 0.000 + 0.025 = 0.025 \text{ rad}
$$

**Result**: $P_{k+1} = (1.02199,\ 2.00028,\ 0.025)$ rad. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the ROS Navigation Stack architecture for AMCL-based navigation. Identify the role of each node and the TF tree involved.
**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the AMCL particle filter algorithm step-by-step. Why is the algorithm called "Adaptive" and what is KLD sampling?

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Architecture Overview**:
The Navigation Stack consists of the following cooperating ROS nodes (refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1):

- **`map_server`**: Loads the pre-built occupancy grid (YAML + PGM) and publishes it on `/map`.
- **`amcl`**: Subscribes to `/scan` (laser), `/odom`, and `/map`; publishes the correction transform `map → odom` and pose estimate on `/amcl_pose`.
- **`move_base`**: Hosts the **Global Planner** (`navfn`/`global_planner`) and **Local Planner** (`dwa_local_planner`/`teb_local_planner`), along with the `costmap_2d` and recovery behaviors. It accepts a navigation goal via actionlib and outputs `/cmd_vel`.
- **`diff_drive_controller`**: A `ros_control` hardware interface that converts `/cmd_vel` (Twist) into individual wheel velocities, and publishes wheel encoder odometry on `/odom`.
- **`robot_state_publisher`**: Uses the URDF to publish static TFs (`base_footprint → wheels`, `base_footprint → laser`).

**2. TF Tree** (Section 4.4):

$$
\text{map} \xrightarrow{\text{amcl}} \text{odom} \xrightarrow{\text{diff\_drive\_controller}} \text{base\_footprint} \rightarrow \text{wheels, laser}
$$

**3. Data Flow**:
- `/scan` + `/odom` + `/map` → `amcl` → TF correction
- `/map` + `/scan` + `/odom` → `costmap_2d` → planners
- `2D Nav Goal` (RViz) → `move_base` → `/cmd_vel` → robot base.

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**1. Particle Filter Concept**:
AMCL represents the robot's belief $\text{Bel}(x_t)$ as a set of weighted samples (particles) $\{x_t^{(i)}, w_t^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$, where $x_t^{(i)} = (x, y, \theta)_t^{(i)}$ is a candidate pose and $w_t^{(i)}$ is its importance weight.

**2. Step-by-Step Algorithm**:

**Step 1 — Initialization**: Particles are drawn either uniformly from the free space of the map (global localization) or from a Gaussian around the initial pose estimate (with known initial position).

**Step 2 — Motion Update (Prediction)**: On receiving odometry $u_t$, each particle is sampled from the motion model:

$$
x_t^{(i)} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_t)
$$

A common motion model is the **odometry motion model** with Gaussian noise on translation $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ and rotation $\alpha_3, \alpha_4$ parameters.

**Step 3 — Measurement Update (Weighting)**: For each particle, compute the expected laser scan `z_expected^{(k)}` via ray-casting in the map, then score the actual scan `z_t^{(k)}` against it using a Gaussian likelihood:

$$
w_t^{(i)} = w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{K} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z_t^{(k)} - z_{expected}^{(k)})^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Normalization**:

$$
\tilde{w}_t^{(i)} = \frac{w_t^{(i)}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_t^{(j)}}
$$

**Step 5 — Adaptive Resampling (KLD)**: Compute the effective sample size:

$$
N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\tilde{w}_t^{(i)})^2}
$$

If $N_{eff} < N_{threshold}$, draw $N$ new particles by **stochastic universal sampling** (replacement proportional to weight).

**3. Why "Adaptive"?**:
The "A" stands for **KLD-sampling (Kullback-Leibler Divergence sampling)**. The number of active particles is **not fixed**. Instead, particles are added as long as the KL-divergence between the empirical sample-based distribution and the true posterior exceeds a threshold `ε`. When the robot is confident (particles are tightly clustered), the algorithm needs few particles. When the robot is uncertain (kidnapped, moving through an ambiguous corridor), many particles are spawned. This minimizes computational cost while bounding statistical error.

**4. Pose Output**: The final pose estimate is the weighted mean of the particle cloud:

$$
\hat{x}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{w}_t^{(i)} \cdot x_t^{(i)}
$$

**(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing `v` and `v_r`/`v_l`**: The robot's linear velocity $v$ is the *mean* of wheel velocities, not the sum. Many students write $v = v_r + v_l$ and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the mid-pose chord in odometry**: Using $\theta_k$ (not $\theta_k + \Delta\theta/2$) inside $\cos/\sin$ is the **most common** error in 7-mark derivations. It still gives partial credit but caps your marks at 5/7.
> 3. **Mixing up the TF tree**: Students frequently write `odom → map` instead of `map → odom`. The amcl node publishes the correction from `map` to `odom`, not the other way. This is a 1-mark trap.
> 4. **Forgetting the encoder-tick-to-displacement conversion**: Board questions frequently provide **tick counts** and expect the $\Delta s = 2\pi r \cdot N / TPR$ step.
> 5. **AMCL needs `/scan` AND `/odom`**: A common lab exam pitfall is launching AMCL without the laser driver — students then report that AMCL "doesn't work". The `amcl` node fuses both streams.
> 6. **Units**: $\Delta\theta$ must be in **radians** when passed to $\cos$/$\sin$ — forgetting to convert from degrees costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**
>
> - **AMCL = Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization**, a particle filter that localizes a robot in a known map.
> - **Adaptive** = KLD-sampling dynamically resizes the particle set.
> - **Particle filter loop**: Init → Predict (motion model) → Weight (scan likelihood) → Resample → Estimate.
> - **Differential drive linear velocity** $v = (v_r + v_l)/2$.
> - **Differential drive angular velocity** $\omega = (v_r - v_l)/L$.
> - **Inverse mapping**: $v_r = v + \omega L / 2$, $v_l = v - \omega L / 2$.
> - **Wheel displacement per encoder tick**: $\Delta s = 2\pi r / TPR$.
> - **Pose update (chord method)**: use $\theta_k + \Delta\theta/2$ in the $\cos$/$\sin$ arguments.
> - **TF tree** is `map → odom → base_footprint → wheels/laser`. AMCL publishes the first link.
> - **Required ROS topics**: `/map` (OccupancyGrid), `/scan` (LaserScan), `/odom` (Odometry), `/cmd_vel` (Twist), `/amcl_pose`, `/tf`.
> - **Required nodes**: `map_server`, `amcl`, `move_base`, `global_planner`, `local_planner`, `costmap_2d`, `diff_drive_controller`, `robot_state_publisher`.
> - **2D Nav Goal** in RViz is sent as an actionlib goal to `/move_base`.
> - **Inflation layer** keeps the robot footprint at a safe distance from obstacles (default ~0.30 m).
> - **Recovery behaviors** in `move_base`: rotate recovery, clear costmap.
> - **Special cases**: $v_r = v_l$ → pure translation; $v_r = -v_l$ → pure rotation (in-place pivot).
> - **Lab execution order**: Gazebo world → robot state publisher → map server → amcl → move_base → RViz → set initial pose → set 2D Nav Goal.
> - **Default AMCL frequencies**: 5 Hz for pose, 5 Hz for laser, 50 Hz for odom (in simulation).
> - **Particle cloud visualisation** in RViz: `PoseArray` topic (subscribed to `amcl` with `~/visualize_tesselated_poses` enabled or a separate `amcl` publish).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarise ROS Serial Arduino for hardware interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL607 - ROS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarise ROS Serial Arduino for hardware interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**ROS Serial (rosserial)** is the standard ROS (Robot Operating System) communication protocol package that enables bidirectional, packet-based message exchange between a ROS master node (running on a host PC, embedded board like Raspberry Pi/Jetson, or laptop) and low-level microcontroller-based hardware platforms (Arduino, STM32, ESP32, etc.) over a **serial UART/USB interface**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme (PCBRL607 – Robot Operating System), this experiment familiarises the student with the `rosserial_arduino` library, which provides a hardware abstraction layer allowing microcontrollers to become first-class ROS nodes — capable of **publishing**, **subscribing**, and serving **Services** within the ROS computation graph.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module Experiment 2):**
> The student must demonstrate:
> 1. Installing `rosserial` package on the host machine.
> 2. Generating `ros_lib` for the Arduino IDE.
> 3. Writing a publisher node in Arduino C++ to read a sensor.
> 4. Writing a subscriber node in Arduino C++ to control an actuator (LED/Motor).
> 5. Launching `rosserial_python` serial_node.py to bridge hardware with the ROS ecosystem.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine ROS as a **highly organised corporate office** where every department (node) communicates through a central **PABX telephone exchange** (the ROS Master) using a common language (ROS messages over TCP/IP).

Now imagine an **Arduino board** is a small field worker stationed in a remote construction site. It has no direct internet connection, no Wi-Fi, and no knowledge of the corporate language. It only has a **walkie-talkie** (the USB serial port).

**ROS Serial** is the **translator-cum-radio-operator** sitting at the walkie-talkie. It:

- Translates the field worker's simple instructions (sensor readings, motor speeds) into the corporate message format.
- Speaks those messages over the walkie-talkie at a fixed baud rate (commonly **57600 bps** or **115200 bps**).
- Listens to corporate instructions and converts them back into hardware pin voltages.

Without this translator, the field worker and the office cannot cooperate. With it, the Arduino becomes a fully recognised ROS node (`/arduino`) talking on topics like `/led_state`, `/temperature`, `/cmd_motor`.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Block

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Vocabulary for Board Exam:**
>
> - **Node:** An executable that uses ROS to communicate with other nodes.
> - **Master:** The ROS name service (roscore) — registers and looks up names.
> - **Topic:** Named bus over which nodes exchange **Messages** (asynchronous, many-to-many).
> - **Message:** Strictly typed data structure (`.msg` file) — `Int32`, `Float32`, `String`, etc.
> - **Publisher:** Code object that sends messages to a topic.
> - **Subscriber:** Code object that receives messages from a topic, calling a **callback function** upon arrival.
> - **Baud Rate:** Symbols per second transmitted over serial — must match on both Arduino and host (default rosserial = **57600**).
> - **ros_lib:** Arduino library generated by `rosserial_arduino` that allows sketches to `#include <ros.h>`.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The rosserial Communication Protocol (Why & How)

The rosserial protocol is fundamentally a **synchronised, framed, binary protocol** operating over a byte-oriented serial stream. To understand it deeply, we break it into logical stages:

- **Why a special protocol?** ROS natively uses **TCP/UDP over IP**. Microcontrollers lack a full network stack. We need a lightweight framing protocol robust enough to recover from USB dropouts and noise.
- **How does it work?** The protocol uses a **synchronisation byte (0xFF)** to mark the start of every packet, followed by a header describing the message length, topic ID, and checksum.

#### 2.1.1 Packet Frame Structure

Each rosserial packet is composed of the following fields transmitted sequentially over UART:

1. **SYNC Byte (1 byte):** Fixed value `0xFF` — used by the receiver to re-align to the start of a packet if a byte is dropped.
2. **Version (1 byte):** Protocol version (`0x0F` = 15 for most rosserial releases).
3. **Message Length (2 bytes):** Little-endian unsigned 16-bit integer denoting the length of the message body (in bytes).
4. **Checksum (1 byte):** The lower 8 bits of the sum of all body bytes (255 − sum). Used for lightweight error detection.
5. **Topic ID (2 bytes):** Identifier assigned during runtime handshake for each topic/service.
6. **Message Body (variable):** Serialised ROS message (using CDR-like encoding, no nested type names).
7. **CRC-16 (2 bytes):** Optional 16-bit cyclic redundancy check (when enabled).

#### 2.1.2 Synchronisation & Handshake

When the host-side `rosserial_python` node starts, it does **not** immediately know which topics the Arduino intends to publish or subscribe to. A **multi-stage handshake** occurs:

1. The Arduino advertises its `node name`, topics, message types, and services via a special **Topic Info Message** (`TopicID = 0`, type `Rosserial_Python_Test_Header`).
2. The host computes a **topic-ID hash** for each advertised topic and replies with an ID assignment.
3. Once IDs are agreed, normal data packets flow bidirectionally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this handshake critical for exams?**
> Because the `rosserial_python` launch file typically pauses for **5–10 seconds** to allow this handshake. Students often mistakenly believe their Arduino is "dead" when it is merely waiting for the host to negotiate topic IDs.

### 2.2 Hardware Architecture

The interfacing experiment typically uses the following components:

- **Host PC / SBC:** Runs Ubuntu + ROS Noetic/Humble. Executes `roscore`, `rosserial_python`, and visualisation (`rqt_graph`, `rostopic`).
- **USB-UART Bridge:** Either the Arduino's onboard ATmega16U2 (Uno) or CH340/CP2102 (Nano/clone).
- **Microcontroller:** ATmega328P (Arduino Uno/Nano) at 16 MHz.
- **Sensors:** Potentiometer, LM35, HC-SR04 ultrasonic, DHT11, etc.
- **Actuators:** LED, DC motor via L293D, servo motor.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Value | Formula / Rule | Unit | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Serial Baud Rate | $B$ | $B \in \{9600, 57600, 115200\}$ | bits/sec | Throughput ceiling for rosserial link |
| Transmission Time per Byte | $t_b$ | $t_b = \dfrac{1}{B}$ with start+stop+data bits | seconds | Determines max publish frequency |
| Throughput per Packet | $R_p$ | $R_p = \dfrac{L_{msg}}{L_{msg} + L_{overhead}} \cdot B$ | bits/sec | Overhead of 10-byte header reduces efficiency |
| Maximum Publish Frequency | $f_{max}$ | $f_{max} \approx \dfrac{B}{10 \cdot L_{msg}}$ | Hz | Sensor sampling limit over serial |
| ADC Resolution | $N_{ADC}$ | $N_{ADC} = 10$ (Arduino Uno) | bits | Quantisation of analog sensor input |
| ADC Voltage Conversion | $V_{in}$ | $V_{in} = \dfrac{A_{read}}{2^{N_{ADC}} - 1} \cdot V_{ref}$ | Volts | Converts digital reading to physical units |
| PWM Duty Cycle | $D$ | $D = \dfrac{t_{on}}{T_{period}}$ | dimensionless | Drives LED brightness / motor speed |
| Angular Velocity from PWM | $\omega$ | $\omega \approx k \cdot D$ | rad/sec | Linear approximation for DC motor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** At the default baud rate $B = 57600$ bits/sec and an 8-byte payload, the maximum theoretical publish frequency is roughly $f_{max} \approx \dfrac{57600}{10 \cdot (8 + 10)} \approx 320$ Hz. In practice, message negotiation and ATmega328P processing cap this at **~50–100 Hz**, which is sufficient for almost all educational robotics experiments.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Analysis & Algorithm Flow

Since this experiment is procedural and embedded in nature (not a closed-form mathematical derivation), the equivalent of an "analytical derivation" is the **end-to-end communication algorithm** and the **pin-by-pin wiring logic**. Every step below is exhaustive, mirroring the analytical rigour expected in a 14-mark KTU lab answer.

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Installation Procedure (Host-Side)

**Step 1: Install the rosserial package on the Ubuntu host.**

The student must execute the following sequence on the Linux terminal:

```bash
sudo apt-get install ros-noetic-rosserial ros-noetic-rosserial-arduino
```

**Why?** The Debian package pulls in `rosserial_python` (the host-side bridge), `rosserial_msgs` (custom message types), and `rosserial_arduino` (the source generator for `ros_lib`).

**Step 2: Source the ROS environment.**

```bash
source /opt/ros/noetic/setup.bash
```

This appends the ROS package paths to `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`, allowing the subsequent commands to find rosserial.

**Step 3: Generate the `ros_lib` Arduino library.**

```bash
cd ~/Arduino/libraries
rosrun rosserial_arduino make_libraries.py .
```

**Why?** The generator traverses all ROS messages installed on the host and emits Arduino-compatible C++ headers inside a folder named `ros_lib`. Without this, `#include <ros.h>` will fail in the Arduino IDE.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Arduino-Side Code Structure (Publisher Example — Sensor Reading)

```cpp
#include <ros.h>
#include <std_msgs/Float32.h>

ros::NodeHandle nh;
std_msgs::Float32 sensor_msg;
ros::Publisher pub("temperature", &sensor_msg);

void setup() {
  nh.initNode();
  nh.advertise(pub);
}

void loop() {
  int raw = analogRead(A0);
  sensor_msg.data = (raw * 5.0) / 1023.0 * 100.0;  // LM35: 10 mV/°C
  pub.publish(&sensor_msg);
  nh.spinOnce();
  delay(100);
}
```

**Step-by-Step Conversion Logic:**

- `analogRead(A0)` returns an integer in $\{0, 1, \dots, 1023\}$ corresponding to voltages in $[0\,\text{V}, 5\,\text{V}]$.
- The expression $\dfrac{\text{raw} \cdot 5.0}{1023.0}$ converts the digital count to a voltage in **Volts**.
- Multiplying by **100.0** converts the LM35's 10 mV/°C transfer function to **degrees Celsius** ($1\,\text{V} = 100\,^\circ\text{C}$).
- `nh.spinOnce()` is the **non-blocking equivalent** of ROS's `ros::spin()`. Because Arduino has no preemptive threading, the loop must explicitly yield to the ROS publisher periodically to flush buffers.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Arduino-Side Code Structure (Subscriber Example — LED Control)

```cpp
#include <ros.h>
#include <std_msgs/Int32.h>

ros::NodeHandle nh;
const int LED_PIN = 13;

void led_cb(const std_msgs::Int32& msg) {
  if (msg.data == 1) digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH);
  else               digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW);
}

ros::Subscriber<std_msgs::Int32> sub("led_cmd", &led_cb);

void setup() {
  pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);
  nh.initNode();
  nh.subscribe(sub);
}

void loop() {
  nh.spinOnce();
  delay(10);
}
```

**Step-by-Step Conversion Logic:**

- A `ros::Subscriber` is instantiated with the **template parameter** `<std_msgs::Int32>` declaring the expected message type.
- The callback `led_cb(const std_msgs::Int32& msg)` is invoked **automatically** by `nh.spinOnce()` whenever a new message arrives on `/led_cmd`.
- Note the use of a **const reference** (`&`) — this avoids copying the message body, saving both flash RAM and CPU cycles on the ATmega328P.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Host-Side Launch Procedure

After uploading the Arduino sketch, execute on the host:

```bash
roscore
rosrun rosserial_python serial_node.py /dev/ttyUSB0 _baud:=57600
```

In another terminal, verify the data flow:

```bash
rostopic list
rostopic echo /temperature
rosrun rqt_graph rqt_graph
```

**Why each command matters:**

- `roscore` starts the **ROS Master** (the registrar of all node names).
- `serial_node.py` opens the serial port at **57600 baud**, performs the handshake, and relays packets into the ROS graph.
- `rqt_graph` renders a **live node-topic graph** — the visual proof that the Arduino is a recognised ROS node.

### 3.5 Analytical Algorithm Flow

The complete algorithmic flow of one message publication (sensor → topic → subscriber) is captured below:

$$
\text{Read ADC} \;\rightarrow\; V_{in} = \frac{A_{read}}{1023} \cdot V_{ref} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Scale (LM35 / SHH)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Pack msg} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Frame (0xFF + len + ID + body + CRC)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{UART TX} \;\rightarrow\; \text{USB} \;\rightarrow\; \text{serial\_node.py decode} \;\rightarrow\; \text{ROSCore publish}
$$

### 3.6 Common Failure Modes & Diagnostic Equations

| Symptom | Probable Cause | Diagnostic Command |
|---|---|---|
| No topics advertised | Baud mismatch or `ros_lib` outdated | `rosrun rosserial_python serial_node.py _baud:=9600` |
| Frequent disconnects | USB cable too long / EMI | Test with shorter cable, add `delay(10)` in loop |
| Garbled data | Wrong serial port selected | `ls /dev/ttyUSB*` or `/dev/ttyACM*` |
| `ros.h not found` | `ros_lib` not regenerated after ROS upgrade | Re-run `make_libraries.py` |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **end-to-end data path** between a sensor on the Arduino and a ROS subscriber on the host, including every protocol transformation layer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensorA["Analog Sensor LM35"] --> adcA["ATmega328P ADC 10 bit"]
    adcA --> scaleA["Scaling V to deg C"]
    scaleA --> packA["Pack std_msgs Float32"]
    packA --> frameA["rosserial Framing 0xFF + Header + Body + CRC"]
    frameA --> uartA["UART TX 57600 bps"]
    uartA --> usbA["USB to UART Bridge CH340"]
    usbA --> hostA["Host Linux /dev/ttyUSB0"]

    hostA --> decA["serial_node.py Decode"]
    decA --> masterA["ROS Master TCP XMLRPC"]
    masterA --> pubA["Publish to topic temperature"]

    pubA --> echoA["rostopic echo"]
    pubA --> graphA["rqt_graph visualisation"]
    pubA --> subA["Subscriber Callback on host"]

    subgraph ArduinoSide["Arduino Side"]
        sensorA
        adcA
        scaleA
        packA
        frameA
        uartA
    end

    subgraph HostSide["Host PC with ROS"]
        usbA
        hostA
        decA
        masterA
        pubA
        echoA
        graphA
        subA
    end
```

### 4.2 Topic Flow — Bidirectional Publisher + Subscriber

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates **simultaneous bidirectional flow** when the Arduino both publishes a sensor reading and subscribes to a command topic (the classic closed-loop LED control experiment).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    arduinoNode["Arduino Node /arduino"] -->|Publish temperature| tempTopic["Topic /temperature Float32"]
    tempTopic --> pcSub["Host Subscriber Python rqt_plot"]

    pcPub["Host Publisher rostopic pub"] -->|Publish led_cmd| ledTopic["Topic /led_cmd Int32"]
    ledTopic --> arduinoNode

    arduinoNode --> ledPin["Digital Pin 13 LED ON or OFF"]

    classDef hardware fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef topic fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef host fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    class arduinoNode,ledPin hardware
    class tempTopic,ledTopic topic
    class pcSub,pcPub host
```

### 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Handshake + Steady-State

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Arduino
    participant H as serial_node.py
    participant M as ROS Master
    participant S as Host Subscriber

    A->>H: Topic Info (publisher + subscriber list)
    H->>M: Register node /arduino
    M-->>H: Acknowledge
    H->>A: Topic ID assignments
    Note over A,H: Handshake Complete

    loop Every 100 ms
        A->>H: Publish /temperature Float32
        H->>M: Re-publish to graph
        M->>S: Deliver to subscriber callback
    end

    H->>A: Publish /led_cmd Int32 (from host)
    A->>A: Callback updates Pin 13
```

### 4.4 Physical Wiring Schematic (LM35 + LED on Arduino Uno)

```
                Arduino Uno
              +---------------+
              |               |
              |          D13  |----[ 220 Ω ]----[ LED ]---- GND
              |               |
              |          A0   |----[ LM35 OUT pin ]----+
              |               |                        |
              |          5V   |------------------------+
              |               |
              |          GND  |------------------------+
              +---------------+

USB Cable (Type-B) from Arduino to Host PC /dev/ttyUSB0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** The LM35's three pins are `+Vs`, `OUT`, `GND`. Connect `+Vs` → 5 V, `GND` → GND, `OUT` → A0. The LED is connected in series with a current-limiting resistor of **220 Ω** between digital pin 13 and ground. The USB cable simultaneously powers the Arduino (no external adapter needed for small loads).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Forgetting to regenerate `ros_lib` after upgrading ROS or installing new message packages → `ros.h: No such file`.
> 2. Confusing `ros::Publisher` with `ros::Subscriber` — remember, a **Publisher advertises**, a **Subscriber subscribes**.
> 3. Writing `ros::spin()` instead of `nh.spinOnce()` inside `loop()` — Arduino has no RTOS, this will hang the sketch.
> 4. Baud rate mismatch between `serial_node.py _baud:=` and `Serial.begin()` — the most common reason for "no topics visible".
> 5. Not specifying `/dev/ttyUSB0` (or `/dev/ttyACM0`) explicitly — the script will hang on "Opening serial port".

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]`
**Define the term "rosserial" and state two of its advantages over running ROS natively on a microcontroller.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Rosserial is a ROS protocol that allows microcontrollers (like Arduino) to communicate with a ROS host over a serial UART/USB connection by acting as lightweight ROS nodes.
- **Advantage 1 (1 mark):** Eliminates the need for a full TCP/IP stack on resource-constrained MCUs.
- **Advantage 2 (1 mark):** Enables seamless integration with the ROS graph — publishers, subscribers, TF, and parameters work identically to a PC node.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a ROS Publisher and a ROS Subscriber. Write the Arduino sketch lines that demonstrate each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Publisher | Subscriber |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Sends messages out | Receives messages in |
| Registration call | `nh.advertise(pub)` | `nh.subscribe(sub)` |
| Data action | Calls `pub.publish(&msg)` | Callback function executes |
| Example object | `ros::Publisher pub("topic", &msg)` | `ros::Subscriber<T> sub("topic", &cb)` |

**Sketch lines (publisher):**
```cpp
ros::Publisher pub("my_topic", &msg);
nh.advertise(pub);
```
**Sketch lines (subscriber):**
```cpp
void cb(const std_msgs::Int32& m) { /* ... */ }
ros::Subscriber<std_msgs::Int32> sub("my_topic", cb);
nh.subscribe(sub);
```

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Hardware Sensor Publisher + LED Subscriber**

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to interface an **LM35 temperature sensor** with an Arduino Uno and publish its readings to the ROS topic `/temperature` using the `rosserial` protocol. Include the wiring diagram and the complete Arduino sketch. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

1. **Wiring (2 marks):** LM35 `+Vs` → 5 V, `GND` → GND, `OUT` → A0. USB cable to host PC.
2. **Conversion formula (2 marks):**
   - Voltage: $V = \dfrac{A_{read}}{1023} \times 5$ V.
   - Temperature: $T(^\circ\text{C}) = V \times 100$.
3. **Sketch (3 marks):**
```cpp
#include <ros.h>
#include <std_msgs/Float32.h>
ros::NodeHandle nh;
std_msgs::Float32 temp_msg;
ros::Publisher temp_pub("/temperature", &temp_msg);

void setup() {
  nh.initNode();
  nh.advertise(temp_pub);
}
void loop() {
  int raw = analogRead(A0);
  temp_msg.data = (raw * 5.0 / 1023.0) * 100.0;
  temp_pub.publish(&temp_msg);
  nh.spinOnce();
  delay(100);
}
```

**(b)** Write a complete ROS Python subscriber node that subscribes to `/temperature`, logs an **overheating warning** to the terminal if the temperature exceeds **45 °C**, and simultaneously publishes an integer `1` to the topic `/fan_cmd`. Include the host-side launch commands. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

1. **Subscriber script `temp_alarm.py` (4 marks):**
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import rospy
from std_msgs.msg import Float32, Int32

def callback(data):
    if data.data > 45.0:
        rospy.logwarn("OVERHEAT ALERT: %.2f C", data.data)
        fan_pub.publish(1)
    else:
        fan_pub.publish(0)

rospy.init_node("temp_alarm_node")
rospy.Subscriber("/temperature", Float32, callback)
fan_pub = rospy.Publisher("/fan_cmd", Int32, queue_size=10)
rospy.spin()
```

2. **Arduino-side `/fan_cmd` subscriber (2 marks):** Arduino receives `Int32` and switches a relay on Pin 8 if value is `1`.
3. **Launch sequence (1 mark):**
```bash
roscore
rosrun rosserial_python serial_node.py /dev/ttyUSB0 _baud:=57600
rosrun <pkg> temp_alarm.py
rostopic echo /fan_cmd
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — PWM Motor Control via Subscriber**

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Describe the **rosserial packet frame format** in detail. What is the purpose of the SYNC byte (0xFF) and the checksum? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

1. **Frame structure (4 marks):**
   - SYNC byte: 1 byte (0xFF) — packet delimiter.
   - Version: 1 byte (0x0F).
   - Length: 2 bytes (little-endian) — body length.
   - Checksum: 1 byte — `255 − (sum of body bytes % 256)`.
   - Topic ID: 2 bytes.
   - Body: variable.
   - Optional CRC-16: 2 bytes.

2. **SYNC byte purpose (1.5 marks):** Allows the receiver to **re-synchronise** to the start of a packet if bytes are dropped due to noise or USB glitches. Without it, byte-level desynchronisation would corrupt every subsequent field.

3. **Checksum purpose (1.5 marks):** Lightweight single-byte integrity check — the receiver rejects packets whose computed checksum does not match, preventing **corrupted commands** from being delivered to actuators.

**(b)** Write the complete Arduino sketch to **subscribe** to a topic `/motor_pwm` carrying `std_msgs/Int32` and output a PWM signal on **Pin 9** whose duty cycle equals the received value divided by 255. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

```cpp
#include <ros.h>
#include <std_msgs/Int32.h>

ros::NodeHandle nh;
const int PWM_PIN = 9;

void pwm_cb(const std_msgs::Int32& msg) {
  int duty = constrain(msg.data, 0, 255);
  analogWrite(PWM_PIN, duty);  // PWM duty cycle
}

ros::Subscriber<std_msgs::Int32> sub("/motor_pwm", &pwm_cb);

void setup() {
  pinMode(PWM_PIN, OUTPUT);
  nh.initNode();
  nh.subscribe(sub);
}

void loop() {
  nh.spinOnce();
  delay(10);
}
```

**Duty-cycle relation (3 marks):**
$$D = \frac{duty}{255}, \quad \omega_{motor} \approx k \cdot D$$

**Host-side test command (1 mark):**
```bash
rostopic pub --once /motor_pwm std_msgs/Int32 128
```

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **rosserial** = the bridge that turns an Arduino into a **first-class ROS node**.
- 🔑 Always **regenerate `ros_lib`** after `source /opt/ros/<distro>/setup.bash` if you installed new message packages.
- 🔑 The default baud rate is **57600 bps**; both `Serial.begin()` and `_baud:=` must agree.
- 🔑 Use `nh.spinOnce()` in `loop()`, NEVER `ros::spin()` (will hang the MCU).
- 🔑 The SYNC byte is **0xFF**; it is the heartbeat of every rosserial packet.
- 🔑 `std_msgs/Float32`, `std_msgs/Int32`, `std_msgs/String`, `sensor_msgs/Range` are the most-used message types in KTU lab experiments.
- 🔑 The serial node typically needs **/dev/ttyUSB0** or **/dev/ttyACM0** with read-write permission; use `sudo chmod 666 /dev/ttyUSB0` if needed.
- 🔑 Validate with `rostopic list`, `rostopic echo`, and `rqt_graph` — these are the **canonical KTU viva questions**.
- 🔑 Handshake takes **~5 s** — do not panic if no topics appear immediately.
- 🔑 The maximum realistic publish frequency over rosserial is **~50–100 Hz** for typical payloads.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Obstacle avoidance using a differentially driven mobile robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL607 - ROS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Obstacle avoidance using a differentially driven mobile robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Differentially Driven Mobile Robot (DDMR):** A non-holonomic wheeled mobile robot (WMR) that uses **two independently actuated wheels** mounted on a common axle, with their speed difference producing both translational and rotational motion. The configuration is typically expressed in the planar pose vector $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta]^{T} \in \mathbb{R}^{2} \times S^{1}$, where $(x, y)$ is the robot's position in the world frame and $\theta$ is the heading angle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Obstacle Avoidance:** A reactive (or deliberative-reactive hybrid) navigation behavior in which the robot continuously senses its surroundings using range sensors (e.g., **LiDAR**, **ultrasonic**, or **depth cameras**), computes a safe control command $(v, \omega)$ at each time step, and modifies its trajectory to prevent collision with detected obstacles while still progressing toward a goal.

> [!NOTE]
> In the **KTU ROS (PCBRL607)** syllabus, this experiment is typically implemented on a **TurtleBot3 Burger** (or simulated in **Gazebo**) using the **ROS 2 Humble/Iron** framework, leveraging the `differential_drive_controller`, `laser_scan` messages (`/scan`), and the `move_base` action server (or a hand-written Braitenberg / VFH node).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blindfolded Driver with One Eye"

Imagine a person driving a wheelchair **blindfolded**, but with a single laser pointer fixed to the front that can tell the distance of whatever it hits. That person:

1. Knows only how fast each of the two hand-rims is spinning (wheel encoders → **odometry**).
2. Gets a single line of distances pointing forward (LiDAR → `/scan` topic).
3. Sends commands back: *"spin left wheel X rpm, right wheel Y rpm"* (velocity command → `/cmd_vel`).

When the laser sees a wall straight ahead, the driver instinctively spins one wheel faster than the other to turn away. This is **exactly** what a DDMR does in an obstacle-avoidance loop: *sense → decide → act*, at typically **10–20 Hz** for indoor robots.

> [!TIP]
> The key insight: a DDMR **cannot** move sideways (it is *non-holonomic*). So obstacle avoidance must always be solved through **turning**, not sliding. The robot "carves" curved arcs in the plane whose center is the **Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)**.

---

## 1.3 Why Differentially Driven Robots are the KTU Default

- **Mechanical simplicity** — only two motors, no steering linkage.
- **Zero-radius turning** — can spin in place by driving wheels in opposite directions.
- **Excellent educational value** — the kinematics are tractable, making them the **canonical platform** in ROS tutorials, Gazebo simulations, and TurtleBot3 hardware kits adopted across IITs, NITs, and KTU-affiliated colleges.
- **Tight ROS integration** — the `ros2_control` framework has a built-in `diff_drive_controller` that natively publishes `odometry` and accepts `Twist` commands.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Test-bed Parameters (TurtleBot3 Burger):**
> - Wheel radius $r = \mathbf{0.033}$ **m**
> - Wheel separation $L = \mathbf{0.160}$ **m**
> - Max linear velocity $v_{max} = \mathbf{0.22}$ **m/s**
> - Max angular velocity $\omega_{max} = \mathbf{2.75}$ **rad/s**
> - LiDAR: 360° scanning range, $0.12$ **m** minimum range, $3.5$ **m** maximum range.

---

## 1.4 What "Obstacle Avoidance" Means at Three Levels

| Level | Name | What it Uses | Typical Algorithm |
|:----:|:----|:-------------|:------------------|
| **Reactive (local)** | Local Planner | Only current sensor scan | Braitenberg, VFH, DWA |
| **Deliberative-Reactive** | Global + Local | Map + scan | A\* + DWA (ROS `move_base`) |
| **Learning-based** | End-to-End Policy | Sensor → action pairs | Deep RL (PPO, SAC) |

For **KTU Module Experiment 2**, the assessment is almost always at **Level 1 (Reactive)** — students implement a node that subscribes to `/scan`, computes $(v, \omega)$, and publishes to `/cmd_vel`.

---

## 1.5 The Closed-Loop Control Picture (at a Glance)

A simple textual view of the loop is:

**Goal Pose → Planner → Twist Reference → Controller → Wheel Voltages → Plant (DDMR) → Encoders → Odometry → (back to controller)**, with the laser scan injected **as a disturbance** that overrides or perturbs the reference command to avoid collisions.

This single image is what the rest of these notes will unfold mathematically.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Differential Drive Kinematics — The "Why and How"

### 2.1.1 Mechanical Setup

A DDMR has **two identical drive wheels** of radius $r$, separated by a distance $L$ (the *wheelbase*). Often a **caster wheel** is added at the front or back for static balance. The two drive wheels have independent angular velocities $\omega_R$ and $\omega_L$ (or equivalently, linear velocities at the contact patch $v_R = r\omega_R$ and $v_L = r\omega_L$).

### 2.1.2 Forward Kinematics (Wheel Speeds → Robot Motion)

The robot's **forward velocity** is the average of the two wheel linear speeds:

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$$

The robot's **angular velocity** arises from the *difference* of the two wheel speeds divided by the wheelbase:

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** If both wheels spin at the same speed ($v_R = v_L$), $\omega = 0$ and the robot goes straight. If the wheels spin in opposite directions with equal magnitude, $v = 0$ and the robot rotates about its own axis (in-place turn). Any other combination produces a circular arc.

### 2.1.3 Inverse Kinematics (Desired Motion → Wheel Speeds)

Solving the two equations above for $v_R$ and $v_L$:

$$v_R = v + \frac{L\,\omega}{2}$$

$$v_L = v - \frac{L\,\omega}{2}$$

This is the formula the ROS `diff_drive_controller` uses every control cycle: given a desired Twist $(v, \omega)$ from the planner, it computes the required wheel speeds and sends them to the motor drivers.

### 2.1.4 Pose Update (Euler Integration of the Unicycle Model)

Treating the DDMR as a **unicycle** for kinematic purposes, the rate of change of the pose vector $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta]^{T}$ is:

$$\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v\cos\theta \\ v\sin\theta \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \dfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\[4pt] \dfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \dfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\[4pt] \dfrac{r}{L} & -\dfrac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix}$$

Discrete integration (used in odometry at a sample time $\Delta t$):

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos\theta_k \,\Delta t$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \sin\theta_k \,\Delta t$$

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \,\Delta t$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked *"What is the velocity Jacobian of a DDMR?"*, the $3 \times 2$ matrix above (mapping $[\omega_R, \omega_L]^{T} \to [\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}]^{T}$) is the **exact** expected answer.

---

## 2.2 The Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)

When $\omega \neq 0$, the robot travels along a circular arc whose center is at perpendicular distance $R$ from the midpoint of the axle:

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L(v_R + v_L)}{2(v_R - v_L)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for obstacle avoidance:** When the robot decides to turn away from an obstacle, it is effectively choosing an $R$. A larger $R$ = gentle curve (less evasive); a smaller $R$ = sharper turn (more evasive). The Vector Field Histogram (VFH) algorithm in Section 2.4 uses histograms of "candidate $R$ values" to pick one that is collision-free.

---

## 2.3 Sensor Model — The Laser Scan

A 2D LiDAR returns an array of $N$ range readings $d_i$ at angles $\phi_i$ relative to the robot's forward axis. The measurement model in polar form:

$$d_i = d_i^{true} + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2}) \quad \text{with} \quad \sigma \approx 0.01 \text{ m (typical LiDAR noise)}$$

A reading is considered a **detected obstacle** if $d_i < d_{safe}$, where $d_{safe}$ is the *safety bubble radius* (often 0.3–0.5 m). The angular sector occupied by the obstacle is then $\phi_i \pm \Delta\phi$, where $\Delta\phi$ is the LiDAR angular resolution (e.g., $1°$ for the LDS-01 on TurtleBot3).

---

## 2.4 Reactive Obstacle-Avoidance Algorithms (the three KTU favorites)

### 2.4.1 Braitenberg Vehicle ("Fear" Behaviour)

For each LiDAR beam $i$, compute a "fear" weight $w_i$ that is high when $d_i$ is small. The simplest linear form:

$$\omega_{cmd} = k_\omega \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \sin\phi_i \quad,\quad v_{cmd} = v_{max} \left(1 - \beta \min_i d_i\right)$$

where $k_\omega$ and $\beta$ are tunable gains. The robot slows down and turns away from the closest obstacle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Connection:** The Braitenberg node is the **easiest** to code in a 3-hour lab, which is why it is the typical Module Experiment 2 deliverable.

### 2.4.2 Artificial Potential Field (APF)

Model the goal as an **attractive** potential $U_{att} = \tfrac{1}{2}k_{att} \rho_{goal}^{2}$ and each obstacle as a **repulsive** potential:

$$U_{rep,i} = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}k_{rep}\left(\dfrac{1}{d_i} - \dfrac{1}{d_{safe}}\right)^{2} & d_i \le d_{safe} \\ 0 & d_i > d_{safe} \end{cases}$$

The total force $\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U_{tot}$ gives a desired velocity vector, from which $v$ and $\omega$ are extracted:

$$v = \vert \mathbf{F}_{xy} \vert \quad,\quad \omega = K \cdot \text{atan2}(F_y, F_x) - \theta_{current}$$

> [!WARNING]
> APF suffers from **local minima** — the robot can get stuck between two obstacles. KTU exams often ask: *"What is a limitation of the APF method?"* The answer is **local minima trap**.

### 2.4.3 Vector Field Histogram (VFH)

The VFH method (Borenstein & Koren, 1991) is the **industry-grade** reactive planner used in many real robots:

1. Build a **polar histogram** of obstacle density, with $k$ angular sectors.
2. Compute a **threshold** $H_{high}$ — sectors above it are blocked.
3. Among the **candidate valleys** (contiguous safe sectors), pick the one closest to the goal direction.
4. Smooth the heading using a low-pass filter and command $(v, \omega)$ accordingly.

| Step | Computation |
|:----|:------------|
| 1. Histogram magnitude | $h_{k} = \sum_{i \in S_k} \left( d_{safe} - d_i \right)^{2}$ |
| 2. Binary mask | $h_{k}^{b} = 1$ if $h_{k} > H_{high}$, else $0$ |
| 3. Steering direction | $\theta_{steer} = \arg\min_{\phi \in \text{valleys}} \vert \phi - \phi_{goal}\vert$ |
| 4. Output | $\omega = K_\omega (\theta_{steer} - \theta_{current})$ clipped to $[\pm\omega_{max}]$ |

---

## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — at least 4 of these formulas appear in every KTU ESE.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:------|:------|
| 1 | Forward linear velocity | $v = \tfrac{1}{2}(v_R + v_L)$ | m/s | Average of wheel speeds |
| 2 | Forward angular velocity | $\omega = \tfrac{1}{2L}(v_R - v_L) \cdot 2 = \tfrac{v_R - v_L}{L}$ | rad/s | Difference over wheelbase |
| 3 | Inverse: right wheel | $v_R = v + \tfrac{L\omega}{2}$ | m/s | ROS `diff_drive_controller` |
| 4 | Inverse: left wheel | $v_L = v - \tfrac{L\omega}{2}$ | m/s | ROS `diff_drive_controller` |
| 5 | Wheel angular velocity | $\omega_{wheel} = v / r$ | rad/s | $r$ = wheel radius |
| 6 | Turning radius (ICC) | $R = \dfrac{v}{\omega} = \dfrac{L(v_R + v_L)}{2(v_R - v_L)}$ | m | Infinity when $\omega = 0$ |
| 7 | Unicycle rate equation | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta,\ \dot{y} = v\sin\theta,\ \dot{\theta} = \omega$ | m/s, rad/s | Pose dynamics |
| 8 | Odometry update (Euler) | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k\cos\theta_k\,\Delta t$ etc. | m, rad | Discrete integration |
| 9 | Velocity Jacobian $J$ | $\begin{bmatrix} r/2 & r/2 \\ 0 & 0 \\ r/L & -r/L \end{bmatrix}$ (with $\theta$–coupled top rows) | – | Maps wheel speeds to $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ |
| 10 | Braitenberg turn rate | $\omega = k\sum_i w_i\sin\phi_i$ | rad/s | $w_i$ increases as $d_i \downarrow$ |
| 11 | APF attractive force | $\mathbf{F}_{att} = -k_{att}\,\boldsymbol{\rho}_{goal}$ | N | Linear form |
| 12 | APF repulsive force (1 obs) | $\mathbf{F}_{rep} = k_{rep}\left(\tfrac{1}{d} - \tfrac{1}{d_0}\right)\tfrac{1}{d^2}\,\hat{\mathbf{n}}$ | N | $d_0$ = influence distance |
| 13 | VFH histogram magnitude | $h_k = \sum_{i \in S_k}(d_{safe} - d_i)^{2}$ | m$^2$ | Per angular sector $S_k$ |
| 14 | Tracked path length | $s = \sum_k v_k\,\Delta t$ | m | Odometer |

> [!CAUTION]
> **PITFALL** — when writing absolute values inside a table cell, use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid`, never the bare pipe `\vert x \vert` typed as `|x|` (breaks markdown table). Same rule applies to **all** exam answers.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|:------|:------------|
| **Warehouse logistics** | Amazon Kiva, GreyOrange Butler — DDMRs shuttle shelves. |
| **Domestic robotics** | iRobot Roomba, Xiaomi Mi Robot Vacuum — DDMRs with reactive bumper / LiDAR avoidance. |
| **Healthcare** | Aethon TUG — autonomous hospital supply delivery. |
| **Agriculture** | Small DDMRs for crop scouting and selective spraying. |
| **Research & Education** | TurtleBot3 (the KTU default) — teaching ROS, SLAM, and navigation. |

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Trend (2024–2026):** Modern DDMRs increasingly use **NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano** for on-device VFH/DWA, with **ROS 2 Humble Iron** and **Nav2** stack. KTU 2024 scheme questions may ask to compare **ROS 1 vs ROS 2** in this context.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Forward Kinematics from First Principles

### Step 1 — Define the wheel contact-point velocities

Each drive wheel touches the ground at a single point. Because of the **no-slip** (rolling without slipping) condition, the velocity of the contact point along the wheel's forward axis equals the wheel's tangential velocity:

$$v_{contact,R} = r\,\omega_R = v_R \quad \text{(right wheel)}$$

$$v_{contact,L} = r\,\omega_L = v_L \quad \text{(left wheel)}$$

### Step 2 — Rigid-body kinematics of the axle

The DDMR chassis is a **rigid body** in planar motion. Any rigid-body planar motion can be described as a translation with velocity $v$ plus a rotation with angular velocity $\omega$ about a point called the **Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)**. For the chassis, the velocity at the midpoint of the axle is exactly the forward velocity $v$ of the robot (because the axle midpoint lies on the line connecting the two wheel contact points).

The velocity at the **right contact point** is the sum of:
- the forward velocity of the axle midpoint, $v$, **plus**
- the rotational contribution $\omega \cdot (L/2)$ (the contact point is at distance $L/2$ from the axle midpoint).

But by the rolling constraint, this sum is constrained to be **purely along the wheel's forward axis**. Similarly for the left contact point. This gives the system:

$$v + \omega\,\frac{L}{2} = v_R$$

$$v - \omega\,\frac{L}{2} = v_L$$

### Step 3 — Solve the 2×2 system

Adding the two equations:

$$2v = v_R + v_L \quad\Longrightarrow\quad v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} \quad \blacksquare$$

Subtracting the second from the first:

$$2\omega\,\frac{L}{2} = v_R - v_L \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** This derivation uses only the rolling-without-slip constraint plus rigid-body kinematics — no further assumptions. It is therefore the *fundamental* relationship that KTU examiners expect in derivations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Velocity Jacobian

We want the map $\boldsymbol{\nu}_{wheel} = [\omega_R, \omega_L]^{T} \longrightarrow \dot{\mathbf{q}} = [\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}]^{T}$.

From the unicycle model, the body-frame velocity is $[\dot{x}_b, \dot{y}_b, \dot{\theta}]^{T} = [v, 0, \omega]^{T}$ (no sideways motion). The world-frame velocity comes from a rotation by $\theta$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v\cos\theta \\ v\sin\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

Substituting $v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)/2$ and $\omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/L$:

$$\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \tfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\ \tfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \tfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\ \tfrac{r}{L} & -\tfrac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix} \quad \blacksquare$$

This is the **3×2 velocity Jacobian** $J(\theta)$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — A KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem

> **Problem:** A TurtleBot3 Burger has $L = 0.160$ m, $r = 0.033$ m. The right wheel is commanded at $\omega_R = 6.0$ rad/s and the left at $\omega_L = 4.0$ rad/s. The robot is currently at pose $(x, y, \theta) = (1.0\text{ m}, 2.0\text{ m}, 30°)$. Compute (a) the forward and angular velocities, (b) the turning radius $R$, and (c) the new pose after $\Delta t = 0.1$ s.

### Part (a) — Compute $v$ and $\omega$

Step 1: Convert wheel angular velocities to linear speeds.

$$v_R = r\,\omega_R = 0.033 \times 6.0 = 0.198 \;\text{m/s}$$

$$v_L = r\,\omega_L = 0.033 \times 4.0 = 0.132 \;\text{m/s}$$

Step 2: Apply forward kinematics.

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{0.198 + 0.132}{2} = \frac{0.330}{2} = 0.165 \;\text{m/s}$$

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{0.198 - 0.132}{0.160} = \frac{0.066}{0.160} = 0.4125 \;\text{rad/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign check:** $\omega > 0$ means a **counter-clockwise** turn (robot's left wheel slower → it veers left in world frame). Consistent with $v_L < v_R$. ✓

### Part (b) — Compute the turning radius $R$

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.165}{0.4125} = 0.400 \;\text{m}$$

> [!TIP]
> The robot is tracing a circular arc of radius **40 cm** — about 2.5× its own wheelbase. This is a *gentle* left turn.

### Part (c) — Compute the new pose after $\Delta t = 0.1$ s

Step 1: Convert $\theta$ to radians. $\theta_0 = 30° = \pi/6 \approx 0.5236$ rad.

Step 2: Apply Euler integration (the simplest accurate-enough method for $\Delta t = 0.1$ s).

$$x_1 = x_0 + v\cos\theta_0\,\Delta t = 1.0 + 0.165 \times \cos(30°) \times 0.1$$

$$x_1 = 1.0 + 0.165 \times 0.8660 \times 0.1 = 1.0 + 0.01429 = 1.0143 \;\text{m}$$

$$y_1 = y_0 + v\sin\theta_0\,\Delta t = 2.0 + 0.165 \times \sin(30°) \times 0.1$$

$$y_1 = 2.0 + 0.165 \times 0.5 \times 0.1 = 2.0 + 0.00825 = 2.0083 \;\text{m}$$

$$\theta_1 = \theta_0 + \omega\,\Delta t = 0.5236 + 0.4125 \times 0.1 = 0.5236 + 0.04125 = 0.5649 \;\text{rad}$$

$$\theta_1 = 0.5649 \times \dfrac{180°}{\pi} \approx 32.36°$$

**Answer:** $(x_1, y_1, \theta_1) \approx (1.014\text{ m},\ 2.008\text{ m},\ 32.36°)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exact solution (for examiner reference):** Using the exact arc-based update (no Euler approximation):
> $$\Delta\theta = \omega\Delta t = 0.04125 \text{ rad}$$
> $$x_1 = x_0 + R(\sin\theta_1 - \sin\theta_0) = 1.0 + 0.400\,(\sin 32.36° - \sin 30°) \approx 1.0143 \text{ m}$$
> The Euler and exact answers agree to 4 decimal places — the **exam-friendly** Euler form is sufficient when $\Delta t \le 0.1$ s.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

| Property | Braitenberg | APF | VFH | DWA |
|:---------|:------------|:----|:----|:----|
| **Map required?** | No | No | No | No (uses scan) |
| **Goal-directed?** | Weak | Strong | Strong | Strong |
| **Handles local minima?** | Yes (empirically) | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Computational cost** | $O(N)$ | $O(M)$ obstacles | $O(N + k)$ | $O(N \cdot n_v n_\omega)$ |
| **Smooth trajectories** | No (jittery) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Tunable knobs** | Gain $k$ | $k_{att}, k_{rep}, d_0$ | $H_{high}, k$ | $v_{max}, \omega_{max}, \alpha, \beta$ |
| **KTU difficulty to code** | ⭐ Easy | ⭐⭐ Medium | ⭐⭐⭐ Hard | ⭐⭐⭐ Hard |
| **Real-world deployment** | Toys | Rare | Moderate | High (ROS `dwb_local_planner`) |

where $N$ = number of LiDAR beams, $M$ = number of obstacles, $k$ = number of histogram sectors, $n_v, n_\omega$ = velocity sample grid sizes for DWA.

---

## 3.5 Lyapunov-Style Stability Argument (for the curious examiner)

A common KTU question is: *"Is the Braitenberg avoidance stable?"* The answer uses the **distance-to-closest-obstacle** Lyapunov function:

$$V = \frac{1}{2}d_{min}^{2}$$

Its derivative along the closed-loop trajectory is:

$$\dot{V} = d_{min}\,\dot{d}_{min}$$

The Braitenberg law guarantees $\dot{d}_{min} \ge 0$ (distance is non-decreasing) **as long as** the robot moves forward and the closest obstacle is in the forward cone. So $V$ is non-increasing and the system is **stable in the sense of Lyapunov** within the forward cone, though **not asymptotically stable** (it does not return to an equilibrium).

> [!TIP]
> This argument is worth **2–3 bonus marks** in an ESE answer if quoted correctly.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Reactive Obstacle-Avoidance Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Goal Pose<br/>x_g y_g] --> B[High-Level Planner<br/>computes phi_goal]
    C[LiDAR Sensor<br/>360 deg scan] --> D[Pre-processing<br/>min filter, sectorize]
    D --> E[Algorithm Core<br/>Braitenberg or VFH]
    B --> E
    E --> F[Twist Command<br/>v omega]
    F --> G[diff_drive_controller<br/>ROS 2]
    G --> H[Left Motor PWM]
    G --> I[Right Motor PWM]
    H --> J[DDMR Plant<br/>L r]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Wheel Encoders]
    K --> L[Odometry Node<br/>x y theta]
    L --> M[TF Broadcaster<br/>odom to base_link]
    M --> E
    J --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **feedback loop closes** through the encoders (→ odometry → TF) and through the LiDAR (re-acquired scan). Both feedback paths are **mandatory** for a working reactive system.

---

## 4.2 ROS 2 Computational Graph (Topic and Node Wiring)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    scanPub["/scan publisher<br/>LDS-01 LiDAR"]
    odomPub["/odom publisher<br/>diff_drive_controller"]
    avoidNode["obstacle_avoidance<br/>custom node"]
    cmdPub["/cmd_vel publisher<br/>Twist messages"]
    diffCtrl["diff_drive_controller<br/>ros2_control"]
    motorL["Left Motor Driver"]
    motorR["Right Motor Driver"]
    tfBroadcaster["tf2 broadcaster<br/>odom to base_link"]

    scanPub -- LaserScan msg --> avoidNode
    avoidNode -- Twist msg --> cmdPub
    cmdPub -- Twist msg --> diffCtrl
    diffCtrl -- PWM left --> motorL
    diffCtrl -- PWM right --> motorR
    motorL -- encoder ticks --> diffCtrl
    motorR -- encoder ticks --> diffCtrl
    diffCtrl -- Odometry msg --> odomPub
    odomPub -- nav_msgs/Odometry --> tfBroadcaster
```

---

## 4.3 DDMR Kinematic Geometry (Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph world[World Frame XY]
        origin["World Origin (0,0)"]
        robotPos["Robot Pose (x,y,theta)"]
    end

    subgraph body[Robot Body Frame]
        wheelL["Left Wheel<br/>v_L = r w_L"]
        wheelR["Right Wheel<br/>v_R = r w_R"]
        wheelbase["Wheelbase L<br/>0.160 m"]
    end

    icc["ICC<br/>Instantaneous Center<br/>R = v/omega"]
    radius["Turning Radius R"]

    wheelL --- wheelbase
    wheelR --- wheelbase
    wheelL -. arcs around .-> icc
    wheelR -. arcs around .-> icc
    wheelbase --- radius
    radius --- icc
    robotPos --- wheelbase
```

---

## 4.4 Algorithm Decision Flow — What the Avoidance Node Does Each Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Timer tick 20 Hz]) --> readScan[Read /scan]
    readScan --> filter[Remove NaN and out-of-range]
    filter --> computeDmin[Compute d_min and bearing phi_obs]
    computeDmin --> checkSafe{d_min greater than d_safe?}
    checkSafe -- Yes --> goFwd[Publish v = v_max, omega = 0]
    checkSafe -- No --> computeTurn[Compute omega_cmd from Braitenberg or VFH]
    computeTurn --> saturate[Saturate v and omega to robot limits]
    saturate --> publish[Publish to /cmd_vel]
    goFwd --> publish
    publish --> start
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Diagram-Marking Heuristic:** Examiners award 2–4 marks for a clean control-loop diagram in a 14-mark question. The **minimum** they expect is a *Sense → Decide → Act* triangle. The block diagram above covers all marks.

---

## 4.5 State Machine — Behavioural Modes of the Avoidance Node

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Forward
    Forward --> TurnAway : d_min drops below d_safe
    TurnAway --> Forward : d_min exceeds d_safe + hysteresis
    TurnAway --> Stuck : angular vel saturates for over 3 s
    Stuck --> Reverse : reverse briefly 0.5 s
    Reverse --> TurnAway : continue avoidance
    Forward --> [*] : goal reached
```
