# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
>
> **Q1. Define a differentially driven mobile robot and state its configuration space representation.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A differentially driven mobile robot (DDMR) is a non-holonomic wheeled robot that uses two independently controlled coaxial drive wheels to achieve both translation and rotation. Its configuration is represented by the pose vector $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta]^{T}$, where $(x, y)$ is the position in the world frame and $\theta$ is the heading angle. The configuration space is $\mathbb{R}^{2} \times S^{1}$. The motion obeys the unicycle constraints $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$, with $v, \omega$ as control inputs. **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q2. Differentiate between the Braitenberg and Artificial Potential Field (APF) methods of obstacle avoidance.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | Aspect | Braitenberg | APF |
> |:-------|:------------|:----|
> | **Basis** | Direct sensor-to-actuator mapping | Gradient of a synthesized potential |
> | **Goal seeking** | Weak (needs augmentation) | Strong (attractive potential) |
> | **Computation** | $O(N)$ — simple weighted sum | $O(M)$ — needs gradient of $U_{tot}$ |
> | **Limitation** | Noisy, no global structure | **Local minima trap** |
> | **Map needed** | No | No (uses local distances) |
> **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module Experiment 2]**
>
> ## ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)
>
> **(a)** Derive the forward kinematics equations of a differentially driven mobile robot. Express the linear velocity $v$, angular velocity $\omega$, and the velocity Jacobian in terms of the wheel speeds. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A TurtleBot3 Burger is moving with $v_R = 0.20$ m/s and $v_L = 0.10$ m/s. Given $L = 0.160$ m, $r = 0.033$ m, and current pose $(1.0, 2.0, 45°)$, compute the new pose after $\Delta t = 0.1$ s using Euler integration. Comment on the radius of curvature. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the rolling-without-slip condition for each wheel. The contact point velocity equals the tangential speed:

$$v_{contact,R} = r\,\omega_R = v_R \qquad v_{contact,L} = r\,\omega_L = v_L$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Treat the chassis as a rigid body with planar motion — translation $v$ plus rotation $\omega$ about the ICC. The contact-point velocity is the sum of translational and rotational contributions, constrained to lie along the wheel axis:

$$v + \omega\frac{L}{2} = v_R \qquad v - \omega\frac{L}{2} = v_L$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Solve the system by addition and subtraction:

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} \qquad \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Convert to pose dynamics using the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$, and substitute $v_R = r\omega_R$, $v_L = r\omega_L$ to obtain the velocity Jacobian $J(\theta)$:

$$\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \tfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\ \tfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \tfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\ \tfrac{r}{L} & -\tfrac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix}}_{J(\theta)} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Insert the given wheel speeds into the forward-kinematic formulas:

$$v = \frac{0.20 + 0.10}{2} = 0.15 \;\text{m/s}$$

$$\omega = \frac{0.20 - 0.10}{0.160} = \frac{0.10}{0.160} = 0.625 \;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the turning radius (comment requirement):

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.15}{0.625} = 0.24 \;\text{m}$$

> **Comment (1 mark):** $R = 0.24$ m is only **1.5× the wheelbase**, indicating a *sharp* left turn (since $v_L < v_R$).

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Convert $\theta_0 = 45° = \pi/4 \approx 0.7854$ rad and apply Euler integration:

$$x_1 = 1.0 + 0.15\cos(45°)\times 0.1 = 1.0 + 0.15 \times 0.7071 \times 0.1 = 1.0106 \;\text{m}$$

$$y_1 = 2.0 + 0.15\sin(45°)\times 0.1 = 2.0 + 0.15 \times 0.7071 \times 0.1 = 2.0106 \;\text{m}$$

$$\theta_1 = 0.7854 + 0.625 \times 0.1 = 0.7854 + 0.0625 = 0.8479 \;\text{rad} \approx 48.58°$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Final answer — new pose:

$$\boxed{(x_1, y_1, \theta_1) \approx (1.011 \text{ m},\ 2.011 \text{ m},\ 48.6°)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — where students lose marks here:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$. The 2022 and 2023 ESE papers have repeatedly deducted **1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Sign confusion:** if $v_L > v_R$, the turn is to the **right** (negative $\omega$ in the right-hand convention). Always state the sign convention explicitly.
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** $\omega$ is in **rad/s**, not rpm. $1 \text{ rpm} = \pi/30 \text{ rad/s}$. KTU papers have set numerical questions where the wheel speeds are given in rpm — students who skip this conversion lose **2 marks**.
> 4. **Jacobian form:** the $3 \times 2$ Jacobian must include the $\theta$-dependent top rows; writing only the $2 \times 2$ version with $\dot{x}, \dot{y}$ alone is **incomplete** and is penalized.

---

> ## ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
>
> **(a)** Explain the Braitenberg "fear" algorithm for obstacle avoidance with a differential drive robot. State its governing equations, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the complete reactive obstacle-avoidance loop for a DDMR in ROS 2. Identify the topics published/subscribed by the custom node and the role of `diff_drive_controller`. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Braitenberg Algorithm (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** The Braitenberg vehicle uses a direct stimulus–response mapping where each LiDAR beam $i$ at angle $\phi_i$ with range $d_i$ contributes a weighted signal to the wheel speeds:

$$v_R = v_{max} - k \sum_{i} w_i(\phi_i, d_i) \quad,\quad v_L = v_{max} - k \sum_{i} w_i'(\phi_i, d_i)$$

In the "fear" form, beams on the **right** reduce the right wheel speed (and vice versa), so the robot steers **away** from obstacles.

**Governing equations (3 marks):**

$$\omega_{cmd} = k_\omega \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \sin\phi_i \quad \text{where}\quad w_i = \frac{1}{d_i^{2}}$$

$$v_{cmd} = v_{max}\left(1 - \beta\,\min_i d_i\right)$$

with $d_i < d_{max}$ and $\sin\phi_i > 0$ meaning the obstacle is on the **left**, causing a **right** turn.

**Advantages (1 mark):** extremely simple; no map; $O(N)$ computation; works in unknown environments.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **No goal-seeking** — robot will circle an obstacle indefinitely if it is the goal side.
2. **Jittery / oscillatory** in narrow corridors (no hysteresis by default).
3. **No local-minimum recovery** (unlike APF with random walks or VFH with sectoring).

#### Part (b) — Block Diagram and ROS 2 Roles (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** The reactive loop is:

```
[Goal] → [Planner: phi_goal] ──┐
                                ↓
[LiDAR] → [/scan] → [Avoidance Node] → [/cmd_vel] → [diff_drive_controller] → [Motors] → [DDMR]
                                                              ↑
                                                  [Encoders] → [Odometry] → [/odom] → [TF]
```

**Topics (2 marks):**
- The custom `obstacle_avoidance` node **subscribes** to `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` on `/scan`.
- It **publishes** `geometry_msgs/Twist` on `/cmd_vel`.

**Role of `diff_drive_controller` (2 marks):** It is a `ros2_control` controller that:
1. Subscribes to `/cmd_vel`.
2. Computes per-wheel speed targets using inverse kinematics: $v_R = v + L\omega/2$, $v_L = v - L\omega/2$.
3. Sends PWM commands to the motor drivers.
4. Reads encoder feedback and publishes `nav_msgs/Odometry` on `/odom`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> 1. **Braitenberg derivation marks:** the weights $w_i$ must be **explicitly shown** as a function of $d_i$ and $\phi_i$. A vague "the robot turns away" answer gets **0 / 3 marks**.
> 2. **Topic types matter:** write `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` and `geometry_msgs/Twist` in full — partial credit only.
> 3. **Block diagram must show feedback.** A purely feed-forward arrow chain (no encoder/odometry loop) is **incomplete** and is penalized 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Don't confuse ROS 1 with ROS 2.** KTU 2024 scheme uses **ROS 2 Humble/Iron**. Use `ros2 topic echo`, not `rostopic echo`. Examiners explicitly check this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TOPIC RECAP — DDMR Obstacle Avoidance**

- [x] **DDMR** = two independently driven coaxial wheels + caster. Configuration $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta]^T$. Non-holonomic (cannot move sideways).
- [x] **Forward kinematics:** $v = (v_R + v_L)/2$, $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/L$.
- [x] **Inverse kinematics:** $v_R = v + L\omega/2$, $v_L = v - L\omega/2$.
- [x] **Velocity Jacobian** is a $3 \times 2$ matrix $J(\theta)$ mapping $[\omega_R, \omega_L]^T \to [\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}]^T$.
- [x] **Turning radius (ICC):** $R = v/\omega$. Zero-radius turn is possible (in-place).
- [x] **Pose update (Euler):** $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k\cos\theta_k\Delta t$ (and similarly for $y, \theta$).
- [x] **Three canonical reactive algorithms** in the KTU syllabus:
  - **Braitenberg** — direct sensor-to-motor weights, $O(N)$, no map.
  - **APF** — attractive + repulsive potentials, has local-minimum problem.
  - **VFH** — polar histogram, sector-based candidate valleys.
- [x] **ROS 2 topics:** `/scan` (LaserScan), `/cmd_vel` (Twist), `/odom` (Odometry), `/tf`.
- [x] **`diff_drive_controller`** is a `ros2_control` controller that performs inverse kinematics and publishes odometry.
- [x] **LiDAR range** for TurtleBot3: 0.12 m to 3.5 m, 360° scan, ~1° resolution.
- [x] **Safety bubble** radius $d_{safe} \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ m is typical.
- [x] **Lyapunov stability** argument uses $V = d_{min}^{2}/2$ with $\dot{V} \ge 0$ in the forward cone.
- [x] **Sign convention:** $v_L < v_R \Rightarrow \omega > 0$ ⇒ **left** turn (counter-clockwise).
- [x] **Unit conversion:** $1 \text{ rpm} = \pi/30 \text{ rad/s}$ for wheel speeds.
- [x] **Angle conversion:** always use **radians** in $\cos$, $\sin$, $\omega t$ calculations.
- [x] **Common KTU trap:** writing the Jacobian as $2 \times 2$ instead of $3 \times 2$ — loses 1 mark.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Formula Flash-Card (one-glance revision):**
> $$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}\quad,\quad \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}\quad,\quad R = \frac{v}{\omega}$$
> $$v_R = v + \frac{L\omega}{2}\quad,\quad v_L = v - \frac{L\omega}{2}$$
> $$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta,\ \dot{y} = v\sin\theta,\ \dot{\theta} = \omega$$

**End of Notes — Module Experiment 2: Obstacle Avoidance using a Differentially Driven Mobile Robot.**
